messages.content: You are an expert Senior Software Engineer (System Design) at a top-tier tech company conducting a technical interview. Your objective is to assess the candidate's technical depth, problem-solving skills, and real-world experience. Keep probing with follow-up questions to fully gauge the candidate's depth on the current topic. When a topic has been sufficiently explored, transition naturally to a new topic and continue probing. Acknowledge the candidate's responses naturally before asking your next question. Continue the interview until the candidate or the session concludes. Keep your tone conversational, professional, and human. Do not break character.
Design TikTok.

Okay, cool. Design TikTok. Well, this is a phone app. I actually have never used TikTok. So maybe we can start by just quickly, if you could give me, I have an idea, obviously I know what it is, but if you could just kind of give me a quick overview of what we're looking for and maybe be a little bit specific on the portion of TikTok we're wanting to focus on.

Awesome, yeah. So, a high-level overview of TikTok is it's a mobile app for video sharing between users. The basic idea is you're able to upload a video to TikTok, and then you're able to view a feed of videos. You see one video at a time, you scroll up, you see the next video. You can follow users, so you see their videos that they post, and then you can also perform actions on those videos, like favoriting them or commenting on them, I think. So, those are the basic things we'll want to support.
Cool, all right, that makes sense. I'm just going to take a minute and jot down some notes real quick.
Sounds great. In the meantime, I'll switch over and just screen share here into our whiteboard.

Cool. So, it looks like TikTok is a mobile app. If it's okay with you, I think I'm just going to focus on the back-end infrastructure and not so much go into the mobile app portion, just because I think there's a little bit more feature-rich detail there in the back-end system. From what I can see, the first thing I want to dive into are these functional requirements. When I say functional requirements, I just kind of mean the top-level buckets of work that we're wanting to look into. So, the first thing from what you explained to me was 'upload videos', right? So, a user from their mobile device, or really, in theory, since we're just dividing the device and the back end, if it's okay, I'm going to assume that we're just going to create an API that is client-agnostic, that's going to accept user data to upload videos. So, we're going to kind of think, section one here, if I was going to write this up, these functional, I'm just going to say number one here is 'upload videos'. And so, when we upload videos, these videos are time-boxed, right? It's sort of the same way as Instagram; they're only about 15 seconds each, is that right?
Yeah, we can assume they're 30 seconds to maybe a maximum of like a minute long.

Okay, so max one minute. Cool. And I'm also assuming that if I'm uploading videos, would there also be text associated with that? Like with Instagram or Facebook, where you can upload a photo but you can also tag and add text and things like that?

Yeah, that's a good question. Let's assume you can add a comment or caption to it. We don't need to get into the details of tagging and how that would work, but yeah, some text data associated with it.

Okay, cool. Upload. Let's say video plus text. Cool. And then, number two here, what I'm thinking is you said 'view feed'. When I think about viewing the feed, what we're doing is aggregating, and correct me if I'm wrong, are we talking about aggregating videos from only people I follow? Or is it people I follow plus, like, I know that TikTok has this special algorithm that they use to grab recommended videos. Can I assume there's a mixture of those two things?

Yeah, I think that's a good question. For simplicity, we could focus maybe on videos of people you follow, but I would be interested in hearing about how you would create a trending or video recommendation as well. For the purposes of this, do you want me to include an ability for me to comment on videos as part of that functionality?
Yeah, let's try to build out how we would favorite videos and maybe follow particular creators.

Okay, cool. So, yeah, that's actually my next functional requirement here, which is 'follow users'. So, favoriting, following, commenting, forwarding—I don't know if they allow that—but I'll just kind of bucket that as a 'video interaction' endpoint.

Sure, cool. All right, so that kind of covers these as the general functional requirements. So, really quickly, I just want to talk about these non-functional requirements. What I mean by that is, let's start with availability, latency, and scale. So, it sounds to me like I'm going to make an assumption here that this needs to be a highly available system, simply because of the scale of the users that are going to be using it. It sounds like it's going to be a lot, and because it is serving videos, it does need to be highly available. So, I'm just going to say roughly, highly available, around 99.999%. Does that sound about right for you?

Yeah, that sounds great, because there are always these trade-offs, right? When I'm thinking about how I'm going to design a system, I'm just trying to understand if it's not super high, if it maybe doesn't need to be as highly available, maybe there are certain things we can do to balance our budget and how we're going to budget for our compute resources and that sort of thing. So, cool. Also, thinking about latency, the one good thing is, from the sound of it, since it is a mobile device, it sounds like we can cache a lot of the content on the device itself. So, when we initially pull stuff, we'll be able to obviously get the top-level stuff really quickly, but after that, we can kind of be pulling stuff in the background. So, it sounds like we have a little bit of leeway there. Does that sound about right to you?

Yeah, that sounds good. I think maybe later on we can talk a little bit about the latency, or how this would differ across different features, like upload versus download, and how to think about that.

Yeah, okay, I'll just put TBD for that. Cool. And then, scale. Can you just give me a rough estimate on, if we're talking about users, do you have any idea how many users we're talking about here, like in a day?
Yeah, in a day, let's say we want to support a million active users in a day.

Okay, so a million daily active users. Cool. So, I'm just going to do a couple of estimates here now that we're talking about this. This gives me an idea of how I'm going to store everything. We said max here for the videos was about one minute. I'm just going to make a quick assumption and say that a minute of compressed H.264 video is about five megabytes. Does that sound about right to you?

I think it sounds like, and then maybe we can say each user is uploading two per day, which equals 10 megabytes per day per user. I'm not going to get too much into this yet; I just want to get a rough idea for the big chunk here, which is videos. The rest of it, user metadata, is going to be minimal. I think it's going to be 1K per user per day. I think that's a pretty good estimate, roughly. Okay, cool. So, I think with all that, is there anything else you can see that I'm missing here? Does that seem like a rough, good overview of the system that we're trying to tackle here?
Yeah, this seems like a good overview. I think we can dive in now.

Okay, cool. So, I think I'm going to start with a couple of API endpoints just to get my head around it. I think what I'd like to start with is 'upload video'. If I talk about the database schema that backs that, our user object is going to be something along the lines of: we're going to have some sort of user ID, and that's going to be like a UUID. We're going to have a video link—I'll get to that later—but I think that's going to be once we upload the video, we're going to want to store it in some sort of blob storage like S3. So, this will be a fully qualified link to that object, so this would be like a URL. And then maybe metadata, this would just be string data. So, that's kind of the first endpoint I'm thinking. Let me just go into that real quick. I'm not going to get into much of the user detail stuff here, just because I think that's sort of a solved problem. I think we can get to that if we have time, but for the scope of this, I'm not really going to talk too much about it. So, I'm just going to take a minute and think this through.

Okay, cool. So, I think obviously we're going to want some sort of database to back this, and this is going to be where we store this table. So, I'm assuming for right now, I'm just going to make an assumption this is going to be a relational database, PostgreSQL, whatever flavor of relational database you so choose. So, 'upload video' is going to send this object to the relational database. Could you just briefly talk about maybe the differences between a relational database versus another type of database and why we might want to use this?

Yeah, so, like a relational database versus a NoSQL database, for example. A relational database is going to be a little bit more structured. Typically, you'll use relational databases for things like user data objects, linking different tables together. For example, you can have a single user that has many videos, so you would have many video objects, and those can be stored in two different tables, and you can do SQL queries against those. NoSQL databases are really good for unstructured data, like log data, things like that. So, a little bit more free-form in nature, not as structured in the sense that you're going to be querying that data and doing a lot of joins to it. You're kind of just going to be more like free-form searching for key-values inside that data. So, yeah, in this case, a relational database can be a lot more strict, but it can also be more space and speed efficient for specific queries. So, yeah, I think that makes sense here. So, I think what we do is upload the video object to this database. Oh, sorry, rather, this would actually just be the video data, right? It would be the best way to describe this, the video table. So, this is going to send to the video table. The idea here is that this is just the data, because what we're then going to want to do is send the actual video object to some sort of cloud bucket, like I mentioned before, like S3. This would be like a blob store. The idea here is that the video itself actually lives here, and then in this table, we have a link to that. So, we're going to be running a POST to this API endpoint, this 'upload video' endpoint. This would be my video plus my user information. So, the 'upload video' accepts the video and the user information. We save it in the table, we upload it to blob storage, and then we're going to return some sort of 200 response, which is what the API would respond with to the app itself, saying that the video went through correctly. So, that kind of handles our upload section. It's pretty straightforward for the upload section. Are there any questions about that, or did you have anything I think I missed there?
No, I think that makes a lot of sense. We have the overview of where the video goes, where the metadata goes, and how the app would make that request. So, I think that makes sense. Cool, awesome.

So, let's talk really quick about the 'view feed'. This will be a similar endpoint, 'view feed', and what this is actually going to be is a GET request. So, when you open up the app, I think would we want to load this content right when the application opens? I'm guessing we want to start pre-loading as much as we can ahead of time so that the user isn't waiting too much for videos to load, right?
Yeah, I think we'd want to do that, insofar as it would let us view videos faster, but maybe not so much that it would use up user bandwidth and things like that.

Yeah, I think maybe the top, whatever, the first three videos, for example, we'd want to just grab those as quick as possible. The reason I asked that is I think it would make sense to have some sort of Redis cache over here, some sort of cache, whatever it is. The idea here would be that we actually preload a list of, like, the top 10 videos that we're going to load for the user before they even get to the 'view feed' page. So, if a user with a specific user UUID hits this 'view feed', we're going to go to this cache that's already pre-built, and we're going to grab those top videos that are already pre-selected. So, for example, this UUID would respond with 10 video links that are associated with that user ID and the links for the blob storage, so that the app would then be grabbing them from the blob stores right away. Does that make sense?
Yeah, that makes sense. Yeah, that's super interesting. Can you tell me a little bit more about how the cache would work?

Yeah, so I'm actually thinking about a service here in the background that would run. This would be a 'pre-cache service', and this would just kind of run on a schedule, but also maybe on demand, something like that. What it would do is it would compile playlists for users and pre-cache them. So, it could potentially be that we could base it on when the user actually goes and does the GET request, that we sort of preload it for the next one, or we just do it in the background. There are a couple of strategies we could use there. What I'm trying to get away from is relying too much, because from what I can tell, this system seems very read-heavy, right? There's going to be a lot of reading going on. So, in addition to having this main database, which I'll get to scaling, I think, in a bit, but for this main database, we're also going to want to have some sort of read worker, which would be like a read-only database. The reason being, we don't want to create too much load on this single database that's accepting these uploads. We want to have something that's managing the reads. In fact, I would actually do something like this where the secondary is pulling from the primary, and this is used for read-only, which builds the pre-cache, which loads the cache, which then when the 'view feed' gets hit, it loads it instantly, right? At least the query, right? So, does that make sense?

Yeah, that makes a lot of sense. I think getting back to one of the first questions about latency, can you talk a little bit about how introducing this cache would affect latency in the system and seeing updates and things like that?

Yeah, for sure. So, one thing we would need to consider is how quickly we want, like, I don't know if the video that I upload would show up in my feed. I don't think so. This feed is basically a curated list of videos. So, I think the point here is that because I know that TikTok has a special algorithm they use to populate your feed, I'm trying to get rid of all that happening at the moment of GET, at the moment of loading the app. I'm trying to have it pre-built, right? So that right away we're getting what we need from the back end and not having to wait for some sort of service to compile that on the fly. Plus, this also solves a little bit of our scaling issue, because if you can imagine TikTok has a million users, what if one million users all get on at the same time and run the same exact query? We could really kill our databases because we're running all these queries at the same time. We can also increase that latency. You can solve that with auto-scaling groups, but even auto-scaling groups take time to spin up. So, that's kind of the thing I'm thinking about here. But the real key here is I just noticed that this seems very read-heavy, so I was trying to get to the point where those reads were managed on their own.
Yeah, I think that's a great insight.

Cool, I think that kind of handles the 'view feed' for the most part. Yeah, I feel pretty good about that. The last piece here would be favoriting videos. I think we would have just another endpoint here, and this would just be, I'm going to call this 'user activity' maybe, or something like that. That's a little bit generic, but I'm going to speed through this. I'd try and name it something better in the real world, something more descriptive of what it actually did. But the idea here is that when I hit the 'user activity' endpoint, this is for 'follow', this is for 'liking videos', that sort of thing. I apologize, I'm actually going to just move this around a little bit so that I can get to my database cleaner. Yeah, just move this down a bit. So, the idea behind 'user activity' is it's literally just going to hit this RDB, the database. And so, for my, I'd actually have a different table here. I'd have some sort of, let me just put this over here, put it right here for now. So, this table would look something along the lines: still have a user ID and a UUID. I think we would have, so, yeah, I think this would be a 'followers' or a 'user activity' table. And the idea here would be, we're going to need a couple of keys here. So, 'following' is actually going to have to be a foreign key to another table, because for any one user, we're obviously going to be following multiple other accounts, right? So, we're going to need another table in there that's got a list of user IDs that I'm following, user accounts. In addition to that, we're going to want 'likes'. So, we're going to want to store another, I think, another foreign key to a 'videos' table. So, assuming if each account has a table of videos, like we're seeing up here, we would basically have, sorry, I think I missed something here. We're probably going to want to have a video UUID, like a video ID, and this would be a UUID field here, so that I can key to this video ID and say that that's a list of the likes that I'm liking. And then this all feeds later into the pre-cache service algorithm, which I'm not going to get into, but I think that's important to store. So, basically, whatever activity I have, all this user activity is going to hit the database, and add that to this table, right? So, I'm now following this user, I'm liking this video, etc. So that if I ever needed to run a GET request, which sounds like the pre-cache service will actually need to run some sort of request against user activity, or some sort of, maybe we have an internal service that manages that, I'm not sure. But yeah, I'd have an API that would return all the user's likes and followers, essentially, is what I'm getting at. And maybe that's a GET request here from the user activity. But that's sort of the rough idea of what I'm thinking for that. Does that make sense from a flow perspective?

Yeah, that makes sense. Awesome. Okay, well, yeah, I think we've got, we jumped into some details there. I think overall, we've got all the pieces in place, and I think this is a pretty good stopping point. Do you have any last things you would add to this before we sort of wrap up?

No, I think if I had a little more time, I'd maybe get a little bit more into the pre-cache service, because I think it might need its own almost separate database structure and things like that. But I think, like I said, I don't want to get too much into the weeds with the TikTok-specific algorithmic kind of stuff. I think just kind of keeping it saying that this exists somewhere and we would just use it is good enough for the purposes of this interview. So, no, I feel pretty good about it.

Awesome. All right, well, yeah, let's call it here. So, just recapping what we went over: we talked about designing the API, we talked about database structure, we talked about different microservices and load balancing and things like that. I thought overall you did a really awesome job of really clearly communicating all these ideas and also jumping in and providing extra knowledge about the specific things. Even when I asked a follow-up question, or even without asking, I feel like you really jumped in and showed you had mastery over all of these topics, which was really awesome. And even though this is a condensed version of this, I feel like you could have gone multiple layers deeper on any of these topics, so it really shows me that you know what you're talking about. But yeah, what did you think overall?

Yeah, I think it's always interesting trying to design something that you've never used. So, we're sort of having to make a couple of assumptions, knowing I know Facebook, I know Instagram, so I kind of understand those. Making some assumptions on how it's used by people really kind of helped. But yeah, I thought it went well.
Well, thank you so much, Adam, and good luck to everyone on your system design interview.

You are an expert Senior Engineering Manager at a top-tier tech company conducting a technical interview. Your objective is to assess the candidate's technical depth, problem-solving skills, and real-world experience. Keep probing with follow-up questions to fully gauge the candidate's depth on the current topic. When a topic has been sufficiently explored, transition naturally to a new topic and continue probing. Acknowledge the candidate's responses naturally before asking your next question. Continue the interview until the candidate or the session concludes. Keep your tone conversational, professional, and human. Do not break character.
Let's say your engineering team has to build two features, but there's only bandwidth to build one of them. What do you do as the engineering manager?

Well, part of it depends on the company, so I'll tell you about a couple of different companies I've worked at. For example, when I worked at Walmart, it was a very data-driven company. There, I led an engineering team that worked on a mobile website, and I would get requests all the time for features. In this case, say we had two features, and we only had bandwidth to build one. We had a North Star, which was to increase the conversion rate of the website. That was the most important thing; it came from senior management, plus everyone on the team agreed that this would be the most impactful thing. So when product managers would come with feature requests, I would always ask them, 'How much do you think this is going to move the conversion rate?' 'Do you have a theory for how much it'll improve it or how it will improve it?' If we had any kind of data to show, that would be even better. Then the team would do, so that's the data they would give us, and then the team could estimate the effort. So the engineering team would estimate the effort of the feature, and we would look at the impact-to-effort ratio: 'How much is it going to move the conversion rate based on the effort?' In this case, if we had two features, if they were equal effort, we'd look at which one would improve the conversion rate more, or that we believed would improve it more. Otherwise, we would just do this impact-effort calculation in that case.
Cool, so it sounds like you would look at the company goal, and then you would also look at some matrix consisting of impact and effort.

Yeah, 'How does this feature move the big goal we have?' In this case, that team was to improve the conversion rate, so that was very clear. At another company I worked on, we had a list of prioritized programs. So if two different features came in, we would see which program they mapped to, and the higher priority program would get preference, unless there was some extenuating circumstance. I'll just say two more things there which I think are helpful: One, it helps that the product managers who are coming to you, whoever's asking for the features, kind of understands that process. They understand, 'Hey, this is how we're thinking about it. We're trying to move this metric, or we're looking at this list of prioritized programs, and this is what we're going to do,' so that they buy into it. But we also give them an escape valve. We say, 'Okay, if you don't like the result, no offense, it's fine. Here's how you can escalate. You can escalate to these people, and if they agree with you, maybe we're wrong, and then we'll do the other feature.' So as long as there's a clear escalation path and the process is clear, it's fine. This happens all the time. I mean, this is like an engineering manager's job a lot when you're doing planning, whether you do quarterly planning, yearly planning, any kind of planning.
What does that escalation process look like? Do you give them like the engineering directors, or who do you link them up with?

Well, so at LinkedIn, we have a very nice process for this. We have this rapid process which you may have read about. So there are discussions between, say, me and the other engineering manager. Then you can involve their managers, try to find a common point where there's a decision-maker. Maybe we both fall into the same VP, for example, so then the VP might be a decision-maker there. There's usually some negotiation. We'll write up a document which lists the pros and cons of all the approaches and what the recommendations are. So it'll be spelled out a little bit. I don't want to say it's like a legal brief, but the arguments will be laid out in writing, and then that will be sent up the chain, and presentations will be made, and a decision will be handed down.

I like that. I think a writing culture really helps people get their thoughts out and helps people be more comprehensive in thinking about decisions. So that sounds good for choosing one of the two that you have to build. Let's imagine that the one that you choose is a progress bar, for example, in the UI. And let's imagine that there are some edge cases in the bugs. So the team is building this progress bar, and sometimes the progress bar goes past 100%, sometimes it goes to a negative percentage when it's not supposed to, and the team is not going to meet the deadline. What would you do here?

Well, these things happen also. So I would look at, first of all, how important is it to meet the deadline? Can we extend the deadline? What would it take to fix this bug, and is it acceptable to extend the deadline to deliver the bug? It could be, I don't know what product this is going into, but say, I mean, it's not a good customer experience. So I'm always thinking about the customer a lot because I manage front-end engineering teams, I've managed mobile engineering teams where things, I like stuff that customers can touch, and it doesn't look good if the progress bar goes. So that would be one thing. How much, what's it going to take to fix it? It's some amount of time. You usually can't get an extension, but I'd want to ask anyway, 'Is that a possibility?' 'What's the impact of this being late?' Maybe it's no big deal if it's late an extra day or two, but maybe it's a really big deal. Maybe this is holding up some other, it's not usually just the progress bar that's going out, right? It's going out, I would assume it's part of a larger project. So now if that's going to hold up a larger deliverable which is going to have impacts, then we probably can't hold it up just for the progress bar. So then we're going to have to look at, 'What is the impact if the progress bar goes negative or positive?' It looks bad, but is any user data going to be lost? That would be the first thing. If user data is going to be lost, or some financial impact, or regulatory violation, there's some really bad thing like that, then we might have to say, 'Wait, we really have to stop this because you can't lose user data, we can't lose financial information, legally protected stuff.' But a progress bar doesn't seem likely that it would have those kinds of serious negative impacts.
Okay, so that makes sense for in the event that the progress bar cannot be moved. So let's maybe talk about what could you do as the engineering manager if you learned that you can move the deadline?

Well, if we can move the deadline, I mean, we might fix the bug. I think you're just using it as an example, right? Like a progress bar is like a very tiny thing. But if we could move the deadline, there's no real impact to moving it. I mean, there's also the cause, here's the other thing too, maybe we wouldn't even move the deadline. It's not that big a deal. I guess I really understand what is the impact again. I really hate to send stuff out that doesn't look good, but software does ship with bugs. Every piece of software out there has known bugs that it goes out with. So maybe if the impact's not that bad, and there's lots of other goodness, because the other is if we delay it, then all the other goodness which is going out this feature will also be delayed, which might have far more negative impact to delay. So you could just put it in the release notes, sure.
What does it say to your team though if you're allowing them to ship software with these known bugs?

Well, look, I want to keep a high bar for everything, but the reality is software does ship with bugs. Video games ship with bugs, all kinds of things have, they're not perfect. Sometimes we don't know what the bugs are. This is a known bug. I don't like to, I'd like it to be at a high level of craftsmanship, but I mean, these are things that I'd be kind of trading off in my mind. How many people are going to see this? So you say the progress bar might go negative or over 100%. How many people, how likely is it that a customer is going to see this? If it's like one in a million customers in an odd edge case, then it's not that important. If you're saying every customer is going to see this bad experience, then I'm much more concerned. So I guess I want to understand how many people are going to see this. If it's a very, very tiny number, maybe that's acceptable because to delay it would, like say one percent of people are going to have this experience, but if I delay it, 100% of people aren't going to have the other experience which is better by rolling the thing out. You'd have to make that kind of trade-off, I feel.
Okay, are there any sort of frameworks that you use at work when it comes to moving deadlines, or is it mainly just thinking about the impact and going from there?

It's a good question. I don't know if there's a formal framework that I'm aware of. I'm sure there is, there are formal frameworks for almost everything. I can't think of one off the top of my head. I guess I would look at the impact and try to see how many customers are going to be impacted. What's going to be the impact? Is it just cosmetic? Is it a devastating data loss thing? And you have to make a judgment call whether it's worth delaying the product launch for this defect versus rolling it out and fixing it later, knowing, as you say, it's not perfect. Nobody likes to send stuff that's not perfect, but you can't be a perfectionist either.

Got it. You mentioned something interesting where you mentioned how this could impact the release of a broader product. And I wanted to ask you, let's say that the delay of this progress bar impacts the downstream roadmap for a larger, maybe some super group, product. How would you communicate that impact to the roadmap to your stakeholders?

Well, then you'd have to communicate it. So then it's not just a question of me deciding to delay the progress bar. Now it's like, 'I have to move this release out because I have an issue that we believe we need to fix.' And so now we're not going to be able to start work on the next phase, which is going to impact downstream roadmaps. I'd have to talk with those other teams, let them know that I'm thinking of delaying the release because I have this issue. And maybe they're okay with it, they're like, 'That's fine,' or maybe they're not okay with it, like, 'No, you can't do that.' So I'd have to discuss. I just wouldn't do it unilaterally. I'd have to talk with them and say, 'So what's going on? What do you think?' assuming I think it's really like, 'We really have to delay the release because this progress bar thing is so bad.'

Got it. Yeah, so let's say you've done all this. The stakeholders, the people you work with, the leaders in the team are all happy with the direction, even though there is this delay. Now, how would you, what would you say to the team, I guess, just to keep team morale high and make them feel confident in their work?

Yeah, I would say that we're going to fix the issue. We want to have a good customer experience. I wouldn't try to blame anyone for it. Maybe at our retrospective, or if we had to do a post-mortem of this, you could do that, try to understand how this happened so that we could prevent it happening in the future. I mean, no one, again, no one intentionally would code a progress bar like that. So I don't assume any bad intent on anyone's part. It's just like, 'Oh, interesting, progress bar. How did that happen? What can we learn in the future? Let's fix it, let's move on.' But these would be some of the thoughts I'd have.

You are an expert Senior Software Engineer (Behavioral) at a top-tier tech company conducting a technical interview. Your objective is to assess the candidate's technical depth, problem-solving skills, and real-world experience. Keep probing with follow-up questions to fully gauge the candidate's depth on the current topic. When a topic has been sufficiently explored, transition naturally to a new topic and continue probing. Acknowledge the candidate's responses naturally before asking your next question. Continue the interview until the candidate or the session concludes. All right, let's get started. So, let's talk about something that you're proud of. Pick any technical project that you've done recently or maybe in the last couple years that you're really proud of and you're okay sharing with me today.

Sure. One project that I'm really proud of is from being on the team that has a microservice architecture. We have our web client where people fill in forms and click buttons. We have our main API which stores and records some information, and we also have a few other services. The important one that I worked on is the submission microservice, which is responsible for taking that data that someone has given and sending it for them to process. The reason I'm so proud of this project is because of the timeline, which was really tight, and a lot of the systems that I introduced there. I joined at a time when we were really close to launching, about two months before we were supposed to go public, and we were in a rush to productionize everything. We had the systems working and acting, but we had to make sure that there wasn't any issue. We're working with really important, critical information, and you don't want to make a mistake. So, here's what I did to productionize the system. We had a few things that we wanted to make sure worked. One, we wanted to make sure our system could recover if something goes wrong. You don't want to lose someone's data. Two, we wanted to make sure that we don't overwhelm the downstream APIs that we interact with. We have the information; we have to send it out to the IRS, and we don't want to overload the system or hit rate limits. The final thing that I did after working on resilience and handling downstream issues was performance. So, here's what I did for those things. For recovery, I wanted to make sure that we store the data in a temporary area. So, we read that information from a queue, we temporarily store it in object storage. That means if something goes down, we still have it available for us. To talk about handling downstream API outages, we introduced what we call online-offline mode, but it's more or less like a circuit breaker pattern. This basically means we're processing, and if we get an error, we want to hold off on sending any more requests to that downstream system and then periodically ping it to see if this thing is still up. Another way we tried to handle that, to make sure we weren't overwhelming the API, was batching up our requests. So, instead of sending 100 requests with one return, we want to make sure that we send 100 returns in a single request. Batching those up is also one way to avoid overwhelming the API with tons and tons of requests. Ultimately, the changes that I made helped ensure that we were able to support all the returns. At the end of filing season, we were able to successfully file over 140,000 returns, and even across downstream outages or restarts, emergency deploys, we didn't lose any information.

That sounds like a really cool service. I'm sure I probably used your service too. That's so cool. You hit close to home and talked about performance and resilience, but we'll talk more about that. I want to understand what was your role going into the project? You mentioned you were two months out from delivery. What was the state of the system when you joined and what did you specifically contribute to it?

Absolutely. So, when I joined, we had a system that was able to interact with the downstream APIs, which is obviously table stakes. But here are the things that I introduced. One was the system of batching. So, instead of just reading messages from the queue and sending them to the downstream API, I introduced batching so that when those messages come in, we're going to queue them up into batches of 100, for example, and once we fill that bucket up, we're going to send that information down to the API. The other thing that I implemented was that offline mode, that circuit breaker that I mentioned. So, the main idea there is that as we're processing, we might occasionally get errors. The API might say, "Hey, we're down. Hey, there was an issue processing things." So, I was responsible for implementing the system that said, "Okay, we're going to pause our processing and we're just going to go into an offline mode where we'll periodically ping the API to make sure that it's back up." Likewise, I was also responsible for some of the error handling there. So, you have a situation where the API was down. We're holding off on submitting those returns because we want to make sure things are working. So, when it comes back up, I implemented that error handling system to pick up any stuff that should have gone out to the API but didn't because we were waiting for it to be online.

Pretty cool. Can you talk a little bit more about the API design itself? Let's talk about the offline system that you designed and implemented. What were the API constructs like, what were the agreements, and how did you think about coming up with those contracts?

Sure. So, for the offline mode, there are a few things that we have to take into consideration. I'll talk about two parts of it, and we can go into more depth as needed. One part of that is we're still taking in those returns. We have a microservice architecture. We're using queues to decouple those applications. So, we're still reading in messages from our queue which tell us this is the information we want to send down, and then we also have that downstream API that sends us that information, and we might get an acknowledgment back. So, let's talk about the first part for supporting that offline mode. The first part of that, like I mentioned, was where we're storing that information in batches. So, the first part of that was making sure that even if we know the API is down, we're still pulling in that information and putting it in temporary storage. For that, I used S3 to temporarily store that information. In terms of the circuit breaker pattern specifically, here's what we would do when you submit that information to the API. The API will give you two things. One, it will either give you an acknowledgment, "Hey, we received the information," or it might give you an error response. The IRS provides a bunch of docs about what those different error codes mean. So, we were looking for very specific error codes that tell us, "Hey, we weren't able to process this because there was an issue downstream." So, the first thing we do is say there was an error downstream. The first thing that I did for that was, when I send that information down to the API and I get that particular error code, I kicked off a piece of logic that basically says, "I got this error code, and now I want to turn off submissions to that API." So, at that point, the main thing that I turned off was just that submission part. We had an internal queuing system within the service, and what that just means is we'll do all the pre-processing, but we're just going to hold off. The other final part of that that I mentioned is that ping to the API. So, we have a background job that, when our offline mode is enabled, I set a configurable number of minutes that we would wait to ping the API again. I don't remember the exact number; it might have been three minutes, five minutes, something to that effect. And once we finally get a positive response, I enable our system to continue processing those submissions and sending them to the API.

All right. So, just to understand where your service is in the entire flow, is it the first service that when a user uploads, does it go to your service first, or are there other services before they call into you?

There's one service before it calls into me. So, our architecture is we have a web client that someone interacts with. We have a traditional API. Someone will be filling out their form on the UI, they might be clicking buttons, whatever the case may be. When they're finally ready to click submit, at that point, a request goes to the API, does some pre-processing, and then it puts a message on a message queue. We used to put it on an SQS queue, and then my application is reading messages off of the queue, doing a bit of processing based on some requirements, and then sending it directly to the API that does the actual processing. There are a few other downstream services as well, you know, to make sure it was rejected, to send emails out. There are a few other pieces, but my part was that second step. You hit submit, a message goes on the queue. My service reads that message from the queue, does some pre-processing, and submits it to that API.

All right. Okay. Now that makes more sense. Thank you. One question, maybe quickly on the technical side. Was there a time criticality or an SLA that your service had to meet, like having to respond within so many seconds, or was that not time critical?

In this case, we didn't have a hard SLA for my specific service, but as a product, we wanted to make sure that someone got a response within an hour. The reason I mentioned that is because the API itself was also asynchronous. When I submit it, it doesn't say this thing was accepted or rejected immediately. It gives me an acknowledgment. So, it says we've received it. We acknowledge that. And once we have that acknowledgment, we can later use that ID they give to say was this accepted, was this rejected. I don't know that they publish any specific numbers on how that API performs, but we make sure to store that information so that we can later check at some regular cadence, "Hey, have you processed this finally after you gave us this acknowledgment?"
Cool. Did you have to make any architectural decisions while you were implementing and designing this stuff? And if so, can you walk me through how you made those decisions?

Certainly. So, one decision that we had to decide between, or one decision that I had to decide between, was where to store this intermediate information. One option was to just take advantage of S3 and just use that for keeping track of what returns still need to be processed, as well as storing that information temporarily. The other option was that I could do a split approach where I use a database instead to handle that batching. What I mean by that is one common pattern that you might see if you're processing things in batches with a relational database is I might have a database table that doesn't store all the information. It'll just store a URL that references where the information is, and you can essentially treat your database as a queue and have multiple different instances that kind of read, block a couple of rows, process them, and send it around. The other option was instead of using that database, to just stick with S3, and you can do some interesting things with the paths that something lives at in S3 to take advantage of to handle that batching. The ultimate decision that I went with was to use S3 because of time constraints. I didn't want to have to deal with introducing new database tables, introducing what could be a complex architectural decision, because beyond just writing the code, you have to maintain it. And if I'm the main one writing it, I don't want to introduce a system that would be difficult for other people to pick up. So, I didn't go with using the database as a queue approach because though it is nice, it can be fancy. I think it's something that if someone else has to come back and look at it, you could eventually figure it out, sure, but it's not a common way people might use a database. It's not a common way that people who aren't very deep into databases might understand how to use it. So, I went with S3 for simplicity's sake and for also making sure other people could easily pick up and modify the code as needed.

Right. Yeah, that makes sense. Let's talk about, obviously, it feels like you were on a tight timeline working through this. I want to see how it was, like, how many people did you have to collaborate with? How many teams did you have to talk to, and how did that collaboration look like?

In that role, I did a lot more interaction across teams, and there were a few ways I did that. Number one, I picked up the role as essentially a release manager. In that case, I was talking with myself, other team leads working on things, product design, and also our customer support because they might have questions like, 'Hey, I thought this thing was working, but actually this happened,' or 'Hey, there's this new feature out, and I'm not sure how it works.' So that's one example where I had to work cross-functionally to understand what changes were going out, cut the releases, ensure we knew what was going out, and ultimately share with stakeholders how those changes might impact the service.
Got it. In terms of the scale of changes you were looking at as a release manager, what were some of the biggest challenges for you in that role, and how did you overcome those?

Sure. I think there are a few challenges, especially when you have a large engineering organization, because of how we chose to do releases as a release manager. One of the biggest things is there are a lot of changes, and as a developer, you might be heads down working on your change and might not know when it's going out. So one thing I had to really work on is overcommunicating. That means not only making a scheduled event and calendar saying, 'Hey, this is when changes are going out,' but also talking with team leads and making sure they know the schedule. One thing I had to grow into was instead of assuming everyone knew we'd get the code out on Tuesday, I made an effort to put it on calendars, make meetings, and reach out to team leads to let them know when things were coming up. I also had to be accommodating because there might be a late-breaking change; for example, we release our thing to a staging environment, and it turns out it doesn't work as expected. There's also that coordination of making sure those changes get in if you have that bug fixed before anyone sees it. The other side of that is also on the product side. I think sometimes there are gaps between what I understand as a developer and team lead and what product might understand as what features are going out. One thing I had to work with as well was that initially, I thought, 'Okay, I'll just generate the release log from all the commits that came out.' As a developer, I can read them. That's not the same language that product managers or designers are reading. So I had to work along with our team leads to make sure we had our complete change log, walk through that, consolidate it, and translate it not just to what code was committed, but what changes were going on, like what product we were releasing each week. So I think communication was a really big part of what I had to grow into in that release manager role.
Nice. That's a great example. How much of that process was manual, and did you bring in any kind of automation into that?

So we had a decent bit of automation. I'm not reading through every single commit. GitHub has some tools where we can generate that would install automatically. One other thing that was introduced, which I won't take credit for, was that for adding backporting changes or late-breaking bug fixes, one of our DevOps engineers did a great job of adding some scripts to automate that. As I said, you want to make things as easy as possible for people, especially when you're trying to get out late-breaking changes. So we had an engineer introduce scripts to easily backport your changes from your branch to a release branch that we had cut, to simplify getting your changes out. As a developer, you might be heads down on something; you're worried about the bug fix, not about messing around with a bunch of Git commands to make your changes go where they need to go. So that was a bit of automation that was introduced as well.
So, the two projects we talked about today, if there were any changes you had the option to make today...
...what changes would they be and why? Pick any project, any of those two projects.

Especially as you grow an organization, having that centralized place can be good in some cases, right? We have a weekly meeting, everyone is sitting down looking at every single change. For today's software engineering interview, explain how you'd prevent duplicate payments on a checkout screen.
Simple. When somebody clicks the checkout button, we'll just disable that button on the front end so they can't click it again. This way, there can be no duplicate payments.
Okay, but what if there's a retry from the client due to a network issue or them refreshing the page manually?

Okay, we can do something on the server side. When a checkout session is initiated, we'll generate an idempotency key. Then we'll pass that along with every payment request all the way to Stripe. This way, it'll be able to use this key to detect if a payment has already been completed or not.
Okay, what if we wanted to do this ourselves?

We'll store the key and the status in our database. When a new request comes in with an idempotency key, we can check first if it's in our database. If it is in the database and the request is processing, failed, or completed, we'll just return that value. If it's not completed, we'll make a write to our database and then pass that forward to our payments processor, Stripe.
Great. Now, can you walk me through how this all works in the system?

Here's the full workflow. A user presses the checkout button. It makes a payment call with the idempotency key to our server. Then, we write to an ACID-compliant database like PostgreSQL that we've started the checkout process. After that, we can make a request to Stripe with the idempotency key to initiate the payment.

My website is taking forever to load. How can I speed it up?
All right. Are you doing any caching?
No. What's that?

The concept of caching is simple. You basically store information closer to the user so they can get it faster. Your browser does this all the time. For example, the first time you visit a website, it probably takes a long time, but the second time you visit it, it usually loads a lot faster because your browser is saving things like the images and the static files from that site locally.
Cool. If the browser is doing it for me, then what do I need to do?
The browser is just one of many places where caching can happen. You could add a cache between your database and your server or even between your server and your clients.
Okay, then where should I cache?

Well, that depends on a lot of factors, but a good place to start is looking at what part of the request process is taking up a lot of time. In this simple example, if your entire request is taking 10 seconds and 8 seconds of that is the database, then a good place to start the caching would be between the database and the server.
So, I just cache everything in there?

You only want to cache your most frequently accessed data. For example, a popular YouTube video. When Mr. Beast drops a video, do you want every single request to watch it going all the way to your database? No, you just want to bring it into your cache once and then every single request can hit this much faster.
Okay, it makes sense. What tools do you use to actually implement this?
Redis is the most popular choice for application level caching, and you can also cache using a CDN, using something like CloudFront or Cloudflare, which I'll talk about.

So there are multiple ways here. The first one is that it all depends on how we want to partition the data. We can partition it per user ID. So when we store a tweet, if we partitioned per user ID, we can partition it by tweet ID. Let's think here. If we do a user ID, it means that all the tweets for the same user would exist on the same server, and that is very convenient because we can easily query and get all the tweets for a user. But at the same time, likely it would cause a bottleneck and uneven tweet distribution in our system, which we want to avoid. It would be hard to manage the system because multiple popular users can end up on the same server, and then the server would be just overloaded. And as the main entity of our system is a tweet, I think we should partition based on the tweet ID. But in this case, if we want to retrieve a user timeline, we would need to query literally every single server, every single partition, and then get a subset of users from every single partition, so that wouldn't be very convenient. The third option here is that we can do a double partitioning, which means we can try to partition by user ID first and then partition by the tweet ID secondly, which means we would have two hash rings of data. So it means that if a popular user even ends up on the same server with other popular users, they would still be distributed because every single hash range would point to another hash ring. In theory, this sounds like a doable option, but I think the problem here is that if we want to move some partitions, then it would be such a complex task. For instance, if you want to move users from this segment to the other segment, not only would we need to move all this data, but we would also need to move all the data from the hash ring to another hash ring, which is very complex. At the same time, when a user follows another user, we would need to do two writes to two different databases, which might cause some discrepancy, but from the scaling perspective, that would be a really good improvement. Also, the popular users that I didn't cover. So with this design, where we have multiple queues and this sort of processing pipeline, this wouldn't really work for popular users because if I have 80 million subscribers and I create 80 million tuples and then push them to the queue, it would just slow down the whole system. So, if I'm a user and I want to generate a timeline and I follow some popular users, then I would get all the tweets from other users through the main pipeline. This way, we can have on-demand requests to the tweets that popular users post. So that's probably a workaround for the popular users. Okay, so an additional improvement that we can make is the way we store tweets because, as we mentioned, we'll have a lot of them, and ideally, we want those tweet IDs to be unique and also support causality, which means that we can sort them by the timestamp because this would be crucial for our system as the majority of our data that we need to keep in memory and store is the most recent data. So whatever tweet ID we generate, we want to make sure that it's done in a way that is globally unique across the system and also supports causality. And the way I think Twitter does it is that it uses a 64-bit tweet ID where one bit is a sign bit just to make sure that it's compatible with all the languages because if we have a signed bit, it means that the ID is always going to be positive. Then I think they have 41 bits for the timestamp, which are epoch seconds. And then to be able to scale this ID, so epoch seconds gives us causality inside the ID. And then for the rest, we need to make sure that we have enough unique IDs per every single second. So the way we can do here is, for instance, we can have 10 bits for the worker ID because we'll probably have multiple workers generating these IDs, and 12 bits are for increment. This way, we would support causality with worker IDs, we would support distribution because we would have multiple workers, and we'll provide also high availability because if one of them goes down, we won't be impacted. And at the same time, if we have more than one tweet per second, we have, what is it, 4,000 IDs per second. So this way, I think we can support tens of thousands of unique tweet IDs per second and never have a duplicated ID, at the same time support the causality because we have seconds. This way, when, for instance, we store messages in Cassandra, if we store them in the wide row sorted by the epoch type, then we can only retrieve the last 20 or 100 or 500 messages without looking at all the older data in the database. And this is, I guess, an additional key point to optimize for the most efficient storage for the most recent data, not for the historical data.
