A: this was a ground-up new product which became the central tool of choice for the company I was working in to complete all the financial close activity for that company. B: this was not only a ground-up new product. This was also a team that I developed — basically hired and grew as part of this team that I started. So I started a new team. I hired a lot of good engineers who went on to do very successful things for the product. I was also able to deprecate a lot of legacy systems. I was able to stand up this tool to become the single unified tool for performing financial flows automation. So, okay. So, it was a ground-up product.
So what would you say was your — the highlight of that project for you? What made you the most proud?

The fact that it was a big platform. There were four different modules in it and it was a huge product and at the same time it was very stable from its very go. We had only maybe 40 or 50 people who used the product at launch. It went on to have like more than 500 users within a year or two. And at the same time it was one of those rare products that never got any — you know what we call severe incidents or high priority tasks that kind of blocked users. It was extremely stable in terms of quality. There were — and that kind of led to a lot of adoption of the tool and we kept on adding a lot of big features and yet it always seemed to work scalably without going down or having any kind of product issues.
That sounds interesting. Tell me about a decision or a trade-off you had to make in that project.

I think one of the decisions was, once we started getting a lot of adoption, what I realized is one of the modules had a backend engine which we realized is not going to scale. Now we had an option to go back to the users and say we need to kind of rebuild the internals of that engine to kind of make sure it scales for all the quick adoption that's happening — from like a few hundred cards we would be going to thousands of cards in less than six months. So one of the decisions I took was we stopped a new feature that we were developing to re-prioritize rebuilding this engine. And we did it in a way where we did not want the users to come back and do all these long UATs. We created a service that would compare the before and after in a very easy way for engineers to kind of just work with it. And we kind of created reporting that would go out and show that the new code did exactly what the old code would have done except that it is now far more scalable. So with that what happened is we were able to scale for the year end because the year end close is almost always the most critical for a company. And we went live with this rewriting of the engine and it scaled and we did not have any other problem. Now we did not have a new feature which came later in Q1 but that feature was not — would not have soured the experience of year end close as such.

Okay, thank you. Let's go in another direction. Appreciate you sharing these stories. I think those are great — getting to know you a little better. Let's talk about technical debt. You know, as a product team, technical debt is always a trade-off. How do you — what are your views on technical debt generally speaking? And maybe even tell me about a time where you had to write down some technical debt — but I'm curious to hear your general thoughts about technical debt.

Sure. So I think I just gave an example where I think we kind of built a system that did not have technical debt, or which kind of did not ever lead to a situation with tech debt. So that's the highlight of my career in terms of building that kind of a product. But I would also say that that's because I had a lot of experience getting hurt with technical debt in the past. So there were a lot of lessons learned on, you know, what not to do, which I think I was successful in this new project. But going back to your question, Josh — so I had teams that I have kind of joined, or products that I have inherited, that had a lot of tech debt. And the things — or my observation is — we always tend to either talk too much into the future, that oh this is — if we need to kind of fix this we probably need to rebuild the whole thing, or we need to kind of have a two year engineering plan to kind of rewrite the product or something like that. But I can give some example where one of the teams that I was leading did have a lot of tech debt as such because it was written years ago when the team was much smaller, the product was much smaller, it wasn't processing the kind of billions of dollars that it was processing at that time. So there were a few tiny steps that I think I had my team take on a continuous basis. A: just improve the overall instrumentation in the code and start gathering metrics. So which means to say that, you know, do we even know what's working well and what's not? And if something is not working well, probably can we get more insights on a week-to-week basis. So one or two of the sprints I had my team purely work on adding more instrumentation and kind of generating the metrics that would go into our alerting and monitoring tool and start looking at this on a more constant per-week basis, because if I had to go to my leadership and talk about the weekly operational metrics we did not even have enough of that. So with that in mind, it was a steady progress made in terms of just collecting the metrics and reviewing them internally as a team every week when the on-call would kind of hand off the operations to the next engineer. But one of the things we started seeing is there were a lot of things — or a lot of misconceptions — we had about our own product. We used to have a website and we thought that's where everyone comes and creates these agreements. But what we realized is more than 70% of the agreements in our system are created by an upstream team calling our APIs. They never through the website. So editing an agreement is what was done mostly on our website, never much of creation. So we built all this kind of metrics continuously and it led to something much more impactful. It helped us to identify where even we should focus, what are the things that we should invest on, where if we need to kind of move say from batch-based system to event-based system that gives us much more scalability, which parts of which batch programs are really not scaling, which programs take how long. So as we kind of generated these metrics it kind of led to a very steady state about which things we could prioritize, that slowly we could rewrite, we could keep it in a shadow mode for a long enough time where it's just comparing the results and it's not trying to do anything in production immediately, or just using web labs or AB testing where we would slowly dial up these things, make sure it works, and then come back. And in about one and a half to two years I think we were able to rewrite a lot of the stuff without feeling the pain of big-bang launches. So interesting, yeah — technical debt is — I don't think there's a magic wand that will ever take away all the technical debt. No, it's always very step by step, very gradual, but if you keep doing it, it almost, you know, has this cascading impact where it would have created a lot of free bandwidth by fixing one small thing and then the cumulative effect is what I feel kind of lands us at a stage where we are now ready for much bigger things.

Yeah. I see it as similar to borrowing money as an investment in a business, right? You borrow to grow and then you have to pay it back. Yeah. You mentioned — it sounded like you mentioned a trade-off between batch and event processing. Was that a call that you made, and if so, why did you make that call? What was the what drove the decision?

So I wouldn't say I made the decision. Or I mean, I identified the need for that decision either. It was much more bottom-up where one of the engineers was actually able to find out a lot of the problems we were having. And engineers face the brunt of the operations that they are supporting, right. So which is where this was actually to the engineer's credit that they brought this up. They also worked on a technical design doc that they got reviewed through a lot of very senior engineers in the organization and they went ahead and implemented it and that actually was a pretty good success. It wasn't again immediate. I would say it took several months as we did this, but it was a multi-million dollar savings in revenue — or revenue that we otherwise were losing which we hadn't realized. And it was a recovery of all those millions of dollars of recovery by just changing something from batch-based to event-based. It was a way where, you know, it wasn't just the kind of methodology from batch to event. There were a lot of other dashboards and a lot of other metrics that we generated. It was a complete overhauling of how we process certain types of agreements, which kind of led us to all these discoveries about us losing so much of revenue.
It was more of a refactoring then.
It was more of a refactoring. Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Totally.

That's a — trying to — I'll put this in the question. How did you handle balancing, let's say, new features or whole new products with the refactoring — which was probably happening simultaneously? How did you handle that?

So I think once we were able to establish how much revenue we are potentially losing through a non-scalable system, it wasn't that difficult of a sell to just say that this needs refactoring. So we had a monetary value attached to refactoring this. So it wasn't — it didn't look like a pure engineering project. There was a lot of interest on our business teams as well. And okay so, in that sense, it kind of exceeded the expectations in terms of how much revenue we were otherwise losing — it was like almost double-digit million dollar revenue, it's not trivial. So the decision was an easyish one. Yeah, it was. Because I think, and again, the platform that we set with trying to generate a lot of metrics kind of enabled a lot of these kind of decisions where it was easy to sell that, hey, we are in a system — if we had gone cribbing to our business saying hey our systems don't scale, probably they wouldn't be as invested in it, like it was our job to probably develop good systems. But if the cost of not having scalable systems led to what kind of problems is what I think these metrics enabled.

Great, that's thank you for that. It's interesting. Let's take another turn — a little more general question but I think for an engineering manager this is important to understand. Tell me a little bit about your leadership style.

So my leadership style is a lot more — I would say — growth and mentoring focused. So I am very invested in my team's growth, mentoring, and empowering them. And I'm generally able to grow my teams well because I also like to have a good vision for the team and kind of set ambitious goals and you know try to drive the big bets, so to say. Now that doesn't mean that I don't care for the immediate goals or the short-term goals, but generally speaking if these immediate term goals or the short-term goals can become building blocks to the long-term goals, that's how I generally try to get the set — or structure my organizations around those kind of philosophies. I think I have been pretty successful in that in terms of growing a lot of people and also getting a lot of the big things achieved.
So — excuse me — balancing between short and long-term goals. How do you keep your team accountable to the goals that you set for them?

So in terms of accountability, I think each engineer has the kind of goals set for themselves. And then there's constant feedback that I use with them. If I see them doing something that they shouldn't, I would make sure that they get that feedback during the one-on-ones and other places. There's generally a lot of the reporting that different engineers have for the different projects they own. I kind of review that and make sure that the weekly status reports and other mechanisms that we have in play in terms of tracking projects — those are done. I also kind of am very passionate about operational excellence. I kind of always make sure that each engineer takes the responsibility of making sure that they have enough goals around operational excellence and they are kind of continuously improving our systems. They are not just doing the business projects and all that productivity — whether it's in terms of how many lines of code they shipped or how much code review and code review effectiveness they have had — those are the things I track, and you know if there is feedback needed I make sure that I don't wait for the end of the year to give them this feedback. I make sure they get it as and when things happen so that they have a chance to course correct.

Yeah, sounds good. That sounds good. What drew you to engineering management? From my own experience, some engineers never want to touch management and want to be ICs or independent contributors, and some move into the EM track. What brought you to the EM track?

I think I was a reluctant entry to engineering management. I would say it took me some time to like managing people. My first few — I would say at least months — was more about — because I was very good as an IC. I just thought that that would be a better way to just help more people and kind of impart that knowledge and skills that I had gained as an IC to be able to kind of spread to a much bigger team much more easily. That's how I started it. But then I also learned over a period of time that engineering management is not like just technical knowledge. There's a lot more to it. And some of those I learned over a period of time. I don't think I learned it on day one or I knew what I was getting into on day one. But I started enjoying it. There was a time when I also started managing managers. But the more important thing was always about — I still had that fundamental desire that I should grow people. And that's kind of always kept me much more motivated.

That's nice to hear. Yeah, it's a — I'm sure it was a learning process to become the engineering manager you are. So, this takes me I think to our last question. Looking to the future, what are you looking for in your next role?

So from the next role I think it's being able to solve complex and ambiguous problems and along the way, you know, mentor and empower my teams to solve these kinds of problems and lend that experience of people management and having solved a lot of complex ambiguous problems — just be able to use those skills and experience to grow more people. I would say.

You are an expert Senior Software Engineer (System Design) at a top-tier tech company conducting a technical interview. Your objective is to assess the candidate's technical depth, problem-solving skills, and real-world experience. Keep probing with follow-up questions to fully gauge the candidate's depth on the current topic. When a topic has been sufficiently explored, transition naturally to a new topic and continue probing. Acknowledge the candidate's responses naturally before asking your next question. Continue the interview until the candidate or the session concludes. Keep your tone conversational, professional, and human. Do not break character.
Jordan, the question I want to ask you today is: how would you design a stock trading app such as Robinhood?

All right, thanks for letting me get into it. Let's see if I can type at all. Okay, that seems to work. So, like you mentioned, we're designing a stock trading app. I'm going to use Robinhood since that's the one that I know about the most. I'm going to try and lay out ideally the requirements of something like this. The ones that I can think of are, of course, things like sending orders and keeping track of positions that a certain client or customer might actually have. Also, this is the one I want to focus on, so I'm going to put two asterisks here: showing the actual correct price of stock or options. The reason for that being that I think when it comes to actually sending orders and maintaining your customers' actual holdings, that's kind of trivial. You can do that pretty simply with a couple of databases. Maybe you'll have to shard them a little bit if you really do have a ton of users, but for the most part, all the exchanges have an API; you're basically just forwarding their orders there. I think in reality that gets a little bit more complex with payment for order flow and things like that, but we're not going to touch upon that too much ideally. So, my question for you, Tom, then would be: what are the type of capacity estimates we're looking at? Basically, how many users and how many stocks per user were you thinking?
Sure, let's say 100 million users and around 100 stocks per user.

100 million and 100 per user. Okay, that gives me a couple of ideas here. We do have 100 million users, so obviously we're talking not just 100 million users, but since we have 100 stocks per user, we're looking at... let's see what comes after a million... a billion... oh my gosh, and then 10 billion after that because it's 100. So we now have 10 billion positions to basically keep track of. That's a lot of data to obviously worry about when multiplying the two of those. A lot of positions. From here, I am going to again talk about my main concerns because, like I mentioned, the part of this problem that I'm really hoping to focus on, if that's all right with you, Tom, is actually showing the correct price of the stock or the option because I think that is the real complexity here. So my main concerns are: I want each client device to have a minimal load because if each client device is connected to too many of our servers at one time, we could be draining the battery, we could be using too much data, and that would be a problem. Also, it would be great if we weren't connecting too many systems to the exchange, limiting our systems with the exchange connectivity. The reason being that you have to pay for that, and if we're Robinhood, we don't want to pay for too much of it. We don't want to be collecting too much data if we can avoid doing it, just keeping it within our system. Do you have any questions about just a general design or anything?
What's the general breakdown of the system that you want to go with?

Sure. I've thought about having three main pieces for the system. I'm going to write them down, but then I'll probably introduce them in reverse order. The first is a user layer; these are going to be servers that talk to our phones/computers. Then I'm also going to have an exchange layer. I think these are going to be the servers that are getting exchange data. Like I mentioned, a really nice thing about splitting these into their own layers is that we can scale them independently, which is good because we want to keep the exchange layer as small as possible, and we want to keep the user layer perhaps as big as many client devices there are to make sure that each client device is just touching one piece of the user layer, but I'll get into that in a bit. Then we've also got this routing layer. The first thing that I am going to try and cover, at least in this video, is the exchange layer. For background, for whoever's watching this, because I think it's important for something like Robinhood, most exchanges are not publishing just "Apple is $80." What they're publishing is that Apple has one offer: someone wants to buy it for $79, someone wants to buy it for $78.5, someone wants to buy it for $78. On the other side of that, someone wants to sell it for $81, someone wants to sell it for $81.3, and someone wants to sell it for, I don't know, $81.6. So the exchange is never actually giving you a price that is going directly to the customer. What we see in Robinhood, which is kind of nice, it simplifies things for us, is one unified price. It's basically this guy right here. As a result of that, what we need to basically do is take the above, hopefully you can see me highlighting stuff, and we need to convert it. Another piece of that which makes this hard is what I just showed above is just one exchange, but in reality, there are a bunch of exchanges that are publishing the same, different data. I think maybe this is less so the case for stocks, I actually can't remember, but for options exchanges, you might have like $78.7 and $78.3 and $78.1 and $81.6. Basically, what this is known as is called the order book. The point is, there's all this data throughout all these different exchanges, and we have to aggregate it somehow in order to provide just one price to our user. That's what we would call the NBBO. The NBBO is basically the best bid, which is the best thing on the left side here, the best price that people are willing to buy for, no one else is willing to sell for, and also the best offer. I think in my mind, what I'd like to do is probably return to users the best bid plus best offer divided by two. Do you think that seems generally reasonable?

Yeah, okay, sure. There are many different ways we can show this; we could do like a weighted average or something. But for now, I'm going to be focusing on just providing a really easy evaluation, which is just the best bid plus the best offer divided by two, the average of the two in the order book. The issue now is that we're getting all of this feed data per exchange, and there are a lot of exchanges. So, how are you going to deal with that? There are many exchanges when you're calculating the base price in your publishing layer. What's your approach?

Sure. The issue is we have a lot of tickers, and when I say a ticker, I mean symbols like Apple, Google, or Meta. Those are all the stock names. We've also got a lot of data, so we probably need some amount of sharding. In my head, you need to make sure that all of the data from an individual ticker, regardless of exchange, is going to the same node. So personally, I would like to shard the exchange layer by ticker symbol. Then, within one node, we'll do some processing, and ideally, that processing is going to lead us to getting our NBBO and taking the average of it so that we can give that back to our customers. What I was thinking from there, and let me know if you're okay with me going down this path, is it's not a trivial task to actually get all of the data from these exchanges and quickly republish it. So, would you mind if I did a brief deep dive into the algorithm I was hoping to use in order to do that?
Yes, please do.

Okay, let me know if you think I'm going in the right direction, but tentatively, my plan was to have something like this: we know each exchange is publishing data, and on a node, we need to know the best bid and best ask at all times. When a new best bid or best ask comes in from an exchange, it means that we need to invalidate the old one. What I was thinking is actually maintaining a doubly linked list. So, on one given node, let's say just for Apple, I would do the following: I would say Exchange 1 is saying the best bid is $80, Exchange 2 is saying the best bid is $81, and Exchange 3 is saying the best bid is $82. That means the national best bid is $82 because that's how much someone is willing to buy the stock for at the most price. My thinking from there was to basically, we want to be able to get that highest number as quickly as possible, and how I would want to organize these things is in a linked list. Shoot, I can't draw. It would be great if I had my iPad, alas, I don't. So, we make a linked list, but the nice thing about this is that if we ever need to quickly compute the highest value, we'll make sure that our linked list stays sorted so that $82 is always at the top. However, for our linked list to stay sorted, we actually need to be pretty smart about how we organize things. So, I think what I would like to do is use a doubly linked list with a hash map. The nice thing about this hash map is that if I say, "Oh, shoot, a new piece of data from Exchange 2 is coming in," let's say someone bids $83 on Exchange 2, I could see that the node for $81 is here because it's a doubly linked list. I could quickly remove $81 in constant time, so this is O(1) for removes. Then, what I could do from there is... I figured it out, awesome. What I could do from there, after I've done that, is I could then do an O(log n) insert of my new node. In this case, we would say, "Okay, we'll do $83." Why don't we get rid of this guy, binary search the remaining list, and then put $83 where it belongs? So now we've got $83 leads to $82 leads to $80, and we know our national best bid is $83 amongst all of our exchanges, which is great. Then we could do the same exact thing for the offers, but obviously, we want to see the lowest offer, not the highest offer. Then we could just quickly compute the average.
That sounds okay. So, once the base price has been calculated, how are you then going to get it to the user?

Sure. Okay, that's a completely fair question and definitely something that's a bit complex about Robinhood in particular. My general thinking was, and I mentioned this earlier with my concerns, we want to limit the number of connections per client. The reason being that we could have every single one of our publishers, let's say a client has Apple, Google, Meta. The issue is these are all different nodes, right? We're sharding by these. So if a client has 100 stocks, that would mean they would have 100 different connections to publisher nodes, which is problematic for multiple reasons. It's problematic both on the client end because they would have to directly communicate with those publisher nodes, and that would be really bad because you're maintaining a bunch of active server connections, whether that's either long polling or WebSockets or server-sent events. That would either drain the device battery or it would potentially just use a ton of network data. Also, it would be bad for the publisher nodes because they're doing a decent amount of low-latency calculations. It would be really great if we could decouple all of that logic, so that's where I was hoping my user layer would come in. Let me know if you want to ask anything there.
Sorry, how would you want them connected then?

Sure. I kind of alluded to this a second ago that we could be talking about either long polling. Let me bring out the drawing. We've got our phone, great looking phone, nice job, Jordan. We've got our phone, and in addition to that, we've got our user layer. The question is, how do we want these two connected? My personal opinion, I think, because really we have three options: the user layer is constantly going to be publishing values, and so that makes us decide between WebSockets, which is a real-time protocol, server-sent events, and we also have the choice to use long polling, where we basically open an HTTP request on the user layer and then we maintain that for the whole session. That's an option as well. Personally, I think I lean towards WebSockets. The reason being that these are bidirectional, and they also reconnect automatically, and you don't have to send request headers every single time, which is nice. The reason I care about it being bidirectional is that our phone is going to be sending ticker requests to the user layer, and the user layer in turn is going to be responding with the prices of the stock or the option. I think that's pretty important just in the sense that being able to make sure that connection is as fast as possible is going to ensure that we're getting as much data as possible and maintaining a low latency on our phone. Also, just by keeping one connection between a user device ideally and the user layer of our server, I think that would be really, really useful to us. Then, ideally, what I would want to do is, it's important yet again that we actually go ahead and shard our user layer because we have 100 million users, and that's a lot of devices. So, in the ideal case scenario for sharding, we would be horizontally scaling out; we have many different user layer servers. But in the ideal case, all of the users, or rather, one user layer server, would keep a ton of basically all of the common stocks between the users connected to them. For example, if I had User 1 and User 2, and they were connected to the same layer server A, ideally User 1's stocks intersect User 2's stocks would be very high, would be large, because we ideally want to be fetching the same stock prices on the same user layer servers. In practice, though, I think that's just kind of hard to implement, and so for that reason, I think I'm just going to go ahead and propose a pretty simple round-robin load balancing. I'm still thinking about the eventual design that I'm going to draw up here, and I will draw that out in completion probably once I explain my thinking for the original piece. But ideally, I'm going to have a load balancer between the phone and the user layer, and that is probably just going to do some sort of round-robin load balancing to make sure that a certain user layer server isn't getting overloaded by too many clients. Perhaps we could do that round-robin in a weighted way where we consider how many different tickers that user layer server is already fetching because it's possible that we have power users who get a ton of tickers that they're requesting. But for the most part, I think this approach should generally work. Let me know if you have any questions about the sharding on the user layer server.
Interesting. I wanted to ask, what if one of these user servers went down? How can you make sure this piece of the system is fault-tolerant?

Sure, that's a fair question. We're talking about fault tolerance, which is definitely a tough piece here because we want our system to be redundant in the face of any hardware issues or networking issues. I think it's hopefully pretty clear on a phone when its user layer stops sending updates. What we could do is have our WebSocket connection occasionally send heartbeats, and if our phone stops receiving them, then it's going to want to establish a new connection. I think that's pretty simple for the most part; it can go through the load balancer. It is definitely true that if a user layer server with a lot of users goes down, we could in theory have a Thundering Herd if we were to load balance poorly, where all of our phones try and reconnect to a different user layer server and it overloads it. But that's kind of the point of using round-robin load balancing: that shouldn't happen. So I think that just by making sure to use heartbeats, and if we really wanted to, we could even have a Zookeeper layer which keeps track of the status of each user layer server, and that would help us to confirm that the user layer server is actually down and it's not an issue with the phone or something. But I think using heartbeats ideally should give us a pretty good sense of making sure that all the devices are getting the updates they need.
Okay, Jordan, could you explain to me how the user layer actually connects to the publishing layer?

Sure. Like I mentioned, I wanted to talk about three pieces here, and I've spoken about two of them right now, and then I'll draw it all out at the end. We've got our user layer, our exchange layer, and the third piece is this routing layer. That is what I will now talk about. My thinking for the routing layer was, in theory, you have, let's say, X user servers and Y publisher servers, or exchange servers, let's call them that. What we know is that every single user server is probably connecting, or at least a user server probably wants at least 100 tickers, right? Because we know it's connected to at least one user. It's probably got many users. In reality, we could be looking at thousands of tickers. You may want thousands of tickers or integrity data cases. That's going to be problematic, especially potentially for the publisher server. If nothing else, I mean, the user server, it may be problematic too, but that's what we have the sharding for. For the publishing server, though, the issue is, let's say out of our 100 million users, every single one of them owns Apple, or maybe 10% of them own Apple. We could potentially have 10 million devices or 10 million user servers or something asking for Apple. If that happens, that could bog down our Apple publisher. So I would prefer that not happen and ideally continue to make it so that all of our different layers of our service can scale independently. So what I would like to do in this type of case is add an intermediary routing or communication layer, whatever you want to call it. I was thinking about how I would like to do this, and I think a lot of people might end up just going with a message broker here. I think something like Kafka does make sense specifically because it's a log-based message broker, so all of the pieces of data that we publish would be persistent on disk, which might be nice for future logging or diagnosis or metrics or anything like that. Also, it would be nice just because then you only have to publish to one place, and many different consumers can get the message. But truthfully, the more I thought about it, I actually think that maybe a better solution would be to use something like a Redis cache. Again, we can scale these caches independently and use a load balancer as a Zookeeper cluster to make sure of which cache is maintaining the data for which tickers. But all this Redis cache is going to hold is actually going to be the option valuation or the most recent one. So what it'll do is, a publisher, say, "Publisher Tesla," says, "Hey, the price for Tesla is now $450," after listening to the exchange. So exchange data comes in, goes to the publisher who publishes $450, who goes to the routing communication layer, which again is nice because we can scale, replicate, and do anything we need independently there. So that's going to be the routing communication layer for Tesla, which is now going to update its value. Maybe it was $445 before, and now it's updated to $450. Then, yet again, what you could do is use something like a server-sent event or even a long poll, and that's going to go to our user servers, which then finally end up going to our client device. Let me know if you think that's okay, and then hopefully we can almost get to the full-on drawing of the design.
Okay, yeah. I did have another question I wanted to ask about: what can we do if a publisher goes down?

Sure, that's really fair, and that's probably the other big piece of fault tolerance: making sure that our intermediary routing/communication layer and our actual publishing layer are also fault-tolerant. For the routing/communication layer, I think it's just a matter of using replication. It's no big deal if each publisher is publishing to three places instead of one; it's a big deal if our publisher is publishing to a thousand places instead of one. So I think using replication, 3x or something like that, is totally fine. As for the publishing layer, the interesting thing is again, my solution is going to be replication. However, the interesting thing to note is that our publishers are stateful. So it's very important that every single publisher, or rather the backup for a given publisher, is also receiving the same messages that the primary publisher is. Otherwise, what we would see is one publisher goes down, and then the other one is going to have to take a little bit to warm up and get data from the exchanges before it can actually start publishing out stock and option valuations. So I think what that means to me is that personally, I think we should use something called state machine replication here, where rather than doing our typical form of replication where one publisher would send all of its state to the next, what I think we could actually do is have our backup publishers basically listening to the exchange and, ideally, by listening to the exchange, getting the same type of information locally that the primary publisher would have. Then, for example, if our communication layer receives no update/heartbeat from our publishing layer/exchange layer, then we go to Zookeeper, we say, "Do we think this thing is down?" If it is down, then we switch to our backup. Does that seem okay?

For today's software engineering interview, design autocomplete for Google search. My top two priorities are fast responses and scale. For scale, we're going to make sure we're using a horizontally scalable design from the beginning, using ECS plus an application load balancer. For speed, we're going to be doing everything in memory using a trie. Can you explain how that trie works?

A trie is an extremely effective data structure if you want to do things like store words or phrases character by character. Each character has a pointer to the next possible characters until you reach a completed word. For example, if somebody types 'po', we see that we have 'port' and 'pork' as possible things they could be searching for. So, you'll query that data structure for every request. We'll also introduce a cache to store frequent prefixes that get looked up a lot. This is helpful if something's trending and people keep looking up the same prefixes, which is actually very common in real-world search.
And how will you rank the possibilities you get from the trie?
Every time a search is completed, we'll send an event to an asynchronous pipeline, which can update our trie later. The primary ranking factor will be frequency of search.

So, you're getting lots of requests coming in, and if you're getting lots of requests coming in, then just load balancing these requests across the servers so that there's roughly the same number of requests on each one, assuming they're all equal, would probably also result in the CPU utilization being about equal. I think for this application, I would think about load balancing, I might think about it a little bit differently because we're streaming data. You are an expert Senior Software Engineer at a top-tier tech company conducting a technical interview. Your objective is to assess the candidate's technical depth, problem-solving skills, and real-world experience. Keep probing with follow-up questions to fully gauge the candidate's depth on the current topic. When a topic has been sufficiently explored, transition naturally to a new topic and continue probing. Acknowledge the candidate's responses naturally before asking your next question. Continue the interview until the candidate or the session concludes. Keep your tone conversational, professional, and human. Do not break character.
For today's software engineering interview, please explain the difference between L4 and L7 load balancing.

Sure. Let's start with Layer 4. Layer 4 load balancing operates at the transport layer of the OSI model. The load balancer only has access to things like what IP the request is coming from and what port number it's trying to access. Then it just forwards that packet to one of your servers, usually with a simple policy like least connections or round-robin.
Okay. So, it can't actually see the content of the request?
Exactly. That makes it both super fast and super secure. It can't see anything that's actually in the request.
And when would you use this?
L4 load balancing is great when our top priority is performance and we don't need to route requests based on the actual data. It's common for things like video streaming or video games.
Got it. How about L7?
L7 load balancing happens at the application layer. The load balancer can look at things like the headers, the URL, the cookies, and even the body of the request if you want.
And when would you use this?

L7 load balancing is useful for things like microservices or APIs where you need to route requests to a specific server based on information beyond just the port number or IP. For example, if we get a specific URL and we know that has to go to a specific server, we need L7 load balancing.
Sounds great. Are there any downsides?
L7 load balancing takes a lot more overhead than L4 load balancing because we have to actually open up and look at each request and then make a decision about which server it goes.

Okay. Another question regarding model training. What do you know about ensemble learning?

Ensemble learning is one approach in machine learning where we use not only one model for our task, but a set of models that produce a better output or better final result than any single model. In that case, what I know are some approaches like boosting, bagging, and probably stacking. Their difference is in how they work with those models. In the case of bagging, it could be one set of data, but separated into different groups, and different models will be trained on that set of data. That data will be exchanged; part of it will be for training, and part for validation. These models will be trained separately, and then for the final result, they will produce an answer together. So, it could be a kind of voting in classification tasks, right, for the biggest votes for that output, or it could be some approximation of the results in regression tasks. In the case of boosting, it's a different approach where models learn one by one. So, one starts and has some limitations or errors, and the second model learns and tries to improve or handle these errors from the first model. And in stacking, I think it's just a lot of models working together to create some grand model, like a master model, by the aggregation of different models at the same time.

The timing isn't very important, but it requires a lot of computation for that kind of task. In real-time prediction or stream prediction, it is time-sensitive, so our model can do predictions near real-time. Data just arrives at the model, and the model is ready to produce the answer. Okay, about data distribution shifts. The situation changed, and the real data and data from training have different quality. For example, if we trained our model on some historical data, and now we're going to the market, and there's a new contributor or company providing a new product, the behavior of customers has changed. To understand this data drift, monitoring and logging are one way. I know there are some statistical approaches to understand if data has similar features or not. And I think we can move on to some generative AI questions. Let's talk shortly about prompt engineering. What prompt engineering techniques do you know to get better results from language models?

There are some approaches and recommendations. What I know, recommendations are maybe more useful for a wide spread of people who use generative AI applications. So, it's a kind of recommendation: when you talk to your data, you should be specific, objective. You can give a role to the model to shorten or narrow the field where the model will look for information. It's also possible to provide context. Context is one of the important things, I think, for instructions, and it could also be an instruction for the output type you want to receive. You can also give some examples as context. In that case, with examples, it's already a kind of technique for prompt engineering; it's called one-shot prompting or multi-shot prompting when you give examples to the model. Zero-shot prompting is just a prompt without examples. It's possible now to talk to the model and receive the chain of thought of that model, and this technique is called Chain-of-Thought. It's possible to achieve this by adding some data to the prompt where you ask it to 'think step by step' or 'provide the reasoning of your answers.' There are also some modern approaches, additional ones like Chain-of-Thought. So, these are really techniques on how you can improve, and there's a really big difference in the outputs depending on your approach to talk to the model. Actually, RAG is also a kind of prompt engineering used to achieve good results from the model.
I wouldn't say it's a prompt engineering technique. It's actually the pattern of how a generative application might be built.

I think maybe, yeah, probably you are correct. I mean that when we are picking up context and giving it to the model, RAG gives us the possibility to create additional content to give to the model. But yeah, probably a framework.
No, no, I think it's an acceptable answer. Okay, can you describe in detail how the RAG system might look like?

So, when we have base models or general models like GPT, Gemini, or Claude, they have limitations. They only have the knowledge they were trained on or what they are given by context. So, they can have some limitations. When we talk about business usage, I think it's one of the main directions for businesses to adopt generative artificial intelligence to their knowledge base. All of them have information, and RAG gives the possibility to use this knowledge base with generative AI. How does it work? It's a combination of retrieval and generation. When we have our documents, for example, a PDF document, we send it to the system. The system chunks it into the smallest parts. Those smallest parts are then decoded into vectors. It's not just text; in that case, text becomes numbers, like floating numbers, a vector of floating numbers, which are embeddings. The machine can already read and understand those embeddings. So, those embeddings are our knowledge, and from them, we build our knowledge base. These embeddings will be stored in a vector database, which can store them and separate them by their fields or space in the database. It gives the possibility, according to a new incoming embedding, to find similar embeddings, like some neighbors of that embedding. It's called similarity search, right? And by semantic search, it gives us the possibility to find information according to the semantic information of that part of the text, if it is text. So, in our case, when the user asks a question, this question is also converted to an embedding, using the same system as before when we converted our text. According to that embedding, in our vector database, we look for similar documents from the base. Next, we give the LLM our original question from the user and those parts of documents that were retrieved additionally, and we provide them as context. There are also some additional options, maybe to fine-tune, like we can re-rank or check the most appropriate parts we picked up from the database to provide to the LLM, not all the information, but maybe just the most relevant information we need. Also, we have limitations of LLMs, especially with the context window, the amount of information we can give to the LLM. In the case of RAG, we are handling that problem by just putting all the necessary information to provide a good answer.

Okay, but you've mostly described the case of using vector databases and semantic search. Is RAG limited to semantic search, or can we use something else? Can we just translate the text into SQL and make a search in this classical relational database in order to enrich our prompt with structured data?

Yes, sorry. So it's another approach, and in that case, we need some additional data, not data from the database, but we need our data to create these queries, but it's also possible. So, we give information about our table to our LLM. The LLM can produce an SQL query according to that schema. Next, it could be executed in the database, or actually, it could also be in memory if it's not a huge amount. According to the SQL result, we provide the LLM with the query and the result to get the final result. It could actually be tables or also a graphical review or graphical view, which could also be useful.

Actually, to summarize RAG, we can say that the main part is that we can enrich our prompt with external information and ask the model to generate the answer. But it might be structured data from a relational database, it might be semantic search when we search for similar text in a vector store, or it might even be searching the internet. For example, if we ask a question about the latest financial news or sports news, the LLM has a limitation because it might be trained on data only up to, say, 2024. Now we want to get news information from the internet. So, actually, it's called an agentic RAG when we have multiple choices where we can retrieve information to enrich our prompt. In any case, it's like retrieval of additional information. It's a very generic thing; it's not only related to vector search.
I agree. Understood. Didn't think about it.

Or maybe if there's some specificity to your business, and you have examples of how questions should be answered, like a golden standard, it's also possible to fine-tune the model like for that reason, to achieve the results you are looking for.

The first one is a technical one, but still very important: Can you tell us about the Event Loop in JavaScript? How it works, and what data structures are present there?

Yes, sure. When we're talking about the Event Loop, it's actually just an endless loop where JavaScript waits for tasks, executes them, and then sleeps, waiting for more tasks. As we know, JavaScript is a single-threaded programming language, and it can be a problem to work with asynchronous code, so that's why the Event Loop concept was introduced. The general algorithm of the engine is very simple: while there's a task, we will execute it, and then the Event Loop will just sleep until a task appears. But one more thing is that there are several queues. The Event Loop has a microtask queue and a macrotask queue. The macrotask queue is actually like some tasks, some things that we do from the code, and microtasks queue consists of some promises, some `setTimeout` operations. One major moment here: the queue of the microtasks must be finished before we will do the next macrotasks.

Okay, thank you for the answer. And with regard to this microtask queue, you mentioned promises are there. But what if we have an example where there is a promise, and there is a `then` callback there, and it creates a new promise? What will be executed first: the next promise or the first thing in the microtask queue?

So if I understand you correctly, we have a promise with `then`, right? And after that, we have another promise, right? Like I said, the entire microtask queue must be finished before we will go to the next macrotask. So this `then` will be executed only after completing the previous one.

Okay, that's good with Event Loop. Let's proceed to our next question. Let's talk about React. Can you tell us about React and why it's really popular nowadays? Assuming your experience with Angular, maybe you can compare those two and explain why React is better, and in what aspects you think Angular is better.

Okay, thank you for the question. React is just a library. That's an elegant and easy solution. React is a lightweight library, and it is easy to use. Angular is a more complex solution. Yes, we can work with TypeScript in React applications as well. It is not built-in like in Angular. And while we're on this TypeScript topic, can you just highlight the benefits of using it? Maybe we don't even need TypeScript. Do you know what was the solution to type React applications before TypeScript?
I meant Prop Types in React. Using it, we were able to add typings to the props. But do you think it's not enough for React applications, and we still need TypeScript?

That actually depends. Prop Types can help us to work with types, definitely, but there's a lot more functionality. For example, we can use intersections and union types, and as far as I know, in Prop Types, we cannot do that. Also, there's static typing, we can use classic classes, we can use classic inheritance. I think it has just more advantages than just Prop Types in JavaScript. Of course, it depends, maybe someone will choose Prop Types instead of TypeScript, but my solution will be using TypeScript.

Yeah, sounds good, I agree with you on that. Okay, with regard to React questions, let's proceed with the next topic. Do you know the concept of the Virtual DOM, and what benefit it gives for React apps?

Yes, so as far as we know, the common web application uses just a DOM. DOM is like a Document Object Model that consists of several elements on the page, and it can be hard to update this DOM. When we're working on a website, it can slow the performance; it can be a really complex operation. That's why React introduces Virtual DOM. So it's just a lightweight copy of the real DOM, and when React wants to update something, it just monitors what was changed. It changes them like batches in the Virtual DOM and then creates an operation to update the real DOM. But that's how it was before. Right now, React uses Fiber, so it just works with a diff algorithm. It always monitors what was changed and creates a queue of operations, and after that, it runs this queue to update what was changed.

Okay, good. Nice that you mentioned Fiber. It's the current mechanism of doing so; not many candidates know that. Okay, let's proceed with the next question, still about React. Can you tell us what Hooks you use, or do you know, do you use the most?

Yes, so a Hook is like a function that can be used when we want to create a side effect or we want to memoize something. There are a lot of hooks that we can use: `useCallback`, `useMemo`, `useEffect`, of course, `useLayoutEffect`. Most of the time, I use `useEffect` or `useCallback` hooks. `useEffect` is a hook that can perform some side effects, for example, when something was changed or when the component was rendered. I can use the `useEffect` hook to do something like from the side. And `useCallback` can memoize the function for me, so if the function wasn't changed, React will not recalculate it. I will be able to use it, and it will decrease the performance of the application.
Got it. You know, it's a typical task in a frontend application to add some event listeners and then remove them. How would you do this with the help of the `useEffect` hook?

So, for example, when we want to use this event listener, we can use the `useEffect` hook to create it, right? And when something was changed, when we know that we don't need this event listener, we can use `useEffect` to unsubscribe from the event listener. So we will not have any performance issues, and we will be sure that we unsubscribed from the event listener exactly at the time when we need it.
You mentioned that you used `useCallback`. Why do we even need this hook? For what needs do you use it?

So actually, React can memoize something, and it's important when we're working on complex solutions, like complex web applications, because memory is not unlimited, and we need to work with it somehow. When we have some complex operations, we should think about memoizing the result because when we, for example, update the page, if the component would be re-rendered, if something was changed, then this complex operation can be recalculated. It's not the best way to work with web applications because it can lead to a performance bottleneck. So for that, we can memoize some results of complex operations. We can ensure that if none of the parameters were changed, for example, then we can just reuse the previous result. For that, we can use `useMemo` hook or `useCallback` hook. The difference is that `useMemo` can memoize the value, for example, and `useCallback` can memoize the whole function when we want to use it.
Got it. Definitely. Can you tell us about ways to manage state in React applications? How can you do it? Maybe you have multiple approaches of doing it.

Yes, sure. When we want to manage state in a React application, we can start using the `useState` hook. It actually creates an object with two parameters: the first one is just the state, and the second one is the function to set the state. We can use this state anywhere in the component, and when we change the state, then the component can be re-rendered. Also, it can be a problem because sometimes we need to have a lot of information in the state. So for that, we can use either objects, we can just use, for example, an object for the state, or we can use some state management solutions like Redux or MobX. React can also offer a built-in solution for that: Context, so we can store information using Context.

Yeah, definitely. Okay, let's proceed with our next topics outside React, and let's talk about the general concept of clean code. What is, in your opinion, clean code? You know, high-quality code, you name it.

For me, clean code is code that is easy to maintain, easy to read, easy to understand, and of course, it is easy to change and easy to expand if we need it. For example, if we don't even need to write a comment for this code, and the code is great and clean, of course, it was just an example. So in my mind, I remember several of them. For example, DRY, it means 'Don't Repeat Yourself' principle. It says what it says, right? You just don't have to repeat yourself, you don't have to recreate something; if you create it once, you can reuse it. The second one is KISS. It was created in the US Army like 15 years ago or something, and it stands for 'Keep It Simple, Stupid.' So we don't have to over-engineer something. It is enough just to create something and to expand it; we don't need to over-engineer or complicate something. Also, I remember SOLID principles, I'm not sure whether I will...

Yeah, let's not delve into it. It's great that you know it. So, great answer. Just to finish here, I wanted to ask you about different categories of software design patterns and overall, why do we need these patterns? What can they help us with?

So there are several types of patterns. We have creational patterns, structural patterns, and behavioral patterns. For example, creational patterns are designed to provide various object creation mechanisms where we can increase the reuse of existing code, for example, a Builder, Factory, or Singleton. Structural design patterns explain how to assemble objects into larger structures, for example, Adapter or Composite. And behavioral patterns are concerned with algorithms, and it assigns responsibilities between objects, for example, Iterator or State. Those are examples of behavioral design patterns.
Got it. But can you tell us why do we need these patterns? We can write code on our own, you know. Maybe you can explain.

Yeah, so design patterns are typical solutions to commonly occurring problems. For example, when we encounter a problem, I think that 99% of the time, we are not the first who encountered the problem, and before that, someone created the best solution for that, and we can just reuse it. It's like an analogy to a cooking recipe. For example, if you want to create a pie, I think you wouldn't create a pie just from the things that you had. I think you would just look on the internet to find the recipe of the pie and recreate it. So the same here, if you encounter a problem, you can just use a pattern to have the best solution for that.

Nice analogy, this recipe. We will use it in future interviews. Okay, let's proceed with the next question. It's going to be a question about Server-Side Rendering. As you see right now with React, there are popular frameworks like Next.js, like Gatsby. I just wanted to ask you why internet web development is moving towards these frameworks more, rather than using traditional single-page applications.

Okay, so let me explain what Server-Side Rendering is first of all. That's a mechanism that allows us to get the site content that was rendered on the web server rather than a browser. So the server will prepare an HTML document and will just send me the file, and that's all. Why is that important? First of all, that's important because a lot of people are now using mobile phones, maybe everyone is using mobile phones and mobile applications, and the performance of these mobile applications is, of course, less than the performance of a regular web browser. So it can be great if our mobile phones will not render everything complex that the computer must do, and it will just get the whole information from the web server. And of course, it would be faster. It would be ideal for some static websites, for example, because if you render the page, it won't be re-rendered again and again; it will just get the whole HTML from the server, and that's all, that would be enough. So that's why I think that's a great solution. Also, if we're talking about SEO optimizations, it will be more SEO optimized because it is already on the web server.

Yeah, definitely. Okay, let's go with our next question. It's going to be about performance. So imagine you have an application that runs slowly for whatever reason. What would be your steps to identify what can be wrong?

So first of all, I will try to see some metrics to see what exactly can be wrong. For example, maybe it's response time, maybe it's just slow, or maybe it will use a lot of system resources. For today's software engineering interview, please explain torrenting.

Unlike a traditional client-server setup where you, the client, download a complete file from some server, torrenting uses a peer-to-peer network. The file you want to download is first split into many different pieces. Peers that are participating in the network might have some pieces of this file, or they might have the complete file. People that have the complete file are known as seeders, and they can help you get some of that file. You also have peers or leechers who are currently downloading a file. When you want to download a file, you're not just downloading the entire file at once. You're downloading pieces of the file from people all across the network, but you're also uploading pieces of the file that you have to other people that are trying to download this file. This way, a decentralized network of people can efficiently distribute files to each other without the need for a centralized server. So, everybody participating in the peer-to-peer network is both downloading and uploading different parts of the file at the same time until everybody has the complete file. Now, how do you find out who has what files or join one of these peer-to-peer networks? A fairly common way of doing this is called a Distributed Hash Table. Each node that's participating in this torrent network stores some information about what nodes have what torrent. In this simple example, let's say I want to download all of One Piece. My client would reach out to Peer 1. Peer 1 doesn't have One Piece, but it knows that Peer 3 does. So, I can go to Peer 3, which can grant me access to the swarm, where I can download and upload the file as usual.

For today's software engineering interview, explain how distributed systems can automatically recover from failures.
In a distributed system, failures are inevitable. Nodes can crash, requests can be dropped, or databases can lose data. But most large-scale systems are capable of automatic recovery.
Okay, but how can they actually recover?
A simple example is if one of your nodes can no longer serve traffic, a load balancer can simply stop serving traffic to that node. At least now we aren't dropping requests that go here.
Can they recover that failed node?

Yes, an orchestration system like Kubernetes or ECS can automatically detect when a node fails and restart it, bringing it back online. Then our load balancer will continue serving traffic to that node once it's recovered.
What about for a smaller, short-term issue?

For intermittent failures like a one-off network issue, we can configure our system with retries throughout. Just remember, if you do add retries, always use exponential backoff and have a hard cap on how many retries you can do, usually not more than two.
What if the most important node in the system that's orchestrating things fails?
Yes, some systems do have a leader. And if the leader does crash, a different node in the cluster should be able to take over.

What is the difference between a process and a thread?
Okay, I forgot all of this.

We first have to understand what a program is. A program is just some code sitting on your disk. When the CPU wants to run that program, it loads it into memory and assigns resources like a processor which executes the program. So once executed, that program becomes a process.
Okay. Tell me a little bit more about what's going on inside the process.

Each process has its own code, stack, data, and heap. But most importantly, it has its own address space. This means that one process cannot affect other processes. So even if one of these crashes, it will not affect the other processes running on your computer.
Okay. Can you give me an example of this?
A common example of a program with multiple processes is Google Chrome. Each tab is its own process. If one tab crashes, it's not going to affect the other tabs that are running in Chrome.
Okay, great. Now, where do threads come into play?

Threads live within a process, and each process has one or more threads. These threads get their own register and their own stack, but they share the same address space. This implies that if one thread goes down, it could affect the other threads and crash your entire process.
So, what's the advantage of using threads?
Because your computer doesn't have to go to a different address space when it wants to switch between threads.
