All right. So, we are going to discuss a new question: design sponsored ads experience at google.com. So, this is a very vague and ambiguous problem statement provided to you by your interviewer. And think of it that you are a product manager talking to a customer figuring out the requirements in order to design sponsored ads experience at google.com.

So first we'll collect the functional requirements for this question. As a candidate my first question would be: hey, what are sponsored ads? So sponsored ads let retailers advertise their products on Google and when a user like you and me click on it, Google will earn referral fees, right? So as an example, if I search iPhone in my browser, you can see sponsored ads, you know, on the site. My next question will be, can a sponsored ad contain products from different sellers? Yes, it can. And can a sponsored ad contain several products from one seller? For example, there can be multiple iPhone products from Amazon.com. And yes, the answer is it can — it can have products from multiple vendors as well as it can have multiple products from a single vendor in the ad. Is there a ranking system involved that selects which retailers to show in the sponsored ads? Yes, there's a ranking system. Let us say it's simple. It's based on the clicks. If the user clicks on the ad, the score of that ad will increase by five points and eventually this will be a feedback loop cycle that will keep on running and as and when the ad is scoring more and more points it will have more probability of being shown in the sponsored ads for that product. How many sellers and products we're talking about? Let's say 100 million sellers and 1 billion products.

Now let's go over the non-functional requirements. We want a system which is highly available — at least four nines of availability — and what does availability mean here: that sellers submitting the products for sponsored ads, the system should be available to intake those products in our system and process them; users searching for a product, let's say I go to google.com and I search iPhone, and I should be getting the sponsored ads, right, so the system should be highly available. And we can have a stretch goal of five nines of availability. Eventual consistency — so when you explain these non-functional requirements you have to be very clear in terms of explaining what it means in this current context — right, so eventual consistency here means rendering the ads based on the score. When a user one searches maybe he'll get result ABC, another user gets BCD, and let's say user one again searches, he or she gets BCD. But let's say BCD are the eventual consistent ads results based on the updated scores. So that's what I mean by eventual consistency here. The latency and score processing for the ads after ingestion of the clicks — let's say the SLO is 3 minutes at P99. Latency in rendering ads when user searches the ad on the browser — P99 less than 200 milliseconds. Latency in new ad processing — let's say a seller or a retailer is submitting an ad product — we'll call it P99 as 1 minute. Durability — we don't want any data loss in our pipeline. When user clicks on an ad or a seller or retailer submits an ad product, we need to ingest that — we don't need to lose the data in our systems and in the pipeline. The system should be fault tolerant. It should be scalable. Scalable here means that when the traffic is increased, the load is increased, number of users, number of sellers, number of ads per seller — our system should be able to handle those cases. Now, if we get some time, we can define here are the things below the cut line like the system should be frugal or cost-friendly. We need to take care about the security in the system because we're dealing with, you know, user data, you know, seller data, etc. So, we need to make sure our system is secure, but we'll get to that if only the time permits.

Now, design doesn't come for free. Scalability doesn't come for free. We need to do some data calculations or back of the envelope calculations. Right? Here, your interviewer is expecting you to come up with some reasonable assumptions like how many daily active users, how many daily active advertisers you are thinking about, right? And you're building a scalable system. So they expect some good judgment from you on these numbers. So search TPS — you know, let's say the daily active users searching for a keyword like iPhone, let's say it's 10 million. Average search per user per day let's say is 10. So that means that 10 million daily active users multiplied by 10 divided by 10 to the power 5 — so usually one day has roughly 86,400 seconds but I have rounded it off to 10 to the power 5 which is 100,000 — so if you divide you know daily active users and number of searches happening per day per user the answer is 1,000 requests per second. However peak can be 100x of that because usually when we see during the daytime in the geography, whatever geography we are part of, the calls are more than outside the business hours. So 100x translates to 100k requests per second at the peak TPS. Now ad creation TPS — let's assume daily active advertisers are 100k and average new ads per day per user is 10, or per seller in this case. So the TPS translates to 100k advertisers multiplied by 10 ads per advertiser divided by 10 to the power 5 which translates to 10 requests per second and again peak let's assume 100x so it's 1,000 per second. Now click events TPS — for that let's again say, okay, daily active users we already called out 10 million and average clicks per user per day on the sponsored ads is 10 — and this translates in a similar way to 1,000 clicks per second and peak is 100k clicks per second. Storage — let's say we need to store all this data for 10 years for audits, compliance, etc. And we know that we have daily active advertisers as 100k and then 10 ads per advertiser per day and let's ballpark, when one ad product we are storing in a database or storage layer it's 500 bytes — we'll do the data modeling down the line but at this point in time we're just taking a ballpark estimate. And then 365 days in a year, for replication at least three copies, and for 10 years — okay we need to store the data — this translates to roughly 5.475 terabytes of data we are talking about. It's good to talk about system properties now that we have done the back of the envelope calculations. The system seems to be more IO intensive — network and storage IO. It doesn't seem to be a compute intensive system. We are not doing image processing or running heavy compute algorithms on top of our processor. So the system seems to be more IO intensive rather than compute intensive.

Let's talk about the design. The high level design — very very high level design which is a 32,000 ft overview. So let's call — this client is a user and this client is a seller or retailer. Okay. So we'll first go with this client, you know, seller or retailer submitting their new ads to our system. So our first layer will be an API gateway. The responsibilities of API gateway will be rate limiting to prevent us from denial of service attacks or distributed denial of service attacks, authentication, authorization and load balancing. So what happens is, you know, when they want to upload their ad product they need to provide a small image for that ad or a thumbnail — right — first it will get a pre-signed URL from the ad service and it will directly upload to the S3 object storage. We can use S3 in AWS or equivalent in Azure or GCP. So once it's uploaded it will send the whole payload — hey, what's the ad, what is the content of the ad, what is the product etc — to the ad service and after that we have an ad creation topic on Kafka, you know, streaming system and we have a consumer of that Kafka and it will put that an ad record in a database. So as and when the record goes in the database we have a lambda in AWS or Azure function or equivalent in GCP running — which is equivalent to for example triggers in relational database — here we call it like DDB streaming, you know, in DynamoDB case, or equivalent we can have a function which is a serverless function which will process the new entry and based upon that, you know, it will put the data in the cache so that the search is faster. We can read it from the cache for that product type — what ads we have — and before it adds to the database here we need to make sure for a new ad we add some by default some score for that ad and that is where, you know, after the score is added and database has all the elements we process it through a serverless lambda or Azure function and it goes to the cache. The cache will hold, you know, the top 20 for that product type for example, or top 100 or top 25 — some very minimal number, whatever you want to show in terms of sponsored ads, you know, based upon the score. And let's go with a flow, you know, where a user basically clicks on an ad — again through API gateway it will go to click service and the clicks will be, you know, handled by a Kafka topic and there will be a consumer which is a clicks processor which will consume these clicks and based on the clicks incoming, you know, it will have some rules cached in it which will have some TTL time and it will allocate — hey, this ad is clicked, let me give it, you know, plus 30, plus 50, plus 5, you know, whatever score scoring system we have — and based upon the rules defined in the scoring system. And based upon that, it will update, you know, your ad score in the database. Again, as and when score is updated, you know, it will call the serverless lambda and again cache will be refreshed. Let's say this ad has got a good score and it comes in top 25 or top 100 — then this cache will be updated just to hold the top 25 or top N entries for that product type. Now the last one is, you know, a client user searching a product in browser — then it will go to search service through API gateway, first it will look into the cache — hey, is that product type in the cache, let me get the results faster — maybe top 10, top 30 depending on the screen we are using whether it's a, you know, laptop, whether it's a mobile device or an app, you know, depending on that it can return the results right. And if this cache hit doesn't go through then we have a cache miss scenario which means that for that product type we'll just call the database and get the data from the database and render to the client. So this is a very 32,000 ft overview of the design, very high level design. We will go into the internals, we will understand the internals and we'll talk about various trade-offs. So let's jump into it.

Our first trade-off will be the choice of database — you know, whether we are going with a relational database or a non-relational database. Right? So in this case if you see we don't have very hard transactional requirements. We are just getting clicks and this click processor is updating the ad, you know, based upon the updated score. So it's not like, you know, several users in a concurrent fashion for example trying to update their bank account, or some system where concurrency and transactions are important. But in this case we just have click processor only as the only entity updating the ad score in the database. So we should be good, you know, with this model. So we don't want a strict transactional system here. Data velocity and volume is high by the way. So NoSQL databases like DynamoDB also support transactions. However, we don't need transactions in this case as I said. But any scale-out DB, you know, on the cloud like DynamoDB or equivalent in Azure or GCP will work in this case.

What about database sharding? So when we did some back of the envelope calculations above we notice our data storage is around 5.5 terabytes. Usually we do sharding if the data storage is more than 50 terabytes. So based upon the data storage in terms of terabytes we don't need sharding. However the write requests — which means like clicks — number of clicks coming to our system at peak is 100k. Ad creation is at peak 1,000 per second. In total we have updates happening at around 100k per second at peak. This doesn't do well if we don't shard our database. So sharding is required generally when write requests are more than 10k, 15k or 20k per second. Hence in light of, you know, the number of write requests we're getting per second, we'll be sharding our database.

Next is synchronous versus asynchronous processing. So for the ad creation if you see we have a peak of 1,000 requests per second. Now based upon that you might not just use a Kafka — it's not required to have a pub-sub system, you know, sitting in — you can just process the request, you know, create an ad in database and process it synchronously, right, so maybe Kafka sitting in between can be an overkill. But however I thought, you know, once we receive a new ad there might be several validation steps that needs to be executed — if the ad is duplicate or not, trust and safety, compliance, etc — and hence I thought it might take more things to be done and we can process the ad asynchronously and customer experience will be better because we can just process it asynchronously and we can say hey we received your ad we are going to process it and latency that they see on their side will be significantly lower. But I mean there is no right and wrong answer in system design so you can still say I'll do synchronous processing given that number of requests is only 1,000 per second and as per requirements we are not given to handle the duplicate checks, trust and safety issues, etc. So, if you say I want to do synchronous processing because of this and that, that will also work well in this case. Now for clicks if you see we have a peak of 100,000 per second. Now async processing will be really good for processing the clicks because the meat of the problem is to let the user see the ads. Clicks we can process in an async way — you know, it can decouple the processing of the clicks and we can also batch the clicks before we update the database. By consuming the clicks in a way that hey we consume the clicks, we batch them, and then we make a one batch call to the database — and that way we can reduce the load on the database, we can do any number of things. So I feel it's good to do the clicks processing in async way and if our peak is even more than that, let's say 1 million per second, generally it would be better if we do an async processing.

Now batching versus non-batching of the clicks. So we know that our latency SLO for processing the clicks and updating the ad score is 3 minutes. We can leverage this SLO, right, in a way that you can batch the clicks for example based upon 30 seconds, or if the number of clicks we have got to process is 10,000 — all of this is configurable, right, maybe 15 seconds or 1 minute, you know, whatever occurs faster — based upon the time or number of clicks we have to process. And then we can just aggregate the scores, you know, across the ads. For example in 10,000 clicks we have 5,000 clicks for one ad — we can just create — okay if the click yields plus five for that ad and if there are 5,000 clicks for ad one we'll have 25,000 as a score to be added for ad one. And again, you know, this will reduce the load on the database and that's where it's good to do the batch API call — and batching will really help in this case.

What about caching? So we have Redis or Memcached as our options. So I'll prefer Redis in this case because Redis has some good data structures like sorted sets which we want to use — we don't need to reinvent the wheel by implementing these in Memcached. It's a very plain or vanilla cache. So, sorted set is a combination of hash table and skip list. So hash table will have the entry which is the ad and the score for that ad and skip list will have, you know, the category — which means for example iPhone — and the sorted list of ads, you know, sorted by scores. And in terms of cache storage again we can do some back of the envelope calculation here. Let's assume we have 100,000 product types like stationary, comic book, iPhone etc. And we want to store top 25 per product type. And let's assume record size is 1 KB per ad entry. And which translates to 100k product types multiplied by 25 and then 1 KB record size — which is 2.5 GB — which we can easily put in cache. So nowadays we have systems which can have 1 terabyte of cache easily. Yes, cache is expensive, but, you know, we'll have faster results because it will be an in-memory processing and we can provide a better customer experience and reduce latency to our customers.

So, let's do some data modeling here. In terms of data modeling, if you see it, we have an ads table. We have ad ID which is the primary or partition key. We have a product ID, product type can be iPhone, we have a title for that ad, price for that ad, image which is a thumbnail for that ad, seller name, seller ID, score for that ad, some metadata, creation time and update time. We need to create index here. The index here we are creating is on product type and score. So product type is the partition key for that index and the score is the sort key. These are very DynamoDB specific things but in general if you say I'm creating index on product type and score which will be good enough. Because if you do the onion peeling things work in a similar way. So why do we need this index? Because let's say we have a cache miss and the cache is not having — let's say if a product type iPhone — let's say cache restarted or something happened with the cache or it's a product type not found in the cache for now — then we have to fall back on the database. In order to make sure our database queries are faster with low latencies, we can just use this index. Hey, for this product type sorted by score, give me top 10, top 25, right? It's still better than searching the whole table and then finding out who are the top 25. So that way we are leveraging index for goodness. Now, if you see, I've selected ad ID as a primary key for the table, not the seller ID. I'm not storing all the seller ads or ad products in a single partition because I don't want hot partitions or non-uniform partitions, right? Because several sellers can have more ads or ad products than other sellers and this will cause non-uniform partitions. With ad ID being a primary key, you know, we are making sure that we are creating uniform partitions in this case.

So now comes the interesting part of the question which is we'll do several deep dives. Our first is: how to handle the case when a user searches 'gifts for 10 year old'? And before we go into deep dives, you know, usually your interviewer expects you to identify some good areas around your design and they expect you to find the most interesting components of your design and then you at least deep dive into a few of them, right, showing the depth of your knowledge. And that is why we are focusing on deep dives now.

So how to handle the case when user searches for example 'gifts for 10 years old'? You can see I've searched gifts for 10 years olds and I'm getting multiple sponsored products. So the first approach is — we can add a tags field to the database. For example, if it's a comic book product type, someone is creating an ad for that — the product type is comic book — we can add tags in our database like 'gifts for 10-year-old' or 'children's gift' etc. And when the ad entry is added to the database we know that this lambda will process and add to the cache based upon the score. And this will add in the Redis cache and there will be three sorted sets. For example for comic book I have these many ads, and for 'gifts for 10 year old' we are having again the same entries right, and 'children's gift' also all the three same entries. If someone searches 'gifts for 10 years old' I can just look into the cache, hey if there's an entry for this just get me that list and return it. Now this approach is good but it has several limits — right, for example you cannot predict what the user will be searching for. They can search for example 'gifts for maybe 11 years old' or 'give me a gift for my child'. Anything can be like that right? So you cannot predict what user can do in their query. So in light of this, you know, this approach is not very solid and also it is causing repeated data entries which can cause sync issues and redundant data is there and will lead to more memory consumption and duplication. So we have another approach — we can add a machine learning based context service. Whenever the user searches a keyword the context service will return some relevant product types, right. For example, if someone searches 'gifts for 10 years old', you know, this query will be passed to context service — it will return some keywords for example 'stationary', 'comic book', or 'a toy', etc. And based upon that the search service will just search in the cache for those product types, get top 10 for, you know, three product types for example 'comic book', 'stationary' and 'toy', and we'll sort the overall results in memory and we'll return the response to the user. So this way it's more extensible, it's better — you know, you don't have to rely on what user is going to search — and no data replication or more memory consumption etc.

So our next deep dive is: how to handle the case when there are no results for the product — for example, 'pencil'. Now if I go to google.com and I search pencil, if you see there is no sponsored products returned. Right? So we'll keep it simple — just return no results as is.

How to handle a case to ensure new seller ads are shown to the customer? You know, let's say I'm a new seller onboarding and I'm submitting a new sponsored ad product, right, for a product type which is quite popular and which people have already clicked. And if I'm a new entry into the market, you know, Google will not show my product because my score has not been generated — I don't have any score — and for this product type the sellers who have more score for their products will eventually be shown. So it's more like a starvation problem, right. So how do I deal with such a case? For any new seller ad, provide a default score which is more than the current maximum score for the product type so that we give them a fair chance. And this will ensure that new sellers or new ads are promoted and not being starved in our system. And that is what, you know, we want to have because as a business we want different sellers to get products in our system so that we can have sponsored ads and if they click on that, if users click on that, you know, we get — as Google as a business — we get referral fees.

All right. So next is how to handle and process millions of incoming clicks per minute or maybe 30 seconds. So it's a million requests coming to us and if you think about the design, you know, when we get the clicks we're processing the click and just adding the score and updating the database. So we are making an IO call which is expensive and generally how a Kafka consumer works is, you know, it consumes a message, it processes it, then it consumes the next message, processes it — and it works in a sequential way, right. So if there are millions of clicks coming per minute we might have, you know, higher latency in processing them. So how do we make sure we process the clicks very fast also given that we are doing an IO call to process each and every click? Or even in the batch way we are processing a batch, then we update the database, and then we process the next batch — so it's all going in a sequential way how Kafka consumer works. So the solution here is to have a load balancing design. Let's say how Kafka works — Kafka has several broker machines and let's say we have 10 partitions for click topic in Kafka and these partitions are P0 to P9, all 10 partitions. We can create multiple instances of our service which is click processor — let's call it click processor 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5. This is just for demonstration purposes. It's extensible. You can create as many as you want. But think about like, you know, each cluster has one primary and two secondaries. And if primary goes down then secondary becomes primary. You can have a ZooKeeper like system which is monitoring this. But overall the idea is that we load balance how many partitions are being consumed by a primary right. So which means that if we do 2:1 — again for demonstration we can have 3:1, 1:1, right, it's extensible system we are trying to build — but for the example let's say we are doing 2:1 mapping which means two Kafka partitions to one click processor instance mapping. So P0, P1 are being consumed by click processor one, P2, P3 by two, P4, P5 by click processor 3, and then P6, P7 by click processor 4 and P8, P9 by click processor 5. That way we are doing parallel processing. We are moving fast and within a partition the messages are in order. So if our partition key is let's say based upon an ad product — this means that for all the clicks happening for that ad it is coming in in order and we are processing in order. We are not doing out of order processing within a partition. So that way you can scale your system and you can process, you know, the messages faster and you can enable parallel processing.

So our next deep dive will be: how to ensure correctness in processing the ad score. Let's talk about the regular flow, you know, in the click processor — how it works. As I said it consumes the clicks, you know, till the batch size or the time window is elapsed — it does in-memory score updates for the different ads in that batch and then it will call the batch API of the database to update the scores of different ads and after it is done it will basically commit those messages in Kafka. You know, what is the maximum offset — so every message in Kafka has an offset — so if you commit that, all the messages behind that offset or message ID automatically gets removed from Kafka. It means that they have been processed and they can be deleted. So this is the regular flow — it consumes the batch, it updates the scores for different ads in the batch, and then after database is updated with the updated ad scores for different ads in the batch it will commit the offset in Kafka and Kafka will delete those messages and after some restart or failover happens we can just process from the ads which are not processed in general. So the situation is: let's say the click processor updates the ad scores in the database after processing the batch but it didn't commit the offset in Kafka — or the messages which it has processed, it forgot or it missed telling to Kafka that hey I processed these messages — and the primary click processor crashes. It can crash because of many reasons — hardware, software, power, networking, etc. But let's say it was not able to commit. Now upon restart, click processor will again process the uncommitted messages, you know, thinking that they're not being processed and the ad scores will be incorrectly updated. In this case, you know, the scores for the ads which have been processed, you know, it will lead to doubling the score. Not doubling the entire score, but doubling, you know, whatever number it had previously when it processed the batch — and it can cause accuracy, correctness issues, you know, bias in our system. So how do we handle such a case? So the solution here is to add, you know, a new field in the database called 'message offset' for every ad and before processing, you know, the click event per ad the click processor will check if the message offset of the ad click event is greater than the message offset for that ad in the database. Now if it is — the one which we are processing is greater than — then sure we can process it because it's a new message. But if it is less than or equal to what the offset we have stored in the database it will ignore that click event because it's already been processed. So let's go over an example to make things, you know, clearer. So let's say we have this Kafka topic and we have several messages coming with offset 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 and initially we have ad ID 1, 1, then 2, 1, 3, and 1. So after initial processing of this batch, you know, this will be the state of our ads table. Ad ID one the score is 20 because we have four instances of ad one and plus five for each click event — so five multiplied by four is 20. Similarly ad ID 2 is offset three, just one instance, so score is five. Ad ID three also has score of five. The maximum offset for ad ID one we have six, ad ID two we have three, and ad ID three we have five. So this is how, you know, the database is updated. And let's say the click processor didn't commit the offset in Kafka and click processor failed over or restarted — then it will start to reprocess these messages. Now during the reprocessing of the messages it will ignore all the messages for ad ID 1 because, you know, offset one is less than six. Similarly offset 2, 4 and 6 are also less than or equal to six. We need a message or a click event which is at an offset more than the one which we have already processed for this ad. So using this technique, you know, we can make sure we have correctness in our system and we are ensuring that things are accurate. So we can have more deep dives as well — for example, how to handle huge write loads for a given product type. Let's say Apple launched a new iPhone and many sellers are trying to, you know, sell that iPhone or multiple users are clicking for that iPhone. How do we process huge write loads in our system? That is another deep dive we can consider but I'll leave it up to you to think about a solution around it.

Final design and recap. So this is our final design basically. So again let's do a recap — the seller client who is submitting the new ad, you know, it will go through API gateway which does the rate limiting, authentication, authorization, load balancing. Again we are processing it through Kafka — you can just remove Kafka if you want and give a reasoning — but as I said there's no right and wrong answer in the design, it's more about the justification you give. In this case I'm using Kafka because I think in processing there might be many things we have to do — for example compliance, trust and safety, there can be many business logics around it written in ads processor. So I don't want to hold the TCP connection for long. I want to process async for better customer experience and low latency. And once it's processed, I'm creating a new ad in DynamoDB. And I'm adding a new record for this ad. And this score will be the maximum score, current maximum score for that particular product type which can be got from the index. Cool. So next is, you know, the client who is the user who is going to browser and searching an ad product or clicking an ad product. Let's say they click on one of the sponsored ad products — it will go to click service. This click service will, based upon the ad ID as partitioning key, route the Kafka partitions — it will be routed to the right partitions based upon the hash algorithm that Kafka uses based upon the partitioning key, in this case which is the ad ID. To handle the huge load, you know, I've created several instances as we demonstrated earlier and the right primary, you know, will process the in-order messages for that partition and do the batching based upon the time limit or number of messages in the batch. For example if number of messages reached is 10,000 or 30 seconds have passed, whichever is faster — it will process that batch and aggregate the score per ad and make a one batch API call to update the record in DynamoDB and this reduces the load on our database. And before that it can refer to the scoring rule system — there is a — it can cache the scoring rules with some TTL. Now once the record is added here, you know, new entry, there will be a DynamoDB streaming process through lambda and lambda will update the Redis cache in this case and it will maintain a sorted set for each product type — what are the top, you know, 25 or top N based upon the score for that product type. And the user again searching on browser will go to search service — first it will resolve, you know, the keyword, from there it will, for example for 'gifts for 10 years old' or 'children's gift' it will give some relevant product types, and it will search the product types in cache. If it is able to find that then well and good. If it is not able to find that it will refer to DynamoDB. Let's say the cache crashes — it's in-memory data, there's no data, cache needs to build up. So in case of cache miss we will go to the index for this product type, we'll get based upon the score sorted scores, we'll get top N or top 25, top 50, whatever we want to get and we can just give it to the client right. And there might be some product types which are not commonly used or searched for — we don't want to put them in cache to save on cost, we can just go to DynamoDB. The only trade-off is that we'll be able to search — I mean slower than cache — but it will be still faster because of the index availability. One thing I missed is that before creating a new ad we need to get a pre-signed URL and first upload the image to S3 and then send the payload. The advantage here is that we are not sending the whole image payload to ad service and then it is updating to S3 — it's better that we just update it. And pre-signed URLs are pretty secure. It has some time limits for how long it's valid, what IP addresses can access it. So, it's a very safe and secure mechanism that AWS has provided. So, that's our design overall.

We also want to cover some breadth. For example, monitoring. It's good to talk about some technical and business metrics that, you know, we should care about in this system design. Technical metrics like CPU utilization, memory or heap utilization, number of threads, number of pending messages in Kafka to see how we are processing the messages, end-to-end processing latency for ads creation, click event processing, you know, cache hit percentage for that search. Some business metrics like number of clicks per ad, search latency customer-facing, seller ad creation customer-facing latency, customer reported incidents, some qualitative feedback from the customer using the sponsored ad products, etc. Testing — you can do unit testing with more than 90% line and branch coverage, integration testing, performance testing, scale testing, alpha beta testing. For deployments, you know, you can dial up — you know, based upon the customers — assuming it's a brand new system, you can start with 1%, you know, get some feedback, observe the metrics, right? And then dial it up slowly to 5%, 10%, 25%, 50%, 70%, and 100% with proper soak period across the dial-ups. If time permits you can also talk about how you can be cost friendly or frugal and how to handle the security in the system. So these are some breadth topics which you can talk about, you know, with your interviewer.

You are an expert Senior Software Engineer at a top-tier tech company conducting a technical interview. Your objective is to assess the candidate's technical depth, problem-solving skills, and real-world experience. Keep probing with follow-up questions to fully gauge the candidate's depth on the current topic. When a topic has been sufficiently explored, transition naturally to a new topic and continue probing. Acknowledge the candidate's responses naturally before asking your next question. Continue the interview until the candidate or the session concludes. Keep your tone conversational, professional, and human. Do not break character.
For today's software engineering interview, explain how you'd handle authentication for a website.

I will just use basic auth. On every single request from the client to the server, we'll include this header with a B64 encoded version of their username and password, which the server can validate against our username and password in the database.
Okay, so we have to share unencrypted credentials on every single request with this method. Can you do it another way?

That's a good point. Instead, let's use session auth. A user will make one request with their username and password. We can validate it on the server side and issue them a session key via cookie, which is stored in memory on the server side. Any subsequent request from the client will include that session ID, which we can validate against what we have in server memory. So they don't have to send their username and password every time.
Better, but this won't work in a distributed environment unless we're using a shared state with something like Redis.

Okay, in that case, we can use JWT auth. The user will make a login request, and the server will use a secret key to generate a JWT that they can pass back to validate themselves on every single request.
And why is this better than session auth?

This is better because we're not depending on server memory to store a session ID. Any server on the backend which has access to this secret key and the JWT passed from the client can validate that user. So no matter how many servers we have on the backend, if they have this secret key, they can validate that request.

You are an expert Senior Backend Engineer (Node.js / AWS) at a top-tier tech company conducting a technical interview. Your objective is to assess the candidate's technical depth, problem-solving skills, and real-world experience. Keep probing with follow-up questions to fully gauge the candidate's depth on the current topic. When a topic has been sufficiently explored, transition naturally to a new topic and continue probing. Acknowledge the candidate's responses naturally before asking your next question. Continue the interview until the candidate or the session concludes. Keep your tone conversational, professional, and human. Do not break character.

So, let's proceed with some general questions. I usually like to ask them at the start of any interview. I just want you to talk about your experience, some challenges that you face, probably describe the problem that you are most proud of to resolve – not necessarily a problem, but a challenge that you had and that you successfully passed. Do you think that this is like the greatest of your achievements?

Yes, I can start with my experience. At EPAM, I had a project with AWS in it, but we used to have only EC2 instances. Everything was running on EC2 instances, but it was not really effective for our case because we didn't have a lot of loads, and we were wasting some of our money. We decided to move to AWS Lambdas, and it was pretty difficult for us to do that because of many unexpected things that were occurring during the process. But eventually, we did that, so I'm really proud of that.

Good. I also actually wanted to ask about AWS; it's one of my questions, by the way, for this interview. Since you mentioned it, let's proceed with it. I wanted to ask you about what makes AWS and other cloud providers game-changers right now. Why don't we want to implement everything ourselves to reduce money, you know, at all? Just pay for our own infrastructure, our own use, don't depend on a cloud that can, you know, just raise the prices one day, and then we are in debt.

I guess it is the time that is very crucial because when you use something like AWS, you don't need to spend a lot of time building your own infrastructure, finding proper specialists, and so on. Probably most big companies tend to spend that time implementing real features to provide to their users and to get feedback and profit quicker, faster. Yes, I think that is the most important thing with clouds.
Okay, and specifically to AWS, are you aware of the term 'shared responsibility model' of the cloud providers? What does it mean?

If I'm not mistaken, it is like a diagram that represents what is the responsibility of AWS and what is the responsibility of the customer, us. Like about security, for example, physical security of the machines is on the AWS side, so the customers don't need to think about that at all. And the security, for example, in the application that is hosted in AWS, that is already the thing that customers need to think about.

Yeah, this is correct. So, I wanted to understand that when we talk about security, AWS does not magically make our application secure. Something like you said, the physical one is on their side, but something that we set up is on our side. This is why they wanted to make it really clear. So, yes.

Good. Let's talk about AWS Lambda service, like you said. Is it ideal for each and every task, or would you consider it only for certain kinds of tasks and come up with some other solutions for other cases? I just want to understand the pros and cons of this service.

AWS Lambdas have some limits, limits of time that it runs. If I'm not mistaken, it's 30 seconds if you use Lambdas with API Gateway. So it is not enough for really big, really complex operations. That's why AWS Lambda is mostly preferred for quick, small tasks that need to be done.
Do you prefer any real-life examples? Sure, if you have such, go ahead.

Lambdas are really flexible, and they can be used with multiple other services of AWS. One of them is S3. So you can set up a trigger for Lambda when you publish a file, for example, in S3, and you don't need any server for that. So you don't need a server to listen to or check S3. Lambda in this case is really effective because it will be just triggered when the file is uploaded, and you operate the file.

Okay, multiple questions on AWS. We will move further. So, with regard to many startups, it's not news that they prefer to choose AWS. Sometimes I see some articles that somebody is in great debt because overnight the application went popular, and this AWS executed many times than expected. Other cases like DDoS attacks, etc. What would you do in order to prevent such cases as a developer for AWS services?
Probably we need to use some services for security to prevent those attacks. I don't really remember the name of that service, but AWS itself really has those kinds of services.
Yeah, it's called AWS Shield. Indeed, it's a number of services. And also, do you know about AWS Budgets service?

AWS Budgets is a service to help you plan your budget. You can set up a budget for each service, for example, and set up a value, for example, $10. So if you spend more than $10, you will be getting an email to your...

Okay, let's proceed with another question similar to this topic. AWS services are usually used for microservice applications. Can you tell me what these microservice applications are about, and can you compare them with monolithic applications? Also, provide some pros and cons for each approach.

Yes, I used to work in a microservice project. A microservice is like a smaller version of a monolith, but with a particular purpose, like manipulating users or a subscription service. So, a microservice has a particular domain it's responsible for. From a technical side, it usually has its own database and its own server, so it's isolated from other parts of the application. One of the most important pros of microservices is that different parts of your application are not dependent on each other. If one of them stops working for some reason, other parts of your application can easily continue, which is not usually possible with a monolithic application. However, microservices can sometimes be difficult to maintain because you will have a server for each microservice. If you have 20 microservices, you'll have 20 servers and 20 databases to maintain. So, for maintainability, you'll need specialists.

I usually use YAGNI, which stands for 'You Ain't Gonna Need It'. It's about not writing code for the future, like thinking 'I'll need it next week, so I'll write it right now.' That's usually a bad practice. Also, KISS, 'Keep It Simple, Stupid.' It's about the complexity of the code; you don't need to write difficult code, you need to write simple, understandable code. DRY, 'Don't Repeat Yourself,' means trying to reuse code. These are probably the main ones.

There's a testing pyramid I can start with. It consists of unit tests, integration tests, and end-to-end tests. Unit tests are usually written for a small piece of code, like a simple function or method. They test it without any outside dependencies. We usually mock dependencies if the function has some. Integration tests check how two or more parts of the application work together. The last one, end-to-end tests, is the really difficult one for me. It usually tests real use cases or user paths, for example, user login or user registration, and it calls real endpoints of the backend one by one to pass that user case.

`package-lock.json` is a file that contains a tree of dependencies of our project. We usually need it when we work together with multiple developers, for example. Let's say I installed a new dependency into the project, and when I install it with npm, npm updates the tree in `package-lock.json` also. Without `package-lock.json`, for example, I might have the first patch version, and you might have the ninth patch version, and they might be different in some cases, which may lead to some problems. But `package-lock.json` explicitly sets the version without any carets or tildes, so you install the particular version of the package. If we talk about `npm-shrinkwrap.json`, it is almost the same file, but it is usually used when you write some npm packages yourself, when you publish your package to npm registries. The event loop's heart is its phases. It has six phases and two side phases. When we run our `index.js` file using `node index.js`, Node.js goes through the file and evaluates everything. It first runs the asynchronous operations, it runs the timers if we have any, and so on. If we talk about phases, there are six phases. The first one is timers, responsible for timers like `setTimeout` and `setInterval`. The next one is pending callbacks, which is for any callbacks that are ready to be executed after some operation. The third one is the idle phase, when we wait... oh, I'm really lost in this phase. I would move to the next one. The fourth one is polling, when we expect some I/O operations to come to our event loop. The next one is check, when we run our `setImmediate` callbacks, callbacks that are set with the `setImmediate` function. And the last one is the close phase, when we run the callbacks of any closing operations, like if we have any sockets. For example, sockets usually have an event, like `on close`, where we pass a callback, and that callback is usually executed in this phase.
What about the microtask queue? I forgot to tell about them.

Why sometimes we see this problem in the browser but don't see this problem in Postman, for example?

Yes, CORS is Cross-Origin Sharing or Cross-Origin Resource Sharing, I guess. It is something like a policy that exists only in browsers, as you said, and we don't have it in other tools. What does it do? When you request an endpoint from a backend application, usually, mostly if CORS is enabled on the backend side, the browser makes a pre-flight request. It is sent to the backend firstly before the main request to check if the requesting site can access the data. For example, the backend might accept only certain domains or certain HTTP methods, and so on. So, for example, if the backend configured CORS to accept only domain.com, and if you are requesting the backend from domain2.com, you will not get the data because you are not in the list of the backend's CORS configuration. But why don't we get it in Postman? Because this check, when we send that option, that pre-flight request, it is done by the browser itself. So, it is the browser's responsibility to control this process. That's why we don't have that kind of errors in Postman.

For today's software engineering interview, explain how you'd handle an API that needs to return large amounts of data at once.
First thing I'd do is implement pagination so we don't have to send all the data back at once.
Can you explain exactly how it works?

Pagination is just breaking up the data into multiple chunks and only giving chunks back to the client one at a time as they request them. Google search does this. When you search something, it can't possibly return all the results on the internet, so it only returns one page. If you click the second page, it gets the second page of results.
Okay. But what if I want the users to get all the data at once?

The second thing we could try is compressing the data. This is like when a file is too large to send over email and you make a zip file instead, then send that over. We're doing the same thing but within our server.
Makes sense. What if the compressed file is too big?

We could use a third-party service to deal with it. When somebody makes a request for the data, we'll fetch the data from our database, then send it over to some service like S3, which is made for sharing large files. Once we're done processing, we can send them a URL where they can directly download the file from S3 instead of us having to manage.

Thank you. Hi, Ruchi, how are you doing today?
Hi, Mahesh, I'm doing good. How are you?

Yeah, good, thank you. As you know, Ruchi, this position is mainly for a .NET fullstack Angular developer position, and we are looking for someone who can design, develop, and maintain web applications using technologies like .NET, C#, and Angular. With that, let's go ahead and we'll be covering different .NET technologies first, then we'll move to Angular. As part of .NET, let's discuss ASP.NET Web API, different topics in that section. Can you explain what are the different REST principles we have?

If I talk about REST principles, it's a set of principles which we use to design capable and effective web services. Key principles for REST are cacheability, so responses can be cached to enhance the response. We have a client-server architecture, so client and servers operate independently. We have a uniform interface, which is the way of interacting via HTTP methods: GET, PUT, POST, PATCH, and DELETE. Also, we have versioning, so we can maintain the versioning of the API. We also give user control for filtering and sorting, so filtering and sorting is one of the REST principles. Also, we have something like code on demand, where we can return executable code instead of our responses in JSON or XML. I think these are some of the key principles.
Okay, good. Can you explain this concept of content negotiation that we can achieve using Web API?

Content negotiation, I would say, it's a process where API determines which is the best format for a response based on the client which they wanted to accept. So, API and client can communicate and decide whatever the response they want to accept. For example, we can have a response in JSON, XML, or any other formats. We can set it up in content type as a header so that then we get the response.
And coming to the security part, can you explain how you are securing your API endpoints?

In my application, for API endpoints, we have used authentication and authorization. We have used JWT tokens. For internally communicating the APIs, we didn't opt for JWT tokens; we went for API key authentications. We also have CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) that restricts access to the API, which prevents unauthorized access. In our project, we have also done rate limiting and throttling so that we can limit and prevent the abuse of requests per user or API.
Okay, so talking about CORS that you mentioned, can you elaborate more on that?

As I mentioned, it restricts domain access to the API to prevent unauthorized access. We can enable it using the `AddCors` method in .NET Core. There, we can add a policy with origin, so that way we can implement it.
And can you explain the error handling mechanism that you implemented for your API?

For error handling, there is `try-catch` which we use for critical sections. We also have global exception handling; we have used custom middleware for it. We have problem details which are used for consistent responses in .NET Core. That way, I think we have handled it. Also, for monitoring those errors, we have gone with logging, and in our applications, we have used Serilog.
Okay, fine. Moving on to C#, can you explain about generics and why we use generics with an example?

Generics allow us to create reusable code without specifying a data type at compile time. It also helps us in avoiding unnecessary boxing and unboxing. Why do we generally go for generics? It's for type safety prevention during runtime, and it helps us with performance issues; it improves performance. If you ask me for an example, let's say I have a method `Print` that accepts multiple data types. I can create a generic method for it using angle brackets and type `T`. So, if I have a method `Print` and I pass some value as `T value`, now if I call `Print(100)`, it will print an output as an integer `100`. If I pass `Print("hello")`, that is a string type, so it will give me the output "hello". So, we can use multiple data types.
And can you elaborate on what is runtime polymorphism and its applications?

For runtime polymorphism, I would say it is also known as method overriding. It's a fundamental concept used in object-oriented programming. Here, it allows a class to provide a specific implementation of a method that is already defined in the base class. Let's say we have a base class as `Animal`, and there we have a virtual method as `Speak`. Now, if I have a child class, let's say `Dog`, and it inherits the base class `Animal`, we can override the method using the `override` keyword, which is `Speak`, and `Dog` barks. So, we can just write the implementation of that method.
And how can we clean unmanaged resources in C#?

If I speak about unmanaged resources, those are resources which are not managed by .NET itself. These are something like if we are handling things related to files or database connections, those occupy unmanaged memory. To clean up those memory, we have to implement the `IDisposable` interface because the GC does not automatically clean up those resources. So, we implement those interfaces and use the `Dispose` method to release the resources explicitly. Also, sometimes what happens is a developer forgets to call the `Dispose` method, so we go for the destructor, which acts as a safety net to clean up those unmanaged resources before the object is garbage collected.
Did you use TPL (Task Parallel Library) in your project?
Yes, in several places I've used it.
Okay, so can you explain what are the different methods you have used in TPL and their uses?

Mainly in TPL, what I've used is `Task.Run`, which runs the code on a separate thread. I have used `Task.WhenAll`, which is generally used to run multiple tasks concurrently. We have also used `Task.WhenAny`, which waits for the first task to complete and then resumes the next task. Other than that, I think we have also used `ContinueWith`, but I'm not too familiar with it, but yeah, it is basically used for chaining tasks.
All right, and how about `TaskCompletionSource`? Any idea on that?

Yes, I've heard about `TaskCompletionSource`. I've seen those methods like `SetResult`, `SetException`, and `SetCanceled` methods which are used in our code to manually manage task completion. Other than that, I think I've not worked on it explicitly that much.
All right, okay. And I hope you're using .NET Core framework. Okay, can you explain where we can use custom middleware and how we can build a custom middleware?

Custom middleware comes into the picture if we want to customize any logic. Let's say I want to customize the logic for authentication and authorization pipeline, so I can go ahead with custom middleware. Or, if I want to change something in the request, modify the request and response data, there I can go ahead. Or, like I mentioned earlier, if we want to handle exceptions centrally, there we can create custom middleware.
So how do we create it? How to build a new custom middleware?

If I want to create the custom middleware, I can add the `RequestDelegate` parameter in the constructor. I can register that custom middleware in the `Program.cs` class, or maybe if we are using .NET Core 3.1, there we can go for the `Startup.cs` class. Here, we have an `InvokeAsync` method where we can write the logic for the custom middleware.
Okay, fine. And how to implement model validation?
Model validations I have implemented using data annotations, like `Required` and those data annotations I've used in the model.
Okay. And what are the different options we have to store environment-specific information in your .NET application, and which one have you used?

For environment-specific information, in our project, we are using Azure Key Vault, which is a highly secure way. We have also used `appsettings.json` to store connection strings sometimes, or some key-value pairs which we wanted to store. In one of our projects, we have also used environment variables; it was a Windows application, so there we used environment variables, but we kept in mind that we are not going to commit it in our source control.
Okay, so you're using it in Azure and securing it, is it?
Mainly we have used Key Vault.
Okay, so which ORMs have you worked on? Object-relational mappers?
I worked on Entity Framework. We already had an existing database schema, so we opted for a database-first approach.
So can you explain more on how to implement this DB-first approach?

For implementation of the DB-first approach, we need to install EF Core packages. After installing those packages, we need to generate the models from the database which is already there. We configure the DB context class and then we register those DB contexts in our dependency injection and use those contexts to perform CRUD operations.
And what is this Entity Data Model and what does it contain?

If I speak about Entity Data Model, it's a conceptual model. It's a kind of an abstraction layer between the database and the application code, where a developer can interact with the data using those objects. Because earlier we used to use raw SQL queries, now with the help of EDM, we use objects.
Okay, and what are the different entity states we have?
I'll just recall it, but we have `Detached`, which is the state where EF is not tracking the entity. We have `Unchanged`, `Added`, `Modified`, and `Deleted`, I think.
Okay, so where do we use these states? Where are these useful?

These states are basically used when we are adding some data, or when we are modifying the entities. So, there we go for these states, like whether the object is created manually, or they are being added in the DB context, or the data has been fetched from the database or not. These are some use cases.
And you mentioned something about tracking, right? So, what are these tracking and no-tracking mechanisms we have, and where to use them?

Tracking and no-tracking are approaches where we track the changes for the entities which are managed by DB context. Tracking is monitoring the entities which are tracked by Entity Framework; it keeps the original values and the current values of the entities. Whereas, we have no-tracking, which does not track the entities that are retrieved. Mainly, we go for no-tracking where we have performance, specifically for read values, not for write values. So, we go for no-tracking. No-tracking highly improves performance and reduces memory consumption.
All right. And talking about performance, can you explain how to identify performance bottlenecks in an application which is using EF and how to fix them?

If we want to identify performance, we have Entity Framework Profiler, where we can detect slow queries. Those are highlighted, like if there is any query which is slow, those are highlighted in red. It also detects the N+1 query problems where multiple queries are being fetched related to the data instead of a single optimized query. It also identifies redundant queries, like the same queries being executed multiple times, so instead, we can consider caching techniques there. Also, I think it detects missing indexes if there are any indexes which are being missed there, so those are also being detected by EF Profiler.
Okay, got you. And going to SQL Server, you just mentioned about indexes. So, can you explain what are the pros and cons of using indexes?

Pros and cons of using indexes: if you are using indexes, queries become very fast in terms of fetching the data. If you want to sort or filter, it gives us effective sorting and filtering of data. Cons, I would say, it is a very high storage cost in terms of whenever we are storing the data; there are multiple indexes, so it would be high storage cost. And slower insert, update, and delete, whereas select queries are very fast if you're using indexes. Too many indexes can hurt the performance, so that is one.
Any reason why DML operations get slower with indexes?

Whenever we are inserting any index in our table, what happens is data is being stored in a sorted format if there is any index added in a column. So, those are being stored in a sorted format, that is why they are slow.
Got you. Did you work on creating stored procedures?
Yes.
Okay, so can you explain some best practices that you followed for writing efficient stored procedures?

Whenever I used to write stored procedures, I make sure that I am not using `SELECT *`. I avoid using `SELECT *` and I go for specifying the columns which are being used in the stored procedures. As we spoke about indexes, I opt for proper indexes in the tables. We have `IN` and we have `EXISTS`, so I go for `EXISTS` instead of `IN` in the `WHERE` condition for better performance. Also, I have mostly avoided cursors because those are very slow. At the start of the stored procedure, we use `SET NOCOUNT ON`. For temporary tables, instead of creating temporary tables, I generally go for temporary variables.
So what does `SET NOCOUNT ON` do?

If I'm executing the stored procedure, there are multiple insert statements or select statements, so we generally used to get how many rows are being affected in the output window. If we use `SET NOCOUNT ON`, we don't get those values, those number of counts.
Can you explain about execution plan and where we can use it?

Execution plan we generally go for if our queries or, let's say, our stored procedures are very slow, and we wanted to see why these stored procedures are slow, or we want to check how the joins and indexes are being used in our table, and what is the actual cost of operations and what are the bottlenecks. There we go for the execution plan. With the help of the execution plan, we can opt for the optimization of the SQL queries.
And what are the different isolation levels in SQL?

I do not have much information on it, but I've seen we have `READ UNCOMMITTED`, and we have `READ COMMITTED`, and I think `REPEATABLE READ`, `SNAPSHOT` or something, but I do not have much information on this.
And how about unit testing? Have you implemented unit tests for your applications?
Yes, so I have implemented unit testing in my previous project, but here I've not implemented, but yeah, I have an idea about it.
Okay, so which libraries or frameworks have you used?
I have used NUnit in my previous project.
Okay, so can you explain the role of unit tests in the CI/CD pipeline, how effective are they, and what is their importance?

Whenever we are writing unit test cases and we have a CI/CD pipeline, those unit test cases are automatically added and they run automatically. If we have existing unit test cases and if they fail, let's say I committed my changes and my build is run, now my build is failing because I have broken down some unit test cases. That helps us in initially checking up on the code which we have broken, rather than it went to the server or maybe sometimes I have seen the code has gone to production but the existing functionality has been broken. So that way it helps.
Okay, so you'll stop the build there and there itself?
Yeah, we can fix those changes.
Okay, and when we write these unit tests, we follow these principles, right? We call them as AAA principles. Any idea what they are?

AAA principle is like we have Arrange, Act, and Assert. So, we follow those principles to create a clear structure in our unit test cases. Arrange sets up the initial condition for the test. Act executes the method which we are testing. And Assert, those assertion methods, verifies that the behavior is as per the expectation or not. So, there we go for the AAA pattern.
Can you explain about the test pyramid?
We have a test pyramid where we have unit testing at the bottom, then we have integration testing as a middle layer, and we have end-to-end testing as a top layer. So that makes a pyramid.
Right, so what is the importance of unit testing here? Why is it placed at the base? Any reason?

As I mentioned, if we are implementing unit testing in the CI/CD pipeline as well, early detection of issues is important. That is why we kept it at the bottom layer, so that if there are any changes we broke, existing changes, those are detected early.
Okay, can you explain Dependency Inversion Principle?

Dependency Inversion Principle, I would say, it is DIP. It's one of the principles used in SOLID principles. It states that high-level modules should not depend on low-level modules, but they should depend on abstraction, and abstraction should not depend on details, but details should depend on abstraction. What it says is like we should not depend directly on the creation of objects of classes, but instead we should go for maybe interfaces or abstract classes. Like we used to create a class, we used to create objects, and then we used to call those methods. So instead, we can go for the interfaces and then call those methods and do the needful. We have dependency injection also, which helps in achieving DIP.
And we have these code smells, right? What is a code smell and give an example?

A code smell is, if I say, it's a kind of bad practices or poor coding practices which we follow in our project. It generally does not cause any bugs in a project, but I feel over time it leads to some issues or maybe technical debt. For example, I have seen people create methods that are very long, which are very hard to read and understand, and it's difficult to debug them as well. I've seen long classes which violate the Single Responsibility Principle because they're not following the rule, and those become very difficult to understand. There is duplicated code which causes code smells. Also, I've seen whenever some new developer, maybe a junior developer, comes into a project, they use hardcoded values, they use hardcoded numbers which doesn't make any sense if another person is reading that. So, we should avoid those hardcoded values; instead, we can go for constants and we can have a meaningful name for them. That way, I think we can help.
Got you. And also, you said you have been doing code reviews, right? So, how do you find out different code smells that are present in the source code given to you?

We have static analysis tools like SonarQube and ReSharper, where we can find out about the code smells. Also, sometimes when we are reviewing, like I mentioned, if there are hardcoded numbers, if let's say the tool has not fetched those details, but if we are looking and we are comparing that old code, previous code, to the committed code which the developer wants to commit, comparing those codes we can find out these are hardcoded values and we can tell them about it. Also, to avoid long methods, we have something like in Visual Studio, we can have code metrics where we can fetch cyclomatic complexity. If cyclomatic complexity is too high for a method, that means it's a very long method, it contains a lot of `if`s, a lot of loops, and that is why the complexity is high, and it would be very difficult for us to maintain in the future. Yeah, I think that is the way we do it.
And can you explain what are design patterns?

It is a kind of a reusable solution for any software design problems. It does not provide us ready-to-use code, but it gives us the framework which helps in solving any type of problem. We have multiple design patterns which are segregated as creational, structural, and behavioral design patterns. I think these are design patterns.
Okay, so can you mention any specific patterns which you have used?
In my project, I have used Singleton design pattern, and I have used Repository design pattern, and I have also used CQRS and Factory design pattern.
Can you explain any of these, like a scenario you have used, how they are useful?

If I mention, as I spoke, I've used Repository design pattern. It's a structural pattern which provides an abstraction layer over the data access layer. It aims to separate the logic that retrieves data from the business layer. So, we create repository interfaces which basically have the CRUD methods to perform create, read, update, and delete operations. And we have repository implementations where we implement those repository interfaces and perform the data operations.
Okay, so talking about the architectural part, can you explain your current project architecture, Ruchi?

Currently, I'm working with Agile Room, and there, as I mentioned, I'm working on the modernization part. Earlier, the project was related to a document management system, so there we were following the legacy architecture, which is SOA (Service-Oriented Architecture). For communication, we were using WCF and SOAP services. Because of the WCF and SOAP services, we were having large payloads in the response, which led to performance issues, and our services were very highly tightly coupled, so we were not able to scale our application. That is why we opted for a modernization approach. In modernization, what we are doing is we are creating a microservice-based architecture where we are breaking down those WCF services into lightweight RESTful services. Earlier, we had a single service which was handling the entire project; now we have created multiple microservices which handle specific work. For the communication part, we are using REST APIs. For the database, we are using SQL Server database, and for authentication and authorization, we are using OAuth and JWT.
Okay, so you said you have used microservices, right? Can you explain the differences between monolithic application and microservices, where we use each of them, like pros and cons?

If you want to make our application highly scalable, because in microservices we have independent services, we go for microservices. If there are independent services, we can go for a different tech stack as well, so it makes our application more flexible. Also, if you want to go for phased deployment, we go for microservices because if, let's say, I have Service 1 and Service 2, and I want to deploy some updates in Service 1, I need not stop the entire system; Service 2 will remain unaffected, and Service 1 can be deployed easily. So there we go for microservices. For the failure thing also, I would say the entire application is not affected, so there also we go for microservices. In terms of monolithic, scalability is an issue, that is one of the cons for a monolithic. Code is very tightly coupled, that is another con for monolithic, and slower deployment, so there we don't go for monolithic. But if our application is not too big and we want just a single codebase, for easier development and deployment, we go for monolithic. If I speak about debugging and monitoring, if there are any logs being added, monolithic is more preferred because in microservices it becomes very difficult to look for the entire services where the issue has happened. So that way, we choose between monolithic and microservices.
What is the software development process you have been following?
We are following Agile.
Okay, so can you explain what is the difference between Definition of Ready and Definition of Done?

Definition of Ready, we generally use DoR. It's a set of criteria that must be met before the work item is considered ready to be worked on. Like if somebody is assigned a task, those set of criteria should be well written, like we have acceptance criteria, so those should be there. We have gone through the grooming of the user stories; we have already groomed that user story. People understand the user story well and they do not have any questions related to that user story, and we have estimated that user story. So, that time we say that this user story is Definition of Ready. And we have Definition of Done, so it is a set of criteria that defines that the work has been done and it is ready for maybe release. All the necessary standards have been fulfilled, all acceptance criteria have been done, testing from the QA is done, and it is ready for production. If you want to give some demo for the user story, that is also done. So, that time we say that user story is done, and that is considered as Definition of Done.
And what are the different estimation techniques you used?

Currently in my project, we are using sometimes story points and sometimes planning poker. So, we have Fibonacci series where we opt for the numbers based on the complexity of the user stories. In my past project, I have seen people use ideal days and hours, like they are going to complete the story in five days or maybe they use hours, like, 'Okay, let's give 40 hours to complete the user story.' That is also what I've seen. And I have seen in my past project we have T-shirt sizing, so we give estimation as extra small, small, medium, large, and extra large. So that way we use.
