Okay, got you. So in Angular, we have these different compilation techniques, right? So can you explain what they are and what are the differences between those two?

Compilation, we have JIT and AOT. We have Just-in-Time compilation approach and we have Ahead-of-Time compilation approach. Generally, whenever compilation happens in development mode, we opt for JIT because it is very fast. It allows fast changing and it allows hot reloading. But it gives us slower initial load, and it gives us full debugging support, so we debug the entire code and fix those changes in our code. However, if we go for the Ahead-of-Time approach, we generally go for AOT approach in production. It compiles the code at build time and then sends it to the browser. It is more secure because it compiles the code into JavaScript and it reduces runtime injection attacks there. Initial loads are faster when we use AOT. Earlier in JIT, it is very slow because in AOT, the templates are pre-compiled and the browser loads the app very quickly. Also, if we are using AOT, the bundle size is very small, so it removes unused Angular features and it optimizes our code to JavaScript files.
And can you explain how change detection works in Angular?

In Angular, change detection is a mechanism which ensures that the UI stays in sync with the application data model. If there are any application state changes, let's say somebody, a user, has given some input, or there is some HTTP request, or some event triggers, that time Angular reflects the updates on the DOM. Those changes are being detected. We have two flows: there is one change detection strategy which is the default strategy, which runs for every event that happens and it detects the changes there for each and every event. For default, Angular traverses the entire component tree to check for changes. However, for another approach, which is `OnPush`, if we enable `OnPush` approach, it runs the change detection only when input properties are changed or if an observable emits new data. So that time only it runs the change detection. So we need not check the entire components, and it helps in improving the performance.
And suppose you have been tasked with optimizing the performance of an existing Angular application, Ruchi. What steps would you take to identify and address performance issues?
The performance-related issues, like I said, I've not worked much with Angular. So I think I might give you the wrong answer for it.
Okay, fine. And can you explain the purpose and use cases of using RxJS?

We generally go for RxJS when we have any asynchronous data streams. To handle those asynchronous data streams, we go for reactive programming. We go for RxJS, like if there is any HTTP request, to handle those HTTP requests, we use observables whenever we call for an API. There we go for RxJS. We have state management also; for state management, I think NgRx is used for state management, so we go for that. Maybe I think for tracking real-time data also, we have `Subject` and `BehaviorSubject`, so we go for that.
What are the different tools or frameworks used for unit testing in Angular?
In Angular, we have used Jasmine for unit testing, and mainly Jasmine is being used in our project.
Okay, fine, got it. Okay, Ruchi, I think that's it from my side. I pretty much covered all the topics required. Thank you for your answers.
Thank you so much, Mahesh.

You are an expert Senior Software Engineer (System Design) at a top-tier tech company conducting a technical interview. Your objective is to assess the candidate's technical depth, problem-solving skills, and real-world experience. Keep probing with follow-up questions to fully gauge the candidate's depth on the current topic. When a topic has been sufficiently explored, transition naturally to a new topic and continue probing. Acknowledge the candidate's responses naturally before asking your next question. Continue the interview until the candidate or the session concludes. Keep your tone conversational, professional, and human. Do not break character.
Okay, well Dima, the question I wanted to ask you today is: how would you design Uber?

All righty, well we start from certain requirements to make sure we're solving the same problem. Usually in an interview there's a section called FRs and NFRs — functional, non-functional requirements. Personally I don't necessarily like these terms that much but they do help. So let's talk about what we need to solve for. Let's first not forget that Uber is a two-sided marketplace so we have riders — basically if I'm a rider I am at point A, or I plan to be at point A right now, sometime soon, maybe in the future, and what I do is I use the app to call a car, call a taxi, call an Uber, to get from point A to point B now or later. There also are the drivers which is a totally different part of a conversation because Uber again is a two-sided marketplace. So — let's put aside the conversation about contractors versus full-time employees — basically we're going to not touch regulation. We got a bunch of people who have signed up for the platform so that they can be given rides. I assume they pass all the background checks and they are allowed to do so and they also have the app open. They do drive people from A to B. And so to me the most interesting part of Uber is how to connect these two because solving for only riders or only drivers is not as fun as doing the whole thing, as closing the loop. But for the sake of completeness let's talk about what the riders can do. Well, book a ride from a GPS of course so that I can book the ride from where I am right now or from a given point, provided point. I can set custom time, I can change the ride midway, I can cancel it before or midway. There are tons of issues to worry about when it comes to like if it's an airport transfer, pickup certain pickup points, proper terminal locations, if there is a parking lot to enter — that's a big problem itself — I'm not sure we need to go there but I need to put it here. Pick up drop off points — also, to be honest, this would be shared requirements. And also the important thing here is that we have ratings. And when I'm the driver, I start my day, I start my shift, I'm back in business after lunch, and I open the app and I mark myself as available, which is kind of my first requirement — right, out of business on hold. And then what needs to happen is Uber as a company doesn't request rides, other riders do. So what happens when I'm marking myself as available — I'm going to call them bids — the requests to be the request from a particular rider to be taken from point A to point B. I should also add here that we have different car types — X, Black, large, and pets or whatnot, children. And I should also add that the number of people can be different. So as a driver I mark myself available in a particular car — the system knows I'm driving like a Mercedes-Benz E-class which means I can take let's say up to four people.
As a driver, you mean?
Yeah, as a driver, as a driver. Yeah, you need to just change that — just not to confuse the viewers. Yeah, you're absolutely right, so do not confuse the viewers — riders, drivers.

So what happens with — there's a separate conversation behind the scenes that has to do with the price — price creation. We need to talk about this eventually, I don't think it's the right time right now, but there is a price. It's in the shared requirements — price setting engine plus search pricing. Search pricing is a very clever idea: it's that if we have a hot marketplace — if it's Christmas or some large sport event — because we have so much more riders than drivers the price gets higher so that the drivers make extra money and the market works out this thing. But from what I understand, from what I know, Uber by design does not let people bid on prices. Uber sets the price itself. So it's other platforms — other platforms probably let you put the amount as the rider and potential as a driver until the orders match like in the order book — but with Uber it's a fixed price computed by the platform given the current conditions. What's important here is that when I am the driver and I receive the bid — I'm not entirely positive, it's been a while since I've seen this interface from the inside — but I don't think the driver knows a lot about the ride because I think from the product perspective there used to be a failure mode when if you want to go to a certain region where drivers don't want to go to you cannot order a car there. So I think what they would tell you as a driver is: you need to pick up somebody at a certain point — it's going to be on the map on your phone so I know how far it is. Chances are it's going to be sent to me while I'm driving a current passenger so that Uber, as a platform, really wants their drivers to be driving full-time. So when I'm completing the ride my phone will start buzzing me with potential options. And I think — I don't think it's going to tell me where the next potential rider is going — but it's going to tell me if it's a short ride or a long ride. And I also think there's a big important feature which was ultimately introduced and people loved it — drivers loved it — when they can mark themselves as going home, so that — on the one hand you don't want to penalize the riders who go to the area where drivers don't want to go — they would be embarrassing and insane — so the way it works is the platform, Uber, does not tell the driver that this potential customer wants to go to a certain region they don't want to go to. But at the same time when it's like late in my shift and I probably plan to be going home, I can tell the platform that hey I would much rather be going towards that direction. So if I'm living north of town because that's where the airport is I can probably start marking myself as please take me to rides home but not vice versa. So on heading-home mode. Yeah, so — receive pickup location, time, most often now, how long the ride is planned to be, how far, and I guess that's it — how many people, maybe, doesn't matter but not too much.

All right. So now if this makes sense — I think it mostly does so far — let's talk about what we're actually optimizing for. Non-functional requirements — my goal, the objective, is to keep the drivers busy. I think there would also be a rating. My goal is to get the driver utilization — that's the number one priority. From what I understand about the ride-share business it's always the provider of the service, the drivers, who are in short supply. So whatever I can do algorithmically, design-wise, financially — whatever I can do to keep them more engaged, especially when it's most needed by the riders, I should be doing that. In terms of system design from the algorithm standpoint, the most interesting part I think would be to improve the algorithms of suggesting which drivers get routed to which riders because that's my core algorithm. So I'm thinking out loud here — how to approach the problem of logistics. So let's focus on the matching problem — rider-driver. Full disclosure, I have no idea how it works. I'm going to be making guesses which is probably what you guys should be doing in an interview. So let's start to postulate it from first principles. When I don't know what to say I will start saying the most obvious things and go with the flow. The most obvious thing is that — imagine I'm the rider, I'm taking my phone, I'm going to call them up, I expect someone nearby who is either idle or is about to complete their trip — I'm expecting them to be notified of my request, of my ride request. In practice I'm almost positive the way it's going to work internally is that my ride request is not sent to all the drivers in the area at the same time. I don't really want my drivers to feel like they had to compete for the ride — from the product standpoint, from the UX standpoint, if I'm the driver who wants to make money, I don't want to be distracted and frantically typing yes yes accept the ride. Once I'm about to drop off this particular passenger and I'm looking for the next one — in fact, now that I think about it, one obvious thing would be maybe a lot of drivers would actually enter this automatically-accept-rides mode. I'm not sure if Uber works this way, it might even be against certain regulations, but if I'm the assistant designer I would argue — let's extend this: also consider some automatically accept rides until let's say T plus 2 hours, 5 minutes. So obviously if I'm the driver, I have a good rating, I have a good car, everything fits — the platform, if the platform knows my default is to accept the ride — it's like with Airbnb if you have instant book. If I know that the rider has requested a ride and there is a driver available in their vicinity who's idle or about to be idle soon and who has this instant book feature on, I will just send this rider this request and assume that the request is taken.

I think UX-wise I would say I would do it like this. So we're going to be talking about the algorithm plus UX and then we'll start drawing stuff. So from the way I usually do it, I would say like — life of a ride: what happens to the ride as it progresses through the system. First of all it's requested by rider, then the system starts looking for a driver. So the main idea here is that I don't really want to be sending the same ride to multiple drivers currently simultaneously because I don't want them to be distracted. So even if let's say there are no instantly bookable drivers and there are five drivers around me, I would still not want to send a request to all of them simultaneously. Because as a driver once I'm seeing the invitation to accept the ride, I want this driver to have 5 to 10 seconds to accept this ride and they don't need to compete with each other. So like if I'm the driver, I'm dropping you off and someone else is about to take a ride from near where I am and I'm seeing the notification — hey there's a person next to me who needs a ride — as a driver I would like to design the UX and the flow so that once as a driver I see this notification I have the next, let's say, 7 seconds to decide if I accept this ride or no. For the next 7 seconds once it was sent to me, this request is potentially mine — it's not being routed to any other driver. So either I accept it within let's say 7 seconds or I don't, in which case the system finds the next potential driver. So let's say we're going to have a two-tier process: if instant book is not available, pick a driver nearby using AI for selection, send them the request, wait — I don't know — 7 seconds, go find driver. If a driver who accepts it is found, go to pickup mode. So imagine there are no instantly bookable drivers yet — I pick a driver, I send in this request, wait for 7 seconds, if no confirmation within this 7 seconds I'm going back to here in which case I'm looking for the next driver. And instant book gets priority — if an instant book driver is available nearby I would say same thing but I give them like four seconds. Up to seven if no explicit rejection. Is this idea clear? I just made this up, it could be totally off, but my point is that if you have people who are in this hot standby mode and they've committed to accept every ride, I give them like four seconds to say no. And if those guys aren't available I book a person who is not auto-confirming and I give them 7 seconds to confirm.

Now we get to the pickup mode. So to be honest in terms of system design our job is mostly done here. Pickup is pretty much Google Maps navigation or Waze or Apple Maps — I don't think we need to design this, we can use a third party app. Of course if we're a company such as Uber we have our own mapping technology and driving-specific serving capabilities. What I think we should mention here explicitly is the location is shared in real time — moreover maybe tweaked to allow for traffic lights, curbside pickup details, etc. Because I can totally imagine the first version of Uber to be using some standard Google Maps application and then I can totally imagine some standard failure modes when let's say a tiny road is one way and you probably want to be picking up the rider like 10 meters to the north because that's where it's easy to pick up this person. So it's a separate conversation, I don't think we should go there, it's a bit too low level. But fundamentally in the pickup mode, until the match has been confirmed, until we know the GPS coordinates of both phones and until we know that the driver has started the ride, we're still on the pickup mode. Pickup mode can still cancel from either side, might be for a fee. In this case if it's cancelled go back to the starting point. Once approved, once the ride is confirmed, go to in-ride. Note: confirmed includes but is not limited to GPS, manual confirmation by the driver, perhaps a code — I know there's a feature when in certain locations, in certain jurisdictions, you don't really trust people to trust each other so the platform needs to confirm that it's actually the correct rider got into the correct car. And the way Uber solves for this is they have a code like a four-digit code or QR code so the rider needs to share this with the driver and only once that is confirmed the ride can start.
I think we've covered enough there. Shall we get to drawing?

Let's start designing. Yeah. I'm going to start from the rider which is the user because I think it's where the flow mostly — it's where the most intense flow happens. All right, system design diagrams and boxes. There are many ways to approach this. The first high level question to ask is: are we are we using any cloud provider like Amazon AWS, Google Cloud, Microsoft Azure, something else? Or are we totally bare metal like our own hardware? A good design is of course agnostic to this but it's worth mentioning. So the first box inside this cloud is a load balancer. A lot of people miss what load balancers actually do. So it's a cluster which is traditionally denoted by having a couple of them — LB cluster. The way it works is my request hits one of the instances of this load balancer. In the case of Uber it's not that important because extra 2 to 300 millisecond latency extra like 3 seconds would not make things very different — but realistically what you want to do is you want the request from the user to enter your private network as soon as possible. So load balancers are not only used to shard your backend servers so that you can add more of them — they also serve to make sure the request from the device enters my system as soon as possible and whatever network calls happen behind the scenes they happen with my own network which is controlled by me, which is low latency, high throughput, far more reliable. That's what the load balancer does here. Then it would fundamentally — there's a big thing to say here about geo-sharding. I'll get there in a moment. So fundamentally I would say hypothetically this could literally be, you know, api.uber.com — hypothetically that's the resource where the request comes to. If it's HTTP it is of course HTTPS to be safe and secure to be not interceptable at least not easily interceptable. Now behind the scenes internally I would argue, for many reasons — load balancing and regulation — different cities have their own specific sets of servers to serve the cities. So it's like a per-location Uber server. What I'm doing here is I'm starting to get some work of routing the traffic done on the load balancer level. Because once my phone talks to the load balancer, the phone knows where I am — I assume there are some GPS coordinates. So if I'm in Austin it knows I'm in Austin, if I'm in Singapore it knows I'm in Singapore. So the request of the load balancer — even even if it hits the same high level top level endpoint — there's enough metadata for the load balancer to already decide where to route it. I'm drawing multiple of these because they also shed — they know that the request would go to let's say New York, and let's say there is something else which is San Francisco — why not — let's say it's Berlin because we're international. So the load balancer at the moment it needs to make a decision where to pass this request to, it can decide which Uber location it goes to. And there could be like in Europe there could be GDPR rules — so for example per-location data for Berlin cannot leave Germany. So far the only thing I'm saying is that the moment my request, my tab, gets to the actual backend service here, it's already tied to a particular location.

Now this is a tricky part. When people draw multiple boxes like I did here it means a couple things. One thing it means is that if one of them dies the system is still up and running — that's called high availability, that's called resiliency, that's called durability. Well, durability is mostly about not losing the data but still we need multiple of these so that one of them can die. And then once we commit to having multiple nodes, it's a very delicate conversation how to proceed here because in terms of the system design architecture logically we can treat this as a single box and then decide how to best make sure it runs in a way that is highly available so nodes can die but it's still functional and durable so that once nodes die they don't lose any data. So let's pretend for a moment logically that the whole Berlin is served by a single instance of an Uber server for Berlin.

As a high-profile engineer I would actually go as far as saying that it's not impossible because the total amount of traffic to this server — when it comes to — okay this deserves drawing — you have the so-called write path which are the mutations, state changes, and you have the read path that's immutable requests. For example when I'm just opening the app I literally want to see how many cars are there available — I don't need to go to the write path, I can go to the read path. And read path is super easy to scale because that's where strong versus eventual consistency comes into play. Read path can be eventually consistent. If I literally open the Uber app to see how many cars are there around me, if the data I'm seeing is 3, 5 seconds behind — heck even 30 seconds behind — the product still doesn't suck, it still fulfills all the requirements. So the truth is that this is super easy to scale because it can be eventually consistent. Whereas on the write path — if both of us open the app in the same location and we see slightly different pictures, that's okay. But on the write path, if both of us order a ride and we get to different servers here, and you get to server A and I get to server B, and somehow these servers are not in line with what's going on, and the same driver accepts a ride from both of us — that's a disaster, that's a catastrophe, that's what should be prevented. So logically the write path is where this single source of truth — singular, like a mutex, so to speak — synchronized section, that's where it happens. So I'm going to put it here and say: should be synchronized single source of truth. And so one of the most difficult problems of system design is how to build synchronized source of truth systems that are distributed. But logically for now we can pretend there's a single machine. And again as a high-performance engineer I know it's possible — if you take all the read path stuff, if you pretend there is enough machines that are only eventually consistent, they can be behind the world time — they might be like 5, 10, 15 seconds behind the actual state of the fleet — we ignore them for now. For the write path I'm going to claim that the total number of requests for new rides per second within the whole city of Berlin is going to be quite small. If you look at all the state transitions — pickup mode, find the driver mode, then we get the in-ride mode etc etc, then you have to confirm the ride and you may like leave a tip — if you look at all the state transitions that needs to be correctly synchronized with one another, the invariants such as no driver can accept two riders at the same time — I'm going to claim that for a single city of Berlin the number of mutating queries per second is actually quite small. It is certainly well under 1,000. I just cannot possibly imagine that on the write path in terms of synchronizing drivers and riders you order more than 1,000 rides per second. So that's actually something one machine can handle.

In practice there will be more complexities here. Let me try to explain. In practice — so the read path is a picture of all the cars available, so to speak. The write path is the rider to driver matching state machine synchronization. We also have something in the middle which is synchronizing specific data — the data specific to a particular ride. I believe Uber has this feature — share my ride — so I can as a rider request a ride, get matched to a driver, get into the car, on a ride to the airport, and I want my wife or my peers to not be worried — so I can send them a link through the Uber app so that they can watch in real time where my location is. Of course internally even if you don't share your ride, Uber backend services absolutely do know where every driver-rider pair is. The point is — this potential component sees a lot more traffic. The state changes such as ride requested, ride cancelled, ride started, ride ended — for these I believe the traffic would be quite small for the whole city, in New York it's likely under three digits, maybe low four digits. For the data specific to individual rides, the QPS is far larger because you can imagine that let's say every five or ten seconds the driver updates their position, their coordinates, connecting to the server in real time. So the traffic here — this is the most important part — this is where we need to maintain the invariant that the same driver cannot pick up two riders. This here — the traffic is manageable by even a single machine. Here — location beacons — we have a lot more traffic. Even if it's every 5 seconds there's a beacon from every single driver currently driving for Uber or waiting to pick up someone, and for every single rider who is at least waiting for the ride or in the ride — that can easily be thousands of this driving life, so you can easily get tens of thousands of requests per second here. But the trick is for this part you cannot potentially have data from one ride colliding with data from another ride. There's no conflict, there's no room for conflicts, you can't break your logical invariants here but not here. So this basically what I'm drawing right now would be logically speaking a different sub-component of this per-location Uber server.

So this is a fleet of servers. I would argue that in practice for the read path you can add as many machines as you want — they just subscribe to the feed of internal updates. It's like a CDN more or less. To get a snapshot of which cars are currently available in Berlin, you get to one of many of these nodes and you get the data that is somewhat accurate. You just scale this as necessary — I would argue there could be dozens of them, maybe 5 to 10 for Berlin, maybe 15 for New York, maybe 50 or 100 if you don't write your code correctly. For these location beacon nodes you need a large number of them too. But each — these nodes are all identical in logic, they're all interchangeable, they're all fungible, if one of them dies you just replace it by a new one — they all serve the same purpose and have the same data. If one node dies what happens is you shard the traffic from your riders, drivers and active rides so that a particular data point in a particular time goes to a particular server. You can use consistent hashing to send it to two or three machines just to make sure if one dies they're not lost. But honestly that's not important because if a machine dies, well, what's the worst that can happen — for the next half a minute you lose track of the keep-alive messages of where the driver is. No big deal on a company scale. For the write path matching, if you're a startup that just needs to launch somehow, I would sincerely suggest that we just have a single machine for the whole Berlin that handles this. If it dies, until it restarts or until a new machine starts on a backup, for a couple seconds no new rides can be requested, no new rides can be confirmed or cancelled or whatnot. But in practice if you are the big Uber itself you don't do it on a single machine — that's too dangerous. You have a few of them. The standard technique is to use data replications — have an odd number of machines, traditionally would be three or five, so that they always are in touch with each other, they always know which one is the main one, who's whose word matters most in a democratic system, so that if one of these machines dies — if it's not the leader that's okay, it's replaced and kept up to date. If the leader dies, within a split second another leader is elected and they guarantee that all the invariants we need to respect are being respected. That's my high level diagram.
Yeah, that's interesting. I wanted to ask you: what metrics are you going to be tracking here? Like, how are you going to know if the system is behaving as it should do?

Oh, that's a big question — there are tons of directions, tons of ways to postulate the question actually. So like, bottom up, as an engineer I want to make sure that my system is up and running. So I would look at the traffic numbers everywhere — traffic numbers here, traffic numbers to every single location, traffic numbers from within internal systems to all these components, all these three. If any of them drop something is wrong — that's something I can totally monitor myself. As a higher level system designer I would also argue we need to track user-facing metrics. I want to know how many rides are currently taking place. I would imagine — imagine Berlin has an Uber office, I don't think it's the case but it doesn't matter — I would imagine there's going to be a large screen to monitor real-time metrics with tons of charts. It will be like active real-time number of rides happening right now. There will be like this daily sine wave because there is more at night than in the afternoon and if suddenly it goes below what you would expect or above what you would expect this should trigger an alarm, an alert, somebody should look into this. I would look into the length of the rides if the traffic pattern has changed. I would look into the ratings of drivers and riders — we clearly want to not deteriorate in performance for the end users. I would absolutely look at more fine-grained metrics such as like average distance from the rider to a driver, average pickup time. Because in practice there's going to be tons of A/B testing behind the scenes. If my engineering team has designed a new matching algorithm that is presumably making pickups faster, chances are it's being A/B tested for parts of drivers and I want to see how it performs, how it behaves so they can make decisions on how to roll it out and when. So yeah tons of cool stuff.

Thank you for a good question — thanks for the answer. Also, just before we wind this up, are there any other kind of features that you think might be interesting to add and how would they affect your design?

Yeah, let me reflect. Let me look back at this as I slowly exit the mode of someone who was interviewed and I enter the mode of the hiring committee member. I think I've tried to give your listeners the best possible experience of seeing how the real interview looks like in practice. If I'm being interviewed like this I would absolutely ask what are we designing for real because nobody designs Ubers these days from scratch. Either it's a smaller startup so that it's not a replica of Uber but rather some more targeted niche product — I don't know, maybe it's a rideshare pickup, maybe they are allowed to use pedestrian roads, maybe they have drones, maybe they have extra regulations on what business advantage they have compared to Uber itself — in which case the design should absolutely be focused on this particular tailored mode. On the other hand if you really need to design Uber, well, I can guarantee you as someone who's in the industry for a long time, this is not how Uber looks like on a high level. Yes. But as we all know from Twitter and Elon Musk, high level whiteboard designs have little to do with what systems look like behind the scenes. So for the interview conversation I think that's approximately what I would imagine candidates should draw when asked this question. But if I'm interviewing — given I'm a couple levels above the senior staff level — I will probably be having a more product-first conversation. I think I managed to strike the balance and actually talk about consistency, about data flows, about databases somehow. But yeah, it's a really big can of worms. And if I can leave you with one thing here — you got to read your interview. You got to figure out what this particular company is looking for. Uber in particular is well known for looking for product-first engineers. If you actually talk to your interviewer about hey I need to optimize like curbside pickup, I need to suggest pickup spots because that's where riders are more happy to be picked up compared to other pickup spots where they complain like they were honked at — this is super useful, this gives you tons of bonus points for Uber. For other companies do your homework. But yeah tons of ways to go from here.

You are an expert Senior Software Engineer at a top-tier tech company conducting a technical interview. Your objective is to assess the candidate's technical depth, problem-solving skills, and real-world experience. Keep probing with follow-up questions to fully gauge the candidate's depth on the current topic. When a topic has been sufficiently explored, transition naturally to a new topic and continue probing. Acknowledge the candidate's responses naturally before asking your next question. Continue the interview until the candidate or the session concludes. Keep your tone conversational, professional, and human. Do not break character.
For today's software engineering interview, explain how you'd select a database.

The first two things I consider are the type of data that we have and how we want to access it. For example, do we have a bunch of text documents and the only operation we want to do is search, or do we just have user data and are fine with doing a standard relational database setup?
Okay, what would you consider next?

All distributed systems are subject to the CAP theorem, and databases are no exception. So of these three, consistency, availability, and partition tolerance, our database will only be able to prioritize two.
Can you give an example?

Something like a bank would prioritize strong consistency. If they didn't, then somebody might be able to withdraw more than the allowed amount in their account using multiple transactions. Now, something like a social media app might prioritize availability. It doesn't really matter if somebody sees exactly how many likes are on their post in real time, as long as it's eventually correct.
Is there anything else you would take into account?

The last thing I always consider is what your engineers are familiar with. If everyone at the company is a wizard with PostgreSQL and MongoDB, you should probably just try to make that work. It can be a lot more headache to learn and use a new technology when the rest of your team is using something.

For today's software engineering interview, explain how you debug a slow database.
The first thing I do is collect data. I look at what queries are taking long, how long they're taking, and if this only happens while under load or all the time.
Okay, makes sense. Then what would you do next?

I'd look at specific requests to make sure they're doing exactly what I think they're doing, and they're doing it the best way possible. It's very easy to write a query that gets data, but does it very inefficiently.
All right. And what if the queries look fine?

If the queries look good, I'd make sure that our database is set up to handle the most common access patterns as efficiently as possible. We can do things like adding indexes or even adjusting the entire structure of the tables if necessary.
Okay, let's say your database is set up well and you already have the indexes in place.

If that doesn't help and queries are repeated and computation-heavy, we can introduce a cache between the database and the server to store the results of common requests so we can instantly return those.
What if requests aren't repeated or caching doesn't help?

If none of that works, we might be using the wrong database altogether. For example, trying to use PostgreSQL when the only operation we want is a fuzzy search. It works, but there are better options out there like Elasticsearch.

How can I stop AI crawlers from coming to my website?
Just add something like this to your robots.txt with all the user agents that you don't want to be able to access your site.
Do you think people actually care about the robots.txt file?
Instead of just using a robots.txt, why don't you build a dungeon to get them stuck and waste their time?
Okay, but how do we decide if somebody gets the dungeon or not?
Start by identifying if a user is a bot or not. You can use things like their user agent, their IP address, and their traffic pattern. This is difficult at first, but it will get easier over time.
Makes sense. What's in the dungeon, and how do we build it?
Hundreds or thousands of pages of real-looking static HTML content about real stuff, just generic useless information.
How does someone go from our regular content to the dungeon, though?
When a suspicious user requests a page, we add invisible anchor links that no human would ever click or see to the web page. Those anchor tags lead to our pre-generated content dungeon.
Okay, but beyond wasting their time, money, and compute, what's the point of this?

Remember the first step, deciding if someone's a bot or not? This is difficult if you don't have data, but the dungeon makes this easy. Anybody that gets stuck in the dungeon is probably a bot, so we can use that to train our profiler to catch bots more quickly in the future.
That's pretty sick. Does anybody do this already?

For today's software engineering interview, explain async APIs and how you design one.
To start, I would only use an async API for a long-running task where we don't need to give the user a response right away, something like video uploads and processing.
So, what happens when a request comes in?

When a request comes in, instead of processing the entire request and returning a response, all we have to do is acknowledge the request and send back a 202 Accepted response code with a job ID that they can use to later track that request.
All right. And where does that job actually go?

Whenever a request comes in, we can immediately return the response and drop that request into a queue. Our workers can pick up tasks from the queue whenever they're ready. They may be sitting in the queue for a couple seconds or a couple minutes, depending on the constraints and how busy our system is. Then, when they're done, they can write the result to a database.
And how does a client know when the job is done?

They have two main options. One, they could poll an API endpoint with that job ID we gave them. We'll just check the database, and if their job's complete, we'll give them back the complete info. Otherwise, we can send them an event via a webhook, an email, or some other method once that worker is complete with their job, and they'll be notified as soon as it's complete.

Hi Sankit. So let's quickly start your system design round. A couple of details before we actually start. First of all, it's going to be around 45 minutes round. So we will have a discussion first for the first 40 minutes and then for the last five minutes if there are any questions and if you want to deep dive into something more we can discuss that. And apart from it, while you're designing the whole system for whatever problem we pick I'll be asking a couple of clarifying questions in the whole process — so I'll stop you in between asking a couple of questions and if I need some more clarity on your design I'll be doing that as well. So are you clear? Should we get started?
Sure. Yeah, we can definitely get started here.
Okay. So what we're going to do is today we are going to design a stock broker. So are you aware of any popular stock brokers in India?
I guess we are referring to something like Groww, Upstock, Zerodha or INDMoney.

Absolutely right. So that is what today's problem is. We have to build a stock broker. Now when I say that — know that a stock broker's responsibility is simply to make sure that the transactions and the buying and the selling which is being initiated by the users is being basically going as a request to the exchange. Now exchange — it could be NSE, it could be BSC. Now you don't have to specifically build the exchange. You can already assume that there is an exchange that is available to you which can basically take in your orders and if they are successful give you those responses. So that is already available. You don't have to build that. We're going to focus more on the stock broker part — right, how internally an application like Groww or Zerodha would actually work, interacting with the clients as well as the stock exchange. Does that make sense?

Got it. Got it. So thanks for the clarification. I believe we can definitely very quickly jot down the functional requirements based on whatever you have actually mentioned here. So technically we want to just focus on designing the stock broker platform, right? We can assume that APIs for stock exchange are with us, stock exchange is given already — we don't have to care about the stock exchange — that means like stock exchange mainly also does the order matching and everything so all of that we don't need to care about. And apart from that, I believe one good functionality to definitely take care of for a stock broker platform something like Groww or Upstock is that users — like whatever are the end users — they should be able to initiate an order, like to buy something and sell something and those orders should be passed on to the stock exchange, right? That should be at least a bare minimum thing — that users should be able to place orders, buy or sell, on the platform.
Mhm. And platform forwards it to the exchange.

Correct. One clarification question that I would like to have here is that an order — buy or sell — can be of type limit order, that means of a specific price. If you want to set that always sell beyond this particular price or buy below this particular price only. Or it can be a market order as well as far as my knowledge goes with respect to stock platforms. So do we want to keep a support on around both like limit order and market order? Or we don't need to care about that?

Yes, ideally what we would like is our platform to be able to support both. If we would like to buy something at the market price ideally we should be able to do that but if we do have a limit order that should be supported as well. I think in the API itself we could have some way to take care of.

Okay, cool. So, that makes sense. We can definitely keep an eye on that. Apart from this, I believe one good requirement would be that the user should be able to see the price of the stock as well. Then only they will be able to actually place a buy or sell and make a decision for that. So I believe users should be able to see the stock price in real time.

Correct. Right. And do you want us to have a functionality where users should be able to see historic price — like maybe a chart system where we are able to show how the stock has performed for the last 1 hour, 1 day, 7 days, last 1 month, something like that? Do you think?

I think we can skip that for now. For the functional requirements I think this looks good. Although it would be good to discuss if we have time we can discuss a bit about this towards the end. But I think for now these functional requirements look good. Okay, this maybe I can add as a good-to-have.

Cool. I believe we have a good set of functional requirements. With that I would like to quickly jump up on the non-functional requirements also so that we can start getting some ideas on the system. I believe that this overall stock brokering platform is going to be kind of like a very hybrid platform. Whenever the user wants to do the buying or selling, I believe that the buying and selling should be done in a very highly consistent manner. When I say highly consistent I mean that I'm doing a trade-off with consistency and availability with respect to CAP theorem — that maybe the user is not able to place an order for a particular stock, that would be still okay — but it should not be like the money has been deducted, they placed a buy order but they don't see that buy order being executed or that buy order is lost. So that's why consistency is something that we should definitely focus on. But on the part of seeing the stock price — this part of the system should be kind of like highly available. Right? User should be always able to see the stock price and the stock movements. There might be a chance that the data that they are seeing is maybe 10 seconds old or 30 seconds old or a minute old and we will be able to push the latest data as soon as we have it, but they should be able to at least see whatever price has been there for the complete day so that they can at least make a decision on how the price is actually moving. So I would say that this stock price part should be highly available and if we can make it as consistent as possible that should be something we should try to look upon. Do you think these two are a good consensus to have?

Yes, I think that looks good. Can we also focus a little bit about on how many requests can we also expect considering let's say we have a 100 million users? What kind of traffic we can expect on our stock broker?

Okay, assume that — so I was about to come to this part. So let's do some back of the envelope calculation, right? Assuming that we have total 100 million users and I'm assuming that let's say 10% is our daily active user. If I expect that makes our calculation go to 10 million users per day are going to interact on the platform. So we have two main types of request: one is going to be actually buying and selling and one is going to be actually looking at the stock price. What we can definitely ensure is that looking at the stock price is going to be slightly more traffic-heavy on our platform rather than buying and selling — because a lot of people will be monitoring the stocks but the number of times they will be placing an order will be slightly less. So we can say that the stock price looking part is going to be kind of like read-heavy and buying and selling is going to be not that read-heavy. So overall the platform looks more like a read-heavy platform in a nutshell. So considering that we have 10 million daily active users, and assuming that let's say every user checks the price of at least 10 stocks maybe 20 times a day, this makes our overall number of requests to — 10 million into 10 into 20 — that's going to be 2 into 10 to the power 9, that is approximately 2 billion requests per day. And the peak load — we can assume that the peak load is at least 5x of it — so 5x makes it 10 billion requests per day. And if we want to calculate it on a per second basis then in one day we have about 100,000 seconds. So 2 into 10 to the 9 divided by 10 to the 5 comes out as 2 into 10 to the 4 QPS — 20,000 QPS — during the peak load that we might have to handle. And the number of buys and sells are going to be 10% of that, so 2,000 QPS is something that we should definitely try to handle. Is that a good assumption to actually get started with?
Yes, that is actually good. We can go ahead.

Okay, cool. Makes sense. With that let's try to focus on both the flows one by one. Now one thing is for sure that we have a dedicated exchange and we don't need to care much about the exchange part — exchange is going to be something that handles a lot of things for us like matching the order and giving us the order history and everything. We are going to have some end user clients who are going to actually interact with our platform. And definitely one thing that I would like to add — because it's a financial platform, this needs to have very high risk consideration in terms of frauds and everything. So we need to have definitely an API gateway in between that is going to ensure that we have all the requests rate limited, authenticated, any kind of a fraud check or whatever we have to do — there should be an API gateway that should technically help us with that. So that is going to be one component of the system very bare minimum.

Now talking about the fact that the client needs to first of all see the stock data — the user should be able to see the stock price. This is the first requirement that I'm trying to fulfill. Now the stock price is going to be regulated by the exchange and the exchange is going to technically give us the corresponding stock price. So technically what we can have is we can have a service in between — let's say a Price Service. What is the responsibility of this Price Service? The responsibility of this Price Service is going to be one thing: it tries to accumulate the data from the exchange for the current price of a particular stock and then gives that stock price back to the corresponding client. So technically this Price Service has to do a communication to the client and there needs to be some way it gets the communication through the exchange as well. So let's see first of all how the communication between this Price Service and the client is technically going to happen. Now this Price Service — the moment it has the latest data from the exchange about the stock price — it needs to send it to the client and there's a good chance that there will be clients who have already opened the page of the stock and will be expecting the new stock price. So a couple of ways: the client can again and again poll our Price Service but polling will be very heavy on the Price Service — we can definitely discard that. WebSockets are something that is a good two-way communication because then the Price Service will be able to send the data back to the client. But in WebSockets generally we use WebSocket when the client also has to send something to the server and the server has to send something to the client — more of a two-way communication. This doesn't look like a two-way communication to us. What is the client going to do? The client is going to technically subscribe or open up a stock page and expect the stock tick to come up. So I believe a good way would be that there should be some sense of server-sent events coming up altogether. These server-sent events should ensure that the Price Service is actually sending to the client through this API gateway whenever a new stock tick is there.

Sorry to interrupt you in between. I just had a couple of questions. You mentioned that you'll go ahead with server-sent events and you'll not be going ahead with the WebSockets approach. Do you think there are any substantial disadvantages of using WebSockets? Is there a reason why WebSockets won't fit in this scenario at all and server-sent events are something which is more preferable? Why do you think server-sent events are very suiting for this particular scenario?

See, to be very honest both could have worked. But I believe — first reason why I would not like to go with WebSocket is that WebSocket as an overall connection platform is mainly to ensure or facilitate two-way communication. This doesn't look like a two-way communication. So first thing is WebSocket might be an overkill. Second thing is that let's say the client has opened two or three stocks on the platform — let's say we are giving them a way that they can add multiple stocks and open the charts in parallel. For every chart, if we will maintain a WebSocket connection then the client has to maintain a lot of WebSocket connections to our server. This puts an overhead on the client side and our client application might be overwhelmed with the number of open WebSocket connections. So I believe the number of open WebSocket connections should be kept as few as possible for ensuring the performance. And as of now we don't see a requirement for WebSocket connection because the client doesn't have to send something to the server — maybe the only thing the client has to do is while setting up the persistent connection for SSE, they can just set up which stock symbol they want the latest price for and that is it. The persistent connection can happen and the data can come back. It's just that the client doesn't have to send anything else after the connection has been set up to receive the price. Hence I believe WebSocket is something that we can definitely avoid as of now.

Okay. And so whenever a person or a client basically opens the stock broker app, you can expect two types of clients. Number one is going to be somebody who's basically trying to look at the stock price for the very first thing in the day — they're opening the app probably after a day or after two or three days. And the second type of client is a recurring user who's constantly on the stock page and consistently trying to see the update of the price or if something has changed or not. So when you talk about SSE, what kind of scenario are we really dealing with over here?

Okay. So for now SSE is going to technically work for those clients which are let's say already on the page — like your client number two — who are already on the page, they were already looking at the stock price and through SSE the stock price updates are going to come up. For the first client — let's say the stock session has opened at 9:15 and it's as of now let's say 11:00 a.m. morning and now somebody like client one opens a page. So they don't just need the latest stock tick update — they also need the complete day data — how the stock has performed for that particular day. So we are going to add the setup for this flow as well that they are able to see the slight bit of history for that particular day of that particular stock and once they are on the page then the SSE should be established and SSE is going to send them the latest stock tick altogether. Does that answer your question?
Yes, you can go ahead.

Cool. Now talking about the two-way communication between the price service — on the server side. Again, price service can maybe do a polling to the exchange but exchanges are platforms which are already having a high amount of load and they have to do a lot of processing. So polling the exchange again and again for a price update is again not going to be the best thing. Ideally what should technically happen is that the exchange should also send us the events for the stock ticks — our price service should try to subscribe. For example I believe on NSE there are 5,000 symbols altogether. So what this Price Service should do is subscribe to a kind of API from the exchange which is going to be driven by server-sent events as well — so once this connection has been established between our Price Service and the exchange, the exchange can keep on sending us the corresponding set of price updates. This puts slightly less load on the exchange because then the exchange will not have to accept any request and process it. Whenever there is a stock price update because of the buys and sell orders and the volume then the update can be sent to the Price Service. And generally for platforms like Groww, Zerodha they have to subscribe and pay some amount of money to this particular exchange in order to subscribe to these events. So the only thing is that with this particular infrastructure we will be able to facilitate the new incoming stock ticks but for a client like client number one who is opening the page in the middle of the day — they also want the historical data for the day. So what we can actually do is we can use single responsibility principle to ensure that the Price Service is only responsible for showing all the prices to the client and because the exchange is more or less an outer world system — what we can have is a service called the Exchange Gateway Processor. What is the responsibility of this Exchange Gateway Processor? First, because this exchange is going to be on the outer world, it's outside of our private network altogether. So this will be responsible for doing any external communication to the exchange. What we can do is the Exchange Gateway Processor is going to actually receive all the server-sent events from the stock exchange like NSE, BSE, and because it has already subscribed it is receiving all of the data for us. What we can have is a storage in between — this Exchange Processor can store that data. So let's say at 9:15 the trading session started. The exchange is going to start sending us the updated stock tick. This Exchange Gateway Processor is going to technically store that data inside this storage. And let's say somebody like client one actually comes up — the client one is going to make a request to show me the last one and a half hour stock price and the latest stock ticks. So for sending the last one and a half hour stock price the Price Service is going to fetch the data from this storage. And apart from this there should be a direct communication between the Exchange Gateway Processor and Price Service — maybe via some API call — that the latest price update that is being fetched from the exchange is going to be sent to the Price Service and the Price Service is going to use SSE to send it to both of the corresponding clients.

Can you talk a bit more about what kind of storage you're going to opt for? That is question number one. And question number two would be: how would you really store the data — in what structure are you going to store the data in this storage?

Okay. So for this storage I believe we would like this storage to be in a way that all the stock ticks for a particular time stamp — at what particular timestamp and what particular stock symbol we are actually looking at — for that we want to store the stock prices. And although it's not our requirement but we had a good-to-have requirement of old price history charts and everything as well. So if we can have this storage as let's say something like a time series DB — maybe something like TimescaleDB — that can be useful for us because we would like to see the price for the last 1 and a half hour or the last particular day whatever has been there. And apart from that maybe we can shard this storage based on the stock symbols — those stock symbols which have extremely high trading volume, maybe the top 50 or top 100 on Nifty, are in one shard and those which are having lesser amount of volume are on a separate shard. So sharding strategy we can think about but I believe a good time series DB we can actually opt in here for the storage. And how would we really store the data? So for example as far as I know TimescaleDB is based on top of PostgreSQL. So we can have a table prepared that is going to be a price history table and inside this table whenever a price update is going to come up we can mention the timestamp of when the price update came up and what price value we have actually got for one particular symbol. And we can bucket it accordingly. We can have the bucketing done let's say on every 3 hours or every day kind of a thing as well. So the timestamp and whatever price value we have — we can have that — and we can index it with respect to the timestamp. And whenever the user actually wants we can show it in order of the right timestamps, like let's say from 9:15 then let's say next tick came up in 9:17 and so on. So however the price fluctuates, based on that we can send the data to the Price Service and then eventually to the client.
Got it. Okay, yeah, let's go ahead.

So the API should technically look like this: it should be a GET request to something like /api/v1/stock-price and in the query params we can send a symbol — something like let's say INFY — and we should be able to see all the stock price history for Infosys and also the moment we see the stock price history this should also help us to subscribe to that particular stock and any latest stock price update should also be sent via the SSE altogether. This is going to be one API that we can have in order to see the corresponding price history. If we want to have the filters and everything — let's say for last one day or something — so we can have a parameter like timeRange equals to something like '1d' or '1month' or '1hour', these kind of time range values we can pass if we want bucketed data to be sent from our Price Service for historic chart viewing. This is going to be the API that we can have. Now our one requirement is fulfilled — we are able to see the stock price and the good-to-have requirement is also there. Now we should be able to place the orders.

I believe the order placing responsibility should not go to the Price Service again because of separation of concerns and single responsibility principle. And I believe we also want to do a couple of things — whenever an order comes up we would also like to maintain an order history on our end so that we do not have to again and again request from the exchange what were the last 30 orders for this particular user. This will be a very expensive API to the exchange. So what we can have is a separate mechanism built in — let's call it the Order Management Service, or OMS. What OMS is going to ensure is that any kind of a buy or sell order call that actually comes up from our client is going to be technically entertained by OMS. Now OMS is going to do a couple of things. First, whenever a buy or sell order comes up, what it technically should try to do is it should try to store that in a separate storage — the Order DB. Why are we maintaining an order DB if the exchange also maintains orders? There are a couple of reasons. First, during trading times — in India for example trading is from 9:15 a.m. to 3:30 p.m. — during the trade times the exchange is already bombarded with a lot of requests and for every request we most probably have to pay the exchange as well. So we would like to keep the number of requests coming back from the exchange as low as possible. Second thing: we are maintaining this Order DB to also see a lot of historic data — maybe we can have some kind of OLAP system or analytic platform built on top of this where we can see what has been the user history or user interaction profile. And for any kind of reconciliation later — if any kind of security or privacy aspect comes up — we can also reconcile that okay this particular order at this point of time was actually recorded on our platform. So a lot of benefits we will get from storing this in our Order DB.

The Order DB's nature should be — we should be able to support good ACID compliance because whenever somebody places an order there might be a lot of operations going on. So either all of those operations should work or none should work. So I believe a good ACID-compliant order database should be there — something like PostgreSQL or some RDBMS. We can have AWS RDS with which we can deploy an RDBMS that can take all the order data altogether. Now the data structure for each order is going to look something like this: we are going to have a unique ID — that is order ID as a primary key. We are going to have let's say the user ID who has actually placed the order. We are going to have the symbol — the stock symbol for which we have placed the order. Maybe the timestamp when the order was placed. And we should have a status of the order — whether the order is in a placed state, pending state, fulfilled state, cancelled state, and so on. Initially the status can be pending. So this should probably be some kind of enum. Now apart from all of this there should be one extra property that we are going to have — that is an exchange order ID. What is this exchange order ID? Because the exchange also maintains a database on their end — so once our Order Management Service is going to technically send the order data to the corresponding exchange, the exchange is also going to store that on their end and there is going to be a unique identifier for that particular order on the exchange side as well. So later for any kind of reconciliation, the exchange will only deal with the unique order ID that the exchange has prepared. So this order ID that I have mentioned — this is our own database's primary key ID. This exchange order ID is going to be the unique ID that is going to be given to us by the exchange. So initially this exchange order ID is going to be null — because as of now we have received the order and we are making an order entry inside the order database.

Once this order entry has been added in the corresponding order database, what we can try to do is after this we can have a streaming system in between — because again we do not want to directly bombard all the orders to the exchange because exchange is already overloaded. So there can be some streaming platform like Kafka. So what OMS should try to do is add the order to Kafka, and then this Exchange Gateway Processor can have one more responsibility — it can take one by one all of the orders and try to put the orders in place to the exchange. And once it places the order to the exchange, the exchange is going to technically give back a corresponding external order ID and that external order ID should then be updated to our Order DB. And again, even the exchange can take some time to process the order. So if we want to get an update about whether the order has been fulfilled — instead of using SSE or polling — I believe exchange should be given some kind of a webhook to our Exchange Gateway Processor that whenever they have an order update they can call this particular webhook and this webhook will update the order status inside the Order DB. This is going to ensure that we are not polling and even for SSE we have to maintain a persistent connection — we can avoid that. Whenever exchange has that order update, exchange can actually give us the corresponding order update through a webhook.

The order placement API should be a POST request to /api/v1/order and in the request body we are going to send: the symbol of the order, the order type — whether it's a buy order or sell order, and then one more type property which will be limit or market price. If in case it's a limit order then we also need to send at what price we want to place this particular limit order and in case it's a market price then this price property can be null as well. Apart from that, the user ID and everything we can send separately in the header to ensure whatever authentication mechanism we have with the exchange.

Another question: so sometimes what happens in a stock broker is that a client tries to place the order and here in your case it will pass through OMS and then it will be passed to the exchange and let's say it's a limit order — I want to buy at a certain price — but that order never really gets processed. And it will stay in our system. So what your current design does is that if there's an order that's in a pending state — it was accepted by the exchange but eventually it never got placed. How do you want to deal with that data? Will it just sit in the RDBMS because it's going to be in the pending state forever? How are you going to design that part?

Exchange is going to call us whenever they have an update. If let's say we want to ensure that we do not wait for this exchange webhook maybe we can have a background process going on that maybe in a batch of orders takes an update from the exchange that okay these were the orders placed in the last 5 minutes — what are the updates for these orders? There can be a batch call that we can do with the exchange with a separate service which is again and again doing a reconciliation with the exchange. But again this will add more API calls that we are going to make to the corresponding exchange. But what if the order never gets placed? So I was saying that this exchange order ID is going to be something that is going to help us. If the order has been received by the exchange we can have a contract between them and us that if we have received an exchange order ID then the order has been accepted by the exchange. In case we do not have an exchange order ID, what we can use is our streaming platform — the order that was actually picked up by the gateway processor and sent to the exchange but we have not received an exchange order ID for that. So that order we can do a retry for a couple of times and let's say after four or five retries if that doesn't work then we can add that particular order to a dead letter queue and maybe with manual intervention or a separate process we can retry those dead letter queue orders or we can see what went wrong with that particular order. So there can be a mechanism for that. And using this exchange order ID we will be able to ensure whether the exchange has accepted our order or not. If the exchange has accepted our order it's now on the exchange that when they fulfill the order.

Okay, yeah, that sounds good. I have one last question and then if you have any closing remarks for your design you can discuss that with me. So if you will see in your design for Price Service and Exchange Gateway Processor — there could be situations where the price of the stock is changing quite frequently right and with that there's not only one stock price there could be multiple stock prices and that could put a lot of load on your Price Service right and it could be bombarded with a lot of price changes. Do you think having a simple API which is just sending the data updates would be very optimized in this scenario?

Yeah, so I was about to take up that because what I see is that we have most of the functional requirements already done. I was about to come to the non-functional requirements — let's see whether the system is able to scale for this much amount of load or not. So I would like to take up all of that one by one. So again because of the fact that we wanted to ensure buy and sell is highly consistent, we have this more transactional-compliant DBMS altogether. Now for making sure that we are able to handle this much amount of scale, I believe we need to do optimization on multiple levels. The first level of optimization — you are saying right that we are bombarding this API again and again to the Price Service and the Exchange Gateway Processor now has a lot of things to do as well. So instead of this what we can maybe try to do is again we could have a streaming platform in between using which this Exchange Gateway Processor can send that data to the streaming platform and Price Service can subscribe to it. But the problem would be — this is definitely a good design — but the problem is that Price Service has to again and again go and pull the corresponding Kafka queue and kind of like consume it. It's more like a pull model from the streaming platform. What would be better is that instead of a pull model we have a push-based mechanism and for that maybe something like a Redis realtime Pub/Sub can actually help us — that whenever we have an update on the price that price update goes to the Redis realtime Pub/Sub and Redis realtime Pub/Sub pushes it to the Price Service. So it will be all in all a push mechanism coming up here and we do not have to bombard our Price Service directly altogether. That is one thing.

Second thing is that again it's not just that we have to handle these many requests. We need to keep the latency as low as possible. So a good strategy in stock broker platforms is that the infrastructure for our complete stock broker platform can be actually placed in the same data center or whatever infrastructure these exchanges are — for example there's a dedicated data center in a particular region where NSE or BSC has deployed their servers. We can try to keep our servers also in the same data center and maybe try to have a contract in a way that we have maybe as much as possible more wired connection directly to the servers. What this is going to ensure is that our overall turnaround time is going to be very less, latency is going to be very less and we will be having much faster order fulfillment and much faster data flowing through us. And one more thing is that even if we are not able to get our servers in the same data center, at least we should try to keep in the same region because there's a good chance that the majority of the load of the stock buying and selling is going to come up from one particular country's region.

Apart from this I believe there should be dedicated servers provisioned for hedge funds and bigger platforms which buy and sell in very high volume rather than the normal retail investors. So I believe there should be dedicated servers pre-provisioned in a warm state for those kind of hedge funds because whenever their request is going to come up their request is going to be in bulk very high value and we need to ensure everything is done for them. So this horizontally-scaled mechanism we might want to have a hybrid strategy here for both of these servers — that we cannot just rely on autoscaling. We would like to have pre-provisioned servers. And sometimes we can also do one thing — let's say one symbol is having very high trading volume. A lot of traffic for one particular symbol can be redirected to one or two dedicated servers. So even the load balancing mechanism we might want to keep hybrid — that instead of just equally distributing all of the load, all the stock data for one particular high volume stock is going through one price server and that server is maybe an extra-large instance on EC2 that is able to take more load. So I believe a good amount of hybrid strategies should be there in order to identify how the load distribution should technically work. And in the Order DB — if we want to ensure that we are able to see past history of orders we should try to do a couple of optimizations. We should try to shard the DB based on user ID so that all one user's orders are going to one shard and every shard maybe we can try to index with respect to the timestamp so that we are able to get the last 30 or 40 orders altogether. So I believe a good sharding strategy should be done here as well.

Okay Sankit, I think we are out of time. Also, just one last question — maybe try to answer it in a few lines only. Your stock broker platform is very popular and a lot of traffic in a very short amount of time is coming from your stock broker itself. How are you going to limit the ordering? Is that something you will take care of or is that something the exchange should take care of — in case of a very high surge number of orders from a particular stock broker — and how would that work?

So again multiple things can be done. For example I believe the exchange might be already doing a bunch of things around the rate limiting on their aspect first. Second, this Exchange Gateway Processor that we have — we can have dedicated rules done on this Exchange Gateway Processor. If you see the orders that we are submitting, the orders are coming to OMS, from OMS we are putting it into a streaming platform and this Exchange Gateway Processor is one by one picking up the order. So we can try to tune in this platform in order to ensure that we do not bombard our exchange with a lot of order requests. That's why this streaming platform is going to help us — it's going to put a lot of good amount of back pressure. So that is going to be there. Apart from that if we see a surge of price history kind of data then again because of this storage we can try to at least show something to the user and we do not want it to be highly consistent. So we can try to limit the amount of requests that we are sending to the exchange for subscribing to the stocks. We can also ensure that we have high priority obtained for some particular symbols — the top symbols on large cap, midcap, small cap — those are going to be something that we cannot get very stale data on. But there are some symbols for which even if we are getting some stale data we are okay with that. And we can have rules applied for the Exchange Gateway Processor. What I can see is that the maximum amount of surge on these kind of brokerage platforms is going to come up at two times: one when the market session starts and when the market session is about to end. So we can have dedicated server provisioning done for that particular amount of time — that we are going to see a high amount of order load — and at least we can start accepting the order but those orders can be processed by the exchange later. So because of this asynchronous mechanism we will be able to limit the amount of orders.
Okay, I think we're done. Thank you so much for this design. I think it looks good and thank you for the interview.
Cool. Yeah. Thanks. Thanks. Yeah. Thank you for your time.

For today's software engineering interview, please explain webhooks.

A webhook provides a way for other applications to let your application know when an event happens. Let me explain this through a simple example. Let's say I'm using Stripe to process payments, and I have some application which does other application logic. Whenever I want to know if there's a new payment, I have to make an API call to Stripe to get the payments, and Stripe can return me a list of all the new payments.
Making constant API requests to look for new data does seem like a pain in this case.

Exactly. It's slow because I might not make an API call right when a payment happens, and it wastes a lot of resources because I'm constantly making API calls to check for new payments, whether or not somebody actually made one. So, here's where webhooks come in. I just send Stripe a URL of where my webhook is on the server side, and from then on out, anytime any event happens, in this case a payment, Stripe can send a post request with the information that I need.
Okay, and then based on that notification, you can do something in real time.

Exactly. Now, I'm not wasting resources making API requests to look for events that may or may not have happened, and as soon as an event happens, I'm able to do something like send my users an email thanking them for buying.
