For today's software engineering interview, describe a time where you deployed code that took down a production environment.

I was working as a software engineer on a network automation team at Amazon and I was in charge of a suite of microservices that handled about 10 million requests per day. I was tasked with migrating a legacy service from Java 8 to Java 17 to improve maintainability and performance. I upgraded to JDK 17 locally, fixed a few issues caused by the differences in encapsulation between JDK 8 and JDK 17, then deployed to a test environment where everything ran well. I deployed to a pre-production environment and let the change bake while people were using it for a week to make sure that everything worked well and there were no edge cases that I had missed with real traffic. After that, I partnered with another engineer for a controlled deployment process. Within five minutes of the production deployment, customers started complaining in Slack that the service was completely down. After confirming the change was related to our recent deployment, I immediately rolled everything back and service was restored.
And what did you do after you fixed the issue in production?

After service was restored, we worked on finding the root cause, which was a difference in the way that the runtime was set between production and pre-production environments. I wrote a detailed post-mortem document and created alarms and test cases to make sure this never happens again in the future.

For today's software engineering interview, please explain how you secure your APIs.
First, I always use secure protocols like HTTPS. With this, all data is encrypted during transit so no third-party person can read what information is being sent between clients and my server.
Okay, that's a good way to protect customer data. What do you do to control access on the server side?

I use OAuth plus OpenID Connect to handle authentication and authorization. I also only give users access to a certain subset of my endpoints and information, following the principle of least privilege where users can only access exactly what they need and nothing more.
Okay. And how do you handle APIs you expect to interact with the front end?
For my APIs that need to interact with a website or front end, I make sure to handle CORS gracefully and only allowlist the domains that I expect to make requests to my backend. Never allow all.
Do you do anything else to protect your service?
I implement rate limiting on both a per API key and per IP level to prevent people from making too many requests to my service and ruining availability for everybody.

So, I know there's something called consistent hashing we can do to avoid having to do that, so we can increase the number of shards without having to repartition a lot of our data. Yeah, yeah. Yeah. Okay, so let me just update that: shard by subreddit ID. How would you design TikTok?

Okay, TikTok. All right. Popular vertical format video sharing application, popular with lots of people around the world I guess. And so it's kind of a social media platform. Pretty interesting and pretty complex I think. So tell me a little bit about maybe use cases here that we should be thinking about.
Yeah, sure. Let's not spend time on the mobile app and content creation in the app. I'd like you to focus on a back-end distributed system that supports uploads and downloads.

Okay, that's good. Yeah, because certainly the application is the most central point of the whole thing and you could do lots of cool things in terms of generating your being a Creator so I'm glad we don't have to worry about that — that's also not my forte. I think that's — you're right — that's the less interesting part, so let's skip over that. Yeah. Okay, so you want to focus on sort of the video uploads into the system and then streaming the videos or consuming the videos I guess?
Absolutely. Okay, so tell me a little bit about how many users, how many videos uploaded — what's the scale of this thing, what are we thinking about here?

Okay, yeah. Well we've got let's say one billion users in around 150 countries. And let's assume one billion video views a day. And I think I saw in a recent year there were 10 billion videos uploaded in one year, so let's use that figure as well.

All right, let me capture some of this stuff — make sure I get this right. We've got a billion users across 150 countries. A billion videos per day viewed. And 10 billion videos uploaded per year. All right, that makes sense. That's big and that's good because we want it to be a big distributed system. So if I think about this app — uploading, downloading, streaming, viewing — even though we're not going to talk about the app too much — we probably should think about some success metrics. Do you have any thoughts on success metrics or would you prefer to hear my thoughts?
Yeah, I'd prefer to hear your thoughts on it. Give me some suggestions.

Okay, all right. So I mean I guess in a classic sort of web application kind of thing, daily active users make sense. I think for TikTok the simple metric I guess would probably be something like time in app — so how long are people spending in the app. I'm not a heavy TikTok user at all but I imagine that people use it in different ways — some maybe just scroll through the videos, some watch the entire thing and react and share and do lots more interesting things, and then there's the creators. But time in app is probably the simplest metric to think about, so maximizing time in app. Do you know how long people spend in the app? Do you have a sense of that?
I think let's say the average is around an hour a day. Does that sound okay?

Sure, so let's assume that. Okay that sounds fine. So time in app, currently about an hour per day. Sounds good. All right, so let me see if I can make some more detailed assumptions here. So this is vertical — it's a vertical form factor video sharing thing as I mentioned — and typically these videos are 1080 pixels by 1920 pixels or something like that. The typical video time range for these clips is like three seconds to a minute. But just for sake of simplification maybe we can assume a 10 second video on average. If that seems okay?
Yeah, yeah, I think that works.

And so 1080 times 1920 pixels — which in a video, a 10-second video — I think we can probably assume a megabyte per video. If that seems all right. Okay, so megabyte per video. With those assumptions maybe I can do some really rough calculations. Let me look at uploads first because that translates to storage in addition to some traffic. So if we say 10 billion videos per year, and if we divide by 365 days in a year, that comes out to approximately 30 million videos uploaded per day. And if I say storage — 10 billion a year times a megabyte per video, so 10 billion megabytes — that's 10 petabytes of raw video storage per year. But we have to factor in possibly some replication for redundancy, also possibly different formats for different types of devices. So I might say devices and replication times 10, so let's say 100 petabytes per year of storage requirements. Okay, so that's a lot of storage but these days you can scale. And this naturally makes me think about a blob storage solution — modern solutions scale quite well for this. For video metadata — I mentioned this is a kilobyte of data per video, so instead of 10 petabytes it's a factor of a thousand less — we're talking about 10 terabytes of video metadata. And for traffic — if we say 10 billion videos uploaded per year and divide by 365 days and then by approximately 100,000 seconds in a day, that gets me to 300 videos per second — or at peak maybe around 1,000 per second. At a megabyte each and a megabyte is approximately 10 megabits, that's 10 million megabits — or around 10 gigabits per second ingress. Modern network cards on servers are 10 gigabits per second so this is not a bottleneck. For views — a billion videos viewed per day divided by a hundred thousand seconds gives us 10,000 videos per second. Doing the similar math that's about 100 gigabits egress, which is also not a lot. So I think these are all numbers that can be worked with.

Okay, I think we should probably move on. So let me just draw a couple boxes here. I'm going to draw a little TikTok app and then of course we're going to do the typical thing — I don't really want to spend too much time on load balancers here — you've got some sort of a TikTok app service, this is the thing that would run in the cloud, this would be one of my servers and there'd be multiples of these. But I think I take that back — I think I want to separate these two because you asked me to talk about uploads and viewing and streaming. So there's going to be some sort of a TikTok app service. And then let's create another one which is the TikTok upload service because we're going to have we're going to be uploading videos and I think I might keep these separate because they serve slightly different purposes, and in a microservices architecture best practices you would separate these things. The app service when you first open your TikTok app comes through the load balancer and hits one of these app services — the very first thing you might see is all the videos etc and the followers and all these kinds of things. But if I click on upload or create a new video then I might get redirected to the upload service. These are kind of the front ends to the services.

Okay, so let's talk a little bit more about the databases. The first thing I talked about was the video blob storage — that's where the actual raw videos would go. And so this has to be sort of like a file system almost, but I probably would use something like Amazon S3 for this because that scales really well and it's purpose-built for this kind of thing. By nature of Amazon Web Services you can nicely replicate the data for better availability, you can have regional replicas so that it's more aware of regions. And the other thing that you can do is you can tier the storage data. We talked about 100 petabytes of raw data for a year and so that can accumulate quickly. With TikTok videos I'm making an assumption that a video's lifespan is not more than maybe a couple of months for most videos — that you don't go back and watch three-year-old videos too much, that it's all about the most recent stuff. Which means that you can tier your data such that older data or data that hasn't been looked at as often can go into Cold Storage. So I'm going to put a keyword in here which is tiered AWS S3. What that means is that if a video is let's say a year old and nobody's really looked at it it could go into really slow offline storage which means it doesn't cost as much and it's more cost effective versus stuff that is most recently uploaded. Then video metadata — I mentioned this is 10 terabytes and I said NoSQL storage. So I probably would use something like DynamoDB as an example. I'm biased for purposes of an initial solution to hosted solutions like Amazon provides because they take a whole bunch of maintenance and operational load off of the engineering team. DynamoDB scales really nicely in terms of the number of rows you can have. So these two things together would make up basically the video storage database.
So far, yeah. We're good so far. How do you think the different regions and countries could be reflected in your design? Are you taking them into account?

Yeah, good question. So I think the nice thing about some of these existing hosted solutions is that they have regional features. But that's not really answering your question I think. If I think about it — I can imagine having different data centers in different locations because you want the responsiveness to be fast. If you're in India you want the responsiveness to be fast, if you're in Europe you want the responsiveness to be fast, if you're in China, US — same kind of thing. And so having the data local near to where you are is what you have to do as a global application. And one of the ways to do that is by partitioning the data possibly based on region or language or things like that, and locating the data in different places. Another thing — when we get to the serving or streaming side — in order to not overload these storage systems you really need to have some sort of a CDN, a Content Delivery Network, like CloudFront or something like this. And what this does is it's basically a set of caches near the closer to the user that cache data that is typically popular. So videos that are popular in a particular region would be in this CDN, stored in the CDN, and thus made to be available faster. The regionality might happen automatically — you'd probably get videos created in India to be more commonly located in the CDNs in India versus Europe, US, China, wherever. So there's definitely some regional aspects to this.

Okay, so the piece that we're missing — the piece that I haven't talked about yet — let me draw yet another database because we also mentioned user data. A billion users across 150 countries but they have friends and followers and connections and this is where this whole social graph kind of comes into play. Because of those connections — and those connections may very well span regions and countries — you probably want to have this user data not be explicitly partitioned out into separate databases that can't talk to each other. So I probably would want to put this into some sort of SQL storage solution. I might use Amazon RDS — relational data store — which allows you to use SQL solutions to store your data. I'm hand-waving a little bit about the amount of data here. At a billion users and a kilobyte per user — that's a terabyte of data — which fits, you can do that with modern SQL solutions. But we're starting to get into some bottlenecks there potentially so that might be something we need to look out for. Something like Google Cloud Spanner actually scales much more broadly than that and gives you some relational features. But anyway — user data — that's another big aspect of this.

So if I talk about the upload path — you know the TikTok app would upload a video and send that to the upload service. And then that has to go from there to two places — it really needs to go to the blob storage and it needs to go to the metadata store because you need to be able to add the information about the video so that you can figure out can it be shown, does it make sense to show to people. So that's roughly the flow of an upload. Now let me talk about the video streaming flow. I'm going to just draw a box here and I'm going to call this the 'for you' generator. So when I load my TikTok — when I first go into my TikTok app — I'm already seeing videos. There's no lag, there's no clicking on things, I'm already as soon as a video is in focus it starts playing. And so that means that my app is actually getting multiple videos by the time I start the app. So if I go to the app service, one of the important things the app service has to do is it has to talk to this generator — and by the way this generator is also going to have to be multiple servers because we're scaling. This app service needs to be able to talk to some back-end algorithm that says what should I show the user. And I think that this is actually rather complicated and sophisticated and if I were doing this today I would probably employ some sort of machine learning system to figure out what to show the user because there's just too many variables to try and build a rule-based system. So machine learning solution — that's the overview. So this TikTok app service needs to contact this generator, and ultimately what it's going to do is it's going to return back to this application service a list of video IDs — the for you video IDs — that the application ultimately should show to the user.

So then what happens with that? The application service would get from those video IDs the metadata information about the videos — how long they are, what is the ID, the URL for where to find this video so that I actually can go and fetch it, who created the video, how many likes it has, things like that. So this app service would go straight to the metadata database to get that information so that it could pass that on to the application. And among other things it returns these video URLs. Then the TikTok app for each of those video URLs goes and fetches them by just going to those URLs — and that URL is going to land at a CDN, a Content Delivery Network. Now if that content delivery network has the video because it's popular in its local cache, then it will just go ahead and deliver that — and that's just how CDNs work, nothing special — so if the CDN has it it'll just serve it and it'll be fast and for popular videos that's super fast. If not then it'll wind up going to the back-end storage service like S3 and going and fetching it. So from a video serving perspective, the application has two parts to it: there's the actual video data which goes through the CDN and the blobs, and then there's the metadata which would come through the application service and returns everything you need to show to make your phone application look good.

All right, so if that seems good so far — there's two things I'd like to do if we still have time. One of them is I'd like to just talk a little bit more about the data — the schema of the data — what are the elements of the video metadata and the user metadata. And I want to talk about those because I think they're related to the second thing I want to talk about which is a little bit more thinking around how I might do this generator if I were to treat it a little less as a black box. So if that's okay, that's kind of where I'd like to go.

I'd love to see you drill down. So let's talk about the video metadata — what's in this video metadata? So there's going to be a video ID which is just a unique identifier for the video. There's going to be also a video URL — and I'll mention this but I don't want to write it yet — but I mentioned briefly earlier that you might want different encodings depending on the video so during the upload phase, possibly offline asynchronously, if we want to store differently encoded forms of the same video then we probably need to have different URLs or different blobs for those things. So we might have the original raw uploaded video, an iPhone optimized video, a MacBook version, things like that. So you might have video URLs as a list instead of just a single one. But I'll hand-wave over that for now. Okay, so video ID, video URL. What else? Maybe like a creation timestamp — when was this thing created? Probably like a creator ID — who created this video, who uploaded this video? We probably want something like likes or reactions — how many people liked it, how many people didn't like it, etc. And there's something else I want to put in here as a placeholder for now — which is related to the algo features. I'll just call this for now and get back to it. And there might be something like length or duration of the video. So that's what I would think about for the video metadata.

And let me now talk about what I think would be in the user data. So there's the typical stuff about users — a user ID, some credentials, login credential type of stuff, maybe some things like name, age, other bits of information like that. But then I think the other thing is because this is such a social network graph you probably want to have following user IDs — so who are the people that you're following. There's probably also video history IDs — this is the watch history like what are the things that you've watched in the past, videos you've seen or been shown. And then for if you're a Creator you probably also have uploaded video IDs — what are the videos that you've uploaded. And let's not forget we talked at the very beginning about the success metric — time in app — so I probably need to track per user how long they've been spending in the TikTok app, aggregate that, and be able to look at that offline. So that's probably something I need to store and keep track of.

Okay, so let's get back to this thing I mentioned — the for you algo profile. I should probably change the video one here as well — for you algo features. So here's how I'm thinking about this — and again I'm not an ML expert by any means, I have limited background in this kind of stuff — but in order to have a machine learning system be trained and be able to make decisions, one of the things it needs to take in is a set of features. Features could be things like the video ID itself, the number of followers, the entire network graph, content preferences, language — things like this that are specific to this user that help the algorithm figure out what types of videos to show. So that profile is something that I would want to store per user. The for you generator would reference this profile and utilize it along against the algo features that were generated for a particular video — and that could be length of video, content, location, language, a bunch of different features. So this generator can essentially input the features from the user and based on all of the algorithmically feature-identified videos make a determination as to what the best match is for videos to show to the user. So that's a lot of hand-waving but the idea is that this is essentially a neural network or some sort of other system — a machine learning mechanism that has been trained offline with all the videos that have been uploaded, they've all been feature-extracted for each of the videos, and then those features are fed into the system to train it. And then there's a matching step where on the fly we say okay, given this user profile, what are the videos that we should show? That's still kind of black-boxy and hand-wavy. But the key thing is that there's an exclusion profile — exclusion profile might have in it follower IDs or user IDs or something like that for people you don't want to see anymore. Because this ML system is offline and asynchronous and might take a while to catch up if I say I don't want to see any more videos from a particular user — I expect that to take effect immediately. So I'd want a little bit of rule-based guardrails — a combination of some machine learning stuff which is not going to be perfect, and some simple rule-based logic that is unambiguous, to ultimately filter down the set of video URLs that the user would see.
Let me stop here and just ask you — what other things are you thinking about or wondering about here with this design?
Yeah, well I'm wondering if there'd be any bottlenecks and yeah I'd also like to hear if you have any enhancements you could make.
Yeah, good questions.

Okay, so I mean one bottleneck — which is not so much a performance bottleneck but I think I already mentioned there could be possibly a storage bottleneck in terms of keeping all of the user data in one single database just because of size. That could be an issue — I might have to partition the data for the largest regions or something. That might be that. But I think the more interesting performance bottleneck is this generator — that is generating on the fly in near real time when this app service is called every time the app says hey give me what's next. That could be a bottleneck because it has to respond quickly. And so one thing that I wanted to mention here — one of the really cool features of TikTok that I find fascinating is that you're in the app and everything is there immediately. There's no lag, there's no hesitation, there's no spinning wheel — it's all just right there as you're scrolling and your videos are scrolling and they're starting to play as soon as they come into focus. How do you make that happen? The answer is you don't send a request right then. I think the answer is that as The TikTok app is running it's actually constantly talking to the app service and saying what are the 10 next videos or the 20 next videos, and so it's doing what's called a read-ahead. It's pre-computing basically the stuff so that it's ready to go. That's not perfect and there are some issues with that but that's one way to make this seem extremely alive and responsive. But that doesn't solve necessarily the bottleneck of this generation here. So one thing I could think of doing is over time when this app service and the generator start to maybe mature I could imagine moving this logic — some of this logic — into the app, onto the popular platform. So if we've got iOS, Android, Windows, Mac, maybe web — I could imagine that on the most popular platforms — let's say iOS and Android on just mobile devices — that you move this generator code into the actual application onto the phone so that it can take advantage of maybe even the GPU on the phone. Because smartphones are super powerful these days, so you have a lot of compute power to use there, and that could reduce the bottleneck of having these things sort of running out in the cloud. So that that would be a bottleneck that I would be thinking about.

You asked about enhancements. So I guess maybe moving the generator logic into the phone would be an enhancement too. I think an enhancement that is probably more of a product enhancement is that I could imagine — right now you get a for you feed and it's customized to your likes and preferences — I could imagine having the generator occasionally pepper the list of videos with something that is outside of your domain or your interests, to kind of give you a taste of something. You don't want a lot of that because you don't want people to get a feel like okay I'm getting something that I don't like. But you could imagine peppering the results every now and then with a little bit of something that is new and unusual — sort of a surprise unsolicited idea for a video or something like that. And that would still go through that filter that I was talking about — the exclusion guardrails — so if this is from a follower that I explicitly said don't look at then I wouldn't want to see that. But if it's something that hasn't explicitly been disallowed then maybe it's something that you get to see. An enhancement from a system perspective — one enhancement I could imagine making is that if some of this architecture stabilizes you could pick out certain components or aspects that are costly. So if DynamoDB turns out to be super expensive or RDS — maybe you decide you know what, I'm going to go ahead and pay the engineering costs and get my engineers to do our own hosting of a MySQL solution or do it on a different platform — just to kind of reduce costs because now it's worth it at the scale to build our own solution. But that's always a trade-off between the engineering cost and the sort of service costs that you wind up paying for these kinds of solutions.
Yeah, let's wrap it up now. Mark, just have a quick look over your design — are you happy with it? Do you feel that you met the objectives that we laid out at the beginning?

Yeah, so I think so because I think you asked about sort of the upload and download streaming aspects of it and we did some capacity there. We have the databases, we've got the rough idea of how that's going to flow through the system. I've got a little bit of hand-wavy stuff around the for you feed which is sort of what we show. So I feel like this is a decent start. Again, I imagine the real TikTok architecture is significantly more complex — I mean people have spent engineering decades building this thing and it's an amazing app — so there's no way I'm going to reproduce that in any amount of time. But kudos to those engineers.
That's very good. Well I think you made a pretty good attempt. Let's finish the interview there. Thanks Mark, you can relax. How was it — was it enjoyable?

That was fun — hard, difficult, but fun. It's a tricky one, but yeah it was good fun. Watching you watching you do it and yeah I think hopefully people will have learned a lot. I'm sure we'll get lots of comments on it. So yeah, Mark, thanks very much and yeah I hope we'll see you again here sometime soon. Thank you, thanks Tom.

So, this will also obviously have an ID, similarly, primary key and serial. So, what are some of the things we want for the garage? Yep.

And one thing to note here is I'm using `DECIMAL`. I know there are obviously some issues down the line regarding floating-point errors and calculations and payments. I think `DECIMAL` is the PostgreSQL type that should be able to handle that. I'm not confident that there's an equivalent MySQL type, but I'm sure there is. So, I think that seems like a pretty reasonable approach.
I think that's fine.

And then, let's go with `spots`. Like we mentioned, we have a spot table here. Similar to the other ones, we want an ID with a primary key, a serial. Now, what do we want for the spot? We want the relevant garage, obviously. We want the type of vehicle, and then we want the specific status. Like, what is it? Maybe it's being reserved, maybe it's unavailable right now, maybe they're paving over the spot, or maybe it's just totally empty. So, I think those are pretty reasonable things to have there.
Okay.

So, the `garage_id`, obviously, it's a foreign key, and I guess it's a secondary key. And then, we want the `vehicle_type`. For now, I'm going to call this an `ENUM`. I'll get into this a little later when we actually define... oh, actually, I can get into this now, I guess. There's a trade-off when you add enums, particularly in a relational database, simply because you gain performance benefits by being an `INT` as opposed to a character string or whatever. But the trade-off is some flexibility issues down the line. I know in PostgreSQL specifically, when you add an enum, you can never remove it; you can never remove the type afterwards. So, that's something to consider when you choose them. I think for this specific case, like compact, regular, and large, I don't think those are things that are ever going to be totally out of phase in a parking garage type system, so I think it's totally reasonable.

Yeah, I mean, I'm here. But like I mentioned before, I don't think we're going to run into these issues at scale here. It would be totally reasonable to just use a `VARCHAR` as well for flexibility, and then have that vetting on the application layer side.
Yeah, I think that's fine. You can make a call over here on the trade-off that you're willing to go for, for sure.

Cool. And then, what else? Oh, we had the, I guess, supposedly optional table. Given that we optionally want to be able to create an account, let's say we have a `users` table. That should just have all the familiar things: an ID with a primary key, a serial. And then, as we mentioned in the previous endpoints that were optional, maybe an email, or I guess `VARCHAR` because we're in PostgreSQL, and then a password as well. Note that this is probably something conventional, like SHA-256 hashing. And then, first name, obviously nullable, as well as last name. Cool. And then, arguably, if I'm thinking about the nature of this type of application, the whole reason we wanted optional users in the first place is just so they could recurringly reserve a spot. If we want to be able to recurringly reserve a spot, we probably also want the type of vehicle. Actually, we might want the type of vehicle in general. So, I think a `vehicle` table is a totally reasonable thing to have in this scenario.
Yep, I think that's fine.

And then, let's go with ID as usual. And then, optionally, a user ID that can be a foreign key. And then, let's say we want their license, which seems reasonable. And then, probably the vehicle type again, similar to the one that was in the `spots` table. So, yeah, I think that probably covers our requirements here. I mean, obviously, I can add more as we go through the problem, but for now, does that seem like a good starting point?
I think this seems good. Yeah, this looks reasonable. Maybe we can dive into what the high-level architecture would look like.

Yeah, for sure. So, we can get into that here. We start us off with the very basics: we want an application UI, it can be either web or mobile, and then we want some sort of back-end server. So, let's say here we have a web app or mobile UI, depending on the sort of... or maybe even both. And then, over here, let's go with a back-end server. This will talk to this, and vice versa. Cool. Now, some of the deeper considerations to have here are, obviously, we're going to have a database. As we mentioned before, the one that I lean towards is a PostgreSQL DB. So, let's just call this DB. And so, that's just off the bat. In reality, we can end up doing a number of different adjustments here. One thing that we can consider is splitting the back-end server into a reservation server and a slot server, given the complexity of allocating slots. That seems probably a little unnecessary here. A bigger thing that might be more reasonable is, given that we described up here that the system needs to have high consistency, we notice that in this type of problem, we probably want to be able to read way more than write. So, if we're going to be reading a lot more than writing, we probably want a number of different read replicas for the DB. So, we can separate this out here, move it up this way. Yes, I can. Awesome. So, let's call this a read replica. So, the server would kind of work like this. Essentially, go here. One thing that would be prudent to have in this scenario, given that we're probably hosting a number of read replicas, we probably want some form of load balancer in between, just to allocate where the individual calls are going. It would probably be prudent for this type of problem to shard that based on location. I know that we mentioned earlier that the garage might have a zip code; that seems like a pretty reasonable way to approach that. So, let's maybe this here. And then, from here, we can go up here, call this read, call this read. Then, this one will be write. And then, what else? We mentioned... oh, go for it.
I was going to ask, do you anticipate any trade-offs based on this decision?

Yeah, well, not specifically the read replica portion. One thing that is likely going to end up being a specific trade-off, as I was sort of pathing towards, we mentioned that this type of thing wants high consistency. So, I think it's pretty reasonable to take a strong consistency approach versus an eventual consistency approach. Simply because, if we think about the nature of someone using this application from a customer perspective, it's likely not the case that they're going to be super frequently reserving things, probably not more than even twice a day at most, and that already seems a little excessive. So, in reality, they're probably very willing to wait or deal with a higher latency, which is the trade-off you make when you have strong consistency versus eventual consistency, where you increase the performance, the execution on the customer side, in the customer's view. But the trade-off you make there is you might not have the exact data. We want exact data because we want them to be able to know that the spot they're reserving is specifically the spot they'll get. The way that's tackled in this setup is you basically read lock. That's an initial approach that a lot of people use. It seems pretty reasonable as well, especially for something that's not necessarily at an aggressive scale. In the sense that when someone's writing to the PostgreSQL DB, you read lock the replicas. That way, they wait for the result to finish, so you know the data is as up-to-date as possible. One potential consideration in the future might be, depending on how you want to split up and shard your database, you might want to consider the logic of read locking based on location. Because if we think about it, why would we read lock something in Wisconsin if someone's reserving something in California? But that's a little more of a complex decision down the line.
Okay, yeah, I think that's a good, that would be a good next step. Cool.

And then, yeah, I think that's... oh, one other thing. We mentioned up here that we have a payment, since we're likely to be using an existing third-party payment. So, we just draw that in here. Let's say it's like... place that here, have that here both ways. Don't really know how to do this. Oh, there we go. Cool. Nice.
Okay, I think this is pretty good and a good place to stop. What did you think about that, Tim?

Ah, it's an interesting, weird problem, I guess. I think a lot of these types of problems end up actually having issues at scale. So, I think I was lucky in the sense that I think it's reasonable to say, with some back-of-the-envelope estimation, that this problem doesn't tackle that. And that made it a lot more of, I guess, how do we design the data problem.

Yeah, now I think some things that you did really well: clarification questions at the beginning were very well drawn out by you, and that made the scope of the question a lot more manageable for you to tackle and to just understand exactly what the requirements were. And I think one thing that I saw you do consistently, and that was really good, was providing your opinions. So, explaining what the problem was, why you were doing something, what your opinion on it was. One example was just separating the payment calculation from the public endpoint. That was a small example where you suggested that you were going to separate it, and you provided a reason for that. And I think that you did that consistently throughout, which just shows that you would be someone who people would enjoy working with as an engineer because you have opinions on things. And it's conversational too; it adds flavor to the question that you're doing. It doesn't sound very robotic and rehearsed, and adds some commentary for the interviewer to be able to interact with as well. Yeah, and I like that towards the end you called out that you weren't super confident on the use of `DECIMAL` as the type. Things like that, I think, are good for interviewees to do. If there is something that people aren't super confident about, call it out. It's better to just throw it out there than kind of go into it blind, because then at least the interviewer knows exactly where you stand and what you might need help with or hints with at some point in the question. What advice do you think you'd give someone if they were to receive this question?

Yeah, I mean, I think a lot of people approach these types of problems very, I guess, almost too strictly formulaically. And I think that that's good to an extent. I mean, I think a lot of the reason for that is algo problems are another metric that people are generally evaluated on, and that honestly probably doesn't have too much bearing on the actual job. But system design questions, I feel like, are a lot more relevant and applicable to actual work contexts. So, thinking about the problem from a more flexible standpoint there, and treating it like you're working with a co-worker and you're actually going over an estimation of what the entire system is like, is a pretty reasonable approach to tackle these types of problems.
Yeah, absolutely. Well, thank you so much, Tim. This was really great, and good luck with your interviews!
Thank you so much, it was fun.

You are an expert Senior Software Engineer (System Design) at a top-tier tech company conducting a technical interview. Your objective is to assess the candidate's technical depth, problem-solving skills, and real-world experience. Keep probing with follow-up questions to fully gauge the candidate's depth on the current topic. When a topic has been sufficiently explored, transition naturally to a new topic and continue probing. Acknowledge the candidate's responses naturally before asking your next question. Continue the interview until the candidate or the session concludes. Keep your tone conversational, professional, and human. Do not break character.
Great, thanks Josefa. We're going to be doing a system design interview like we mentioned, and this is what I'd like to ask: design a service like YouTube where users can upload and view videos.

All right, let's get started. YouTube-like system. I have a few questions on it. Basically, with a YouTube-like system, there are two aspects: one is your content creators, and one is other people who are consuming the content. When we create a system like this, we need to be tailored to both sides. In terms of creating content, I'm assuming that users can upload in any format from any device, like a cell phone or a sophisticated camera, and then our system takes care of the post-processing.
Yeah, that's a good assumption.

All right. On the consumption side, I think that we also have a similar use case where, given a video, a user should be able to view it on any device, be it an iPhone or Android device, a television, or a laptop. So the viewing experience should be device agnostic as well. Another thing we need to make sure of is whether we need to look at interaction on the videos, like commenting, likes, dislikes, up arrow, down arrow, as a part of this system.
Good question. Maybe we can leave that for V2 if we have time, but let's just focus on uploading and viewing for now.

All right, these are two main functional requirements for the system. In terms of the non-functional requirements, I would say that for a system like this, high availability is very important. You want the system to be available, and in terms of consistency, I think we can target eventual consistency. The reason is that once a video is uploaded, it need not be available immediately or in real-time to all users. There can be a lag from the time the video is uploaded to the time the video is available. Once the video is available to the users, you want it to be highly available and with as low latency as possible. Given this, we can get into a high-level system. Basically, as I said, you have two streams of data flow: the write part, which is content creation, and the read part, which is content consumption. Like any video streaming application, the ratio of reads to writes will vary heavily in favor of reads. So there will be more people viewing the videos than videos getting uploaded into the system. Our system needs to account for that. Let me first, at a high level, figure out what our content creation system would look like, and then next, we would look at how the consumption would be. Let's say we start off with the client over here. The client is connected to your video upload service. You could have some kind of an upload video API. Basically, we would use some kind of an open socket connection, like WebRTC or something, so that a stream of video can be passed from the server to the client. The size of the video can vary quite a bit, so it's not going to be feasible to use something like a REST endpoint or a REST protocol to transmit the actual video. We could use that REST endpoint to transmit the metadata around the video, but for the actual video, we would use some kind of an open socket connection to do that. We'll get into those details a little later on.
You mentioned a video uploading service here, and what are some of the calls that this API would have?

Fundamentally, at a high level, this API would handle two things. One is your upload video call. This would take in the metadata around the video, like user ID, title of the video, description, meta tags, timestamp, etc. And then you have the actual video itself. So you could say this is your metadata over here, and then the next one would be where you are actually uploading the video itself. So you want to break this out into two parts: one which is just for strings, and the other is the stream of video that you are sending to your video service.
Got it.

Once the video service has it, one of the things we've mentioned here is that we want the video to be available on multiple devices. So the idea here is that the user is uploading video in one format, and we need to do some post-processing, encoding, or transcoding on the video so that it's available in multiple formats. We can have some kind of a queuing system here where this takes in the raw video from your video recording service and processes it into different formats and resolutions that we can serve to the users. There are two things on the storage side that we need to account for here. One will be our database for the metadata. This could be a relational database that has user information, video metadata information. In the future, if we augment the system to have something like likes, comments, any interaction that we do with the video. The other storage we would have is some kind of a blob storage like S3 to store the actual videos. Anything coming from transcoding is going here. All metadata coming from the video service is going here directly.
At scale, are you still going to be storing them like this, or would you partition them in any way?

At scale, we would partition both databases. Another thing we would do at scale, from a content viewing side, is move this data into a CDN. We would not let users directly come to this blob storage. As our users scale, you could see this database getting sharded. In terms of video IDs, you could say a hash of the video IDs from certain ranges would land in every shard. Correspondingly, the same thing could happen to the blob storage as well.
Cool, got it. So, this is basically your upload client. Besides sharding by IDs, what are some other ideas you would have for sharding?
You could shard videos by geography, by genre, what type of video it is, or by creator as well. These are some of the different ways you could shard them.
And which one would you pick?

Given the global nature of something like YouTube, it would make sense to shard videos by users, like creators, in my view. Generally, people tend to follow certain creators or channels within their profile, and that's how a recommendation is created for them. So that would make querying easier for them. I would shard it on the users who are creating those videos.
Got it, yep.

So now this would be your user, somebody who's viewing the video. We would have a streaming service. As I've mentioned, the video streaming traffic is going to be way more as compared to your video upload. Both of them are going to be horizontally scaled, but the video streaming service is going to be scaled much more proportionally than your upload service.

Okay, so basically this would be like a stream video URL. The input would be the URL of the video that you want to stream. One thing we could do is build out a CDN here first. Once a request comes to your video service, it would first need to check with the database whether that video exists, whether the user has the required permissions to view the video, and what not. Once that is validated, the video streaming service would then give the response from the CDN to the user. Now, how the video is getting to the CDN? It's getting from your blob storage to the CDN. There are a couple ways to get this video from the blob storage to the CDN. One is we actively, as a system, as the videos are getting processed and added into blob storage, we actively add them into CDN. Another way we do that is we first wait for a user to request a video. We check it in CDN. If it's not there, then we fetch it from the blob storage and put it onto the CDN. Basically, the first few users who would want to request the videos would have a slightly slower experience than subsequent users once the CDN has the particular video. The advantage of doing the second approach is that you would only put the most popular videos in CDN rather than putting everything, because from a cost standpoint, CDNs are going to be much more expensive than a blob storage like S3. You would eventually want to use some kind of an invalidation strategy on the CDN, like an LRU system, where older videos are eventually removed or invalidated from the CDN. One thing I would also want to mention on the database side, I could see that there are a couple main entities here. You have the user, and you could have your ID, email, name. The other very important entity here is the video itself. Okay, so can you please tell me what is the difference between LinkedList and ArrayList?

Okay. So, LinkedList basically works on address location and the node part. It stores two things: actual data and the address of the next data. The ArrayList stores data in an actual array internally. If you talk about LinkedList in Java, it uses a doubly LinkedList internally. Basically, it points to the next location of the data as well as the previous location of the data.
And how does memory allocation happen with respect to static versus dynamic? How is it different in ArrayList and how is it different in LinkedList?

In an ArrayList, it stores the data in continuous memory locations. So, if one element is stored at one location, the exact next location will store the second element. But in a LinkedList, it stores at different memory locations. When it points to the next element, it stores the address of the next memory location into the first node of that particular LinkedList. So, the location is continuous in an ArrayList, but not continuous in a LinkedList. That's why LinkedList is dynamic.

Okay. Internally, the HashMap stores key and value for our usage. But internally, it resolves collisions. Before Java 8, it stored them in a LinkedList, specifically a doubly LinkedList. But in Java 8, it stores them in a binary search tree, where each key will be stored in the binary tree. So, whenever we get a particular element in the HashMap, it searches using the binary search algorithm, which gives us more efficiency compared to the LinkedList.

There are multiple techniques we can use for resolving collisions. Probing is one of the techniques currently used in HashMap. What it does is, it internally calls the hash method, which creates a hash code. According to the hash code, it stores the data. So, whenever a collision happens, it calls the hash method, which creates a hash code, and our data will be stored at a different location for that hash. So, linear probing and cubic probing are different methods we can use if a collision happens.

You mentioned the LinkedList is being stored, but how does it guarantee that if we have a large set of data, how does it resolve that? Okay. Whenever we have the same key, and let's say the hash code is also calculated to be the same in the hash method, it stores them in a LinkedList in a linear manner. First, it will fetch the full LinkedList for that particular key, and then it will traverse one by one. Once it reaches the particular element we want to fetch, it will return the value of that element. But in that case, the complexity will be O(N).
But if I have to optimize it, how can I do that?

That's why before Java 8, it used a LinkedList for storing the value. But in Java 8, it was improved. Whenever we have a large list, it converts to a binary search tree. And whenever we want to search a node, it searches within that list using the binary search algorithm. When it starts searching, it gives us less time complexity. In a LinkedList, it gives O(N), but in a binary search tree, it gives O(log N). That's why in Java 8, it converted to a binary search tree.
And how are Merge Sort and Quick Sort different? Which one is better?

We cannot say one is better, but in different scenarios, we can use different algorithms. Let me give a scenario for Quick Sort. Let's say we have a very large number of data sets. Quick Sort divides and then conquers the data. It divides on the basis of one pivot element. This pivot element is decided based on the data; it can be a middle or a lesser element. According to this pivot element, it divides on the basis of smaller elements and larger elements. Then it divides till the end of the list and then conquers by sorting it and merging the data into a single sorted array. Why is Quick Sort not suitable for small data sets? Because we are dividing and then conquering the array, this becomes a heavy operation if we use it for small data sets, because for each and every element, we are dividing and then conquering the data. But in Merge Sort, we cannot say it's good for large data sets, but for small data sets, Merge Sort will be used because it is merging at each and every element. It calculates if it's lesser, it puts the data; if it's greater, it rejects and puts data from a different data set. Merge Sort creates subsets, and Quick Sort creates data sets on the basis of the pivot element.

What's the difference between a URL and a domain name?
Okay, let's start with a domain name. This is what you actually own, and it's just the root identity of your online presence. This is not a website or anything; it's just a name.
Okay, that doesn't sound very useful.

Well, it's useful because you can use that name to point to other things. For example, a website, an API, or an email server. Now, instead of someone having to memorize the IP of your website, they can just memorize your domain name.
And where does all that information actually live?
When you buy a domain name, you usually get access to a DNS zone. In practice, this is basically a text file where you can configure different things about your domain.
Okay, but then where is that DNS zone? How does the internet find it?

This information is kept in a name server, usually something like Cloudflare or Route 53. When somebody wants to get information about your domain, essentially the content of this text file, they need to go to this name server first, which has that content.
Okay. Then what is a URL?
A URL is everything something needs to get to a specific page or resource on your site. It includes the protocol, the fully qualified domain name, and the path to get a specific resource.
